He hits the coat, takes it on one tusk, shakes it free, and swings back to find you gone. Three seconds of pure confusion. Pell does not waste it.

She comes off the oak line at a dead run, ground-low, spear level. The bull hears her, starts his turn — and the binding in the blade sings through your thumb-bone even from here, the death-register reading the proximity of the kill. She drives the spear home behind the shoulder, full weight behind the socket, and the shaft holds — holds — and then something goes wrong in the rune-work. The channel you threaded into the blade last night at the forge-pit seizes, not releases. The Palework you were meant to push through it at the moment of the strike finds no path — the runes close like a fist around the energy instead of passing it, and what comes back up the thread is a hard concussive jolt, a sound like a bell struck inside your chest, and then your knees are in the dirt at the streambed's edge and your vision is white at the corners.

The bull is down. Pell's strike was clean enough without the craft behind it — the spear's iron did its work even if the binding failed its half of the bargain. The animal thrashes once, twice, goes still in the dry grass twenty feet from the tree line. Pell is standing over it with one hand on the shaft, breathing hard, looking across the ridge at you with an expression that is somewhere between sharp focus and a question she hasn't decided how to ask. The Deathbound Runic Spear is still in the bull. Huginn has already descended to a branch above the body. The gold is full in the eastern sky now. Your sternum aches like you caught a kick from a horse.

*You feel it settle into you — a new steadiness. (Level 4: +1 attunement, +5 reserves, and growth to choose — a sub-attribute point and a skill point.)*

turns.player: Let Huginn come to you — hold out your arm and let it do what it does with what just died. The bull is still warm and the death-register in the blade felt something you didn't.
turns.summary: Silas reaches for Huginn and the death-register — both refuse; ridge light too open, moment already closed.
turns.narration:
You hold your arm out toward the bull's body, steadying yourself against the streambed edge — knees still on ground, vision still white at the corners from the backlash. The gesture is plain: come, do what you do.

Huginn does not move.

It sits on the branch above the bull with its wings folded and its eye on you, and it stays there. Not refusing, exactly — but not coming either. You hold the arm until the muscle starts to ache. The gold morning light has no shadow in it anymore, the sun clearing the ridgeline and washing out every anchor-point in the dry streambed below you. The death-register in the blade — whatever it caught when the channel closed back on itself — gives you nothing. The spear shaft is just ash wood. The bull is just a large animal cooling in dry grass. The ridge offers no quarter: open sky, full light, the scrub oaks too spaced to hold a shadow worth standing in. Whatever thread you felt at the kill moment has gone flat and won't be reopened by reaching.

Pell watches you with the unasked question still on her face. She hasn't moved from the bull. She has set the spear upright in the ground like a marker, one hand loose on the shaft, and she is waiting for you to get back to your feet before she says anything at all.

turns.player: Pull the spear from the bull yourself — feel the shaft and the blade with your hands before anything else, and see what the runes give you at close range.
turns.summary: Silas pulls the Deathbound Runic Spear free — the blade is clean and dry, the channel warm but closed; Pell names what she saw.
turns.narration:
The spear comes out with more resistance than it should.

You get your feet under you first — one knee, then standing, the vertigo settling into a low hum behind your eyes. The shaft is warm from the animal's body heat, not from the runes. That's the first thing you notice. You close both hands around it, thumbs finding the binding-wrap you laid on at the forge, and draw it straight back through the entry wound. The bull doesn't shift. The ground holds him steady, already cooling into the dry grass.

The runes give you almost nothing at close range. That's the second thing. You run a thumb along the carved channel in the shaft — the death-register groove, pine-pitch dark, Pell's maker's mark seated in the iron above the cross-guard — and there's a faint residual warmth there, the afterimage of something that passed through and came back. Not the Palework channel. The channel closed. What you're feeling is the shape of the closure: clean, like a door pulled shut, not broken. The binding didn't fail. It refused. There is a difference, and your hands know it even if the rest of you doesn't yet.

The blade is dry. No blood on the rune-channels, which shouldn't be possible given where the spear went in — and yet the iron is clean, the pitch-black marks unsmeared. Huginn shifts on the branch above you, one deliberate taloned step sideways, and resettles. The ridge is loud with morning birds now, and it makes the silence between you and Pell and the thing in your hands feel more solid by contrast.

Pell speaks first, her voice the flat register she uses for everything that matters. "You went down. The rune fired backward." Not a question. She's watching your hands on the shaft, the same way she watched you work the forge — present, reading, waiting for what the material will tell her before she names what she sees.

turns.player: Aside from that... I'd say the plan was an all-in-all success!  Did the rune fire backwards... or did it refuse?  Ask Pell to let you place her hand on the iron mark while your run order sense, so perhaps she can help you figure out what happened.
turns.summary: Order-Sense diagnoses the rune refusal — the binding sealed at the kill moment, read Pell's mark as unfinished, and held the Palework channel rather than pass it.
turns.narration:
"All-in-all," you say, watching the bull settle into the dry grass, "I'd call it a success." Pell's mouth does something that isn't quite a smile. She sets the spear upright between you — butt on the ground, blade at eye level — and that's answer enough.

You turn to the question. "Did it fire backward, or did it refuse?" You say it aloud because saying it aloud is how you think, and Pell is the kind of company that doesn't need you to perform certainty. "There's a difference. Backward means the channel caught something it couldn't pass — refusal means the binding decided not to." You pause. "I want your hand on your mark while I read it. The ironwork is yours. You might be what the channel recognizes."

She doesn't hesitate. She grips the shaft just below the iron socket, thumb settling naturally over the maker's mark as if it belongs there — which it does. You lay your fingers beside hers without touching them, close your eyes, and let Order-Sense come in quiet and low, the way you'd read a pattern of wear in old stonework rather than a crowd in motion.

The structure is clear immediately, but not simple. The death-register groove is full — not empty, not spent, not drained. The channel accepted the Palework push and held it. All the energy you sent down the shaft arrived at the blade and sat there, contained. Not refused. Not reflected. *Held.* The binding closed like a valve rather than a conduit — the runes read the moment of the kill and made a decision the person who carved them didn't anticipate making. Your thumb finds the groove where pitch-ink has darkened over the winter grain of the ash, and you feel the decision point: the death-register is built to measure the passage of endings, and at the kill moment it registered the closing of Pell's mark into something still forming, still live with intent, and it chose to keep what it had rather than let it through. Not a misfire. A seal.

You open your eyes. Pell is watching your hand.

"It didn't fire backward," you tell her. "It closed. The binding read the kill and read your mark as — still becoming. Something unfinished in the ironwork, or in the intention behind it. The rune held the Palework channel rather than pass it, because it didn't know what waited on the other side." You look at her directly. "The binding works. It may work too well."

turns.player: Tell Pell what 'still becoming' might mean — the mark is hers, but the binding reads intention, and that means she's bound to the spear more than either of you planned.
turns.summary: Silas explains the 'still becoming' diagnosis to Pell — she receives it plainly and names her place in the binding without flinching.
turns.narration:
The morning holds still around you while you find the words.

"'Still becoming' isn't a flaw," you say, keeping your thumb on the shaft near hers so the reading stays live. "The binding doesn't look for a finished thing — it looks for something that means what it says. Your mark means what it says. It was just still saying it when the blade went in." You pause, because the next part is the part she needs to hear plainly. "The runes read that as provisional. And a provisional anchor holds — but it holds by *closing*, not by passing. So when I pushed the Palework channel through at the kill moment, the binding sealed around your mark and held the energy inside rather than letting it go."

Pell's thumb doesn't move from the iron. She's looking at the blade rather than at you.

"What that means practically," you continue, "is that you're in it now. Not because I bound you — I didn't, and I couldn't have without your hand on it. But the intention in your mark when you set it was real and unfinished, and the spear read that as *belonging*. You're not a bystander to this weapon anymore." You let that land. "I don't know yet if that's a problem. It might be the opposite — an anchor that's still growing might be exactly what a death-binding needs long-term. But you should know it."

Huginn shifts on the branch above the bull — one foot, then settling again. Not commentary. Just the weight of the moment redistributing.

Pell lifts her thumb from the mark. She looks at the blade for another few seconds, then at you. "Still becoming," she repeats, flat and careful, the way she'd repeat a measurement she didn't expect. "So the spear's half mine until I'm not." She picks up the shaft and sets it against her shoulder, eye running down the line of it. "I can live with that." A pause. "Provisionally."

The bull is cooling in the dry grass. Corvin is thirty yards south in the sedge, working the sow. Two animals to carry, a forge waiting, and a question about the Palework channel that the morning hasn't answered yet.

turns.player: Well, we can talk about that when we're back. Let's get to work on this bull then tag up with Corvin and haul all this back to Aldric... (secretly, I'll take the bulls tusks to make something out of them later).  Ask Pell while we work if she thinks I should take a quick scout to the river and maybe a little north to where Huginn sensed things.
turns.summary: Bull dressed on the scrub oak ridge — tusks taken, party coordinates for carry to Aldric; Silas floats a river scout and Pell gives it quiet endorsement.
turns.narration:
You drive the butt of the spear into the earth to free your hands and crouch beside the bull. The animal is a good three hundred pounds of cooling muscle and thick hide — you start at the belly, knife out, working the incision clean and steady while Pell moves to the haunches without being asked. She knows the work. Her hands are a blacksmith's hands but the economy of motion is the same — no wasted cuts, no hesitation at the joints.

You work the skull last. The tusks come free with the belt knife and a quarter-turn of the wrist, ivory-yellow and curved, dense at the base where the jaw muscle attached. You tuck them into the traveler's pack without comment. They are useful and they are yours and that is the end of it.

"Huginn had a direction before the hunt," you say, not stopping. "Northwest. Felt like soon, or already — wouldn't name what was ending up there." You free the near shoulder and Pell takes the weight, setting it aside on clean grass. "I'm thinking: we haul this to Aldric, I drop the load, and then I walk north along the river a half-mile and see what the water says before the light changes. You think that's worth the legs?"

Pell straightens, wipes her forearm across her jaw, and looks northwest the way you might check a ridge for weather. "The river's been wrong three weeks," she says. "Another day won't change that. But if Huginn put a direction on it—" She lets the sentence sit there, which is as close as she gets to *yes, go*. Huginn, for its part, drops from the branch, lands two feet from the bull's skull, and studies the open cavity with the calm professional interest of a thing that has seen a great many of these.

turns.player: After we field dress the boar and join up with Corvin we haul the meat back to Aldric. I'll ask Pell about the rest of the date day after that.
turns.summary: Hunt haul delivered to Aldric at the smokehouse — both animals credited, arrangement confirmed, afternoon still open.
turns.narration:
The carry down takes the better part of an hour. You haul the bull's haunches in relay — you and Pell trading the heaviest cuts between you while Huginn glides ahead in short hops, landing on fence-posts and fallen timber, not in a hurry. The morning stays clear and warm. By the time you reach the sedge strip, Corvin has the sow wrapped in the hide and a carrying pole cut from the hedgerow. He looks at the bulk of the bull quarters and raises his eyebrows once — not complaint, just arithmetic. The three of you redistribute without much discussion and start south.

Millbrook comes back into view across the lower terraces, smoke from the cookhouse visible above the tree-line. The north gate gap is dry-stone and low, and you ease the bulk through it one piece at a time. A few villagers note the size of the haul — a woman pulling water from the well pauses, and two children trail you to the smokehouse door with undisguised interest. Huginn drops into a slouch on the smokehouse eave and tucks one foot up.

Aldric is at the block when you push through. He looks at the two animals — sow and bull, both dressed clean — and sets his fillet knife down with the flat deliberate motion of a man revising his inventory in his head. He doesn't say much. He says 'good work' and means it, and he calls the boy over to start hanging. You give him the short version: sow in the sedge strip before dawn, bull on the scrub oak ridge by mid-morning. He listens to the ridge detail — northwest slope, scrub oak, dry grass — and when you mention the high ground and the distance from the streambed he nods once, slow.

'Boar pushing uphill,' he says, more to himself than to you. 'Three weeks ago they were working the riverside reed-beds every morning.' He squares the account without being asked — two animals, good condition, clean dressing — and adds a second parcel to your pack alongside what you already carry. Pell has drifted to lean against the doorframe, watching Aldric's arithmetic with the same attention she'd give a piece of ironwork. Once the handoff is done she looks at you, and the unfinished thread — the forge, the afternoon, the thing she agreed to see — is simply still there between you, waiting on your word.

turns.player: Mention the ledger at the general store and that part of the meat money should go to clearing those books.  Ask how much more Aldric owes you for the two boar.
turns.summary: Aldric squares the boar account — Mara's ledger offset, ninety cord-weight balance in Silas's favor.
turns.narration:
Aldric sets his fillet knife flat on the block and pulls out the tally ledger — a narrow thing, cloth-bound, worn at the spine. He runs his thumb down the column without ceremony.

"Sow's a fair animal. Bull's better than fair." He names the cord-weight for each without looking up: the sow at ninety, the bull at a hundred and forty. Two-thirty total, against what he already advanced you in trail parcels — two forties, so eighty back. That leaves a hundred and fifty cord-weight in your favor. You mention Mara's ledger before he can close the book — the ash shaft, the rune-craft materials, the account he runs with her store. Aldric pauses, not surprised, just recalibrating. He nods once. "Sixty off the top to Mara's books, then." He scratches a line and initials it. "Ninety cord-weight to you, cash or credit against the next delivery — your call." He closes the ledger and sets it aside. The arithmetic is clean and he makes no ceremony of it.

Outside, on the eave, Huginn shifts weight from one foot to the other and tucks its head briefly — the exact posture it uses when it has decided nothing else is happening here.

turns.player: Leave 30 in credit with Aldric, ask for the 60 and give Corvin 20 of it, 40 to Pell.
turns.summary: Silas settles the accounts — thirty in credit with Aldric, twenty to Corvin, forty to Pell — and the hunt party disperses.
turns.narration:
You tell Aldric to keep thirty against future trade, and hold out your hand for the remainder. He counts sixty cord-weight from the till behind the block — small stamped discs, worn smooth, the valley's working currency — and sets them in two stacks on the ledger without ceremony. He initials the margin and pushes the book closed.

Corvin is still near the door, pole leaned against the wall, ready to be gone. You cross to him and press twenty cord-weight into his hand before he can object. He looks down at the discs, then up at you — not surprise exactly, more the brief recalibration of a man who didn't expect the accounting to include him. He wraps his fingers around them, gives a short nod. "North gate before the light's high," he says, which is the closest he gets to thanks. Then he shoulders past the door and out into the morning, pole over one arm.

That leaves forty. You turn to Pell at the doorframe. She sees you coming and reads it before you reach her — her chin comes up a fraction, the ironwork look she uses when something lands that she doesn't want to show affects her. You put the forty in her palm. "Materials on the forge afternoon," you say, which is one way to frame it. She closes her hand around the weight, looks at it once, then back at you. "I'll have the fire up," she says. That's it. She tucks the coin into her apron pocket and straightens off the frame, ready to walk.

The smokehouse settles into its ordinary morning. The bull and sow hang silent on the rack above you. Aldric is already back to his work, knife in hand. Outside, Huginn drops one wing off the eave and resettles — the posture of a bird that has decided the interesting part is somewhere else.

turns.player: Say goodbye to Corvin for now, then let Pell know that you'll be by shortly to clean up from the hunt and get ready for the afternoon.  Then head to the general store.
turns.summary: Corvin departs, Pell heads to the forge, Silas walks to Mara's store.
turns.narration:
Corvin is already angling for the door when you catch his eye. You give him a nod — the kind that means 'good hunt, good company, we're square' — and he returns it in exactly the same currency. He lifts the pole off the wall, tucks it under one arm, and steps out into the morning without looking back. The door swings behind him and Aldric goes back to his knife.

Pell is still in the doorframe. You cross toward her and tell her plainly: you'll stop by the store, get yourself cleaned up from the hunt, and be at the forge by early afternoon. She reads that without any drama, gives a single short nod. "Fire'll be ready," she says, and turns out the door ahead of you.

The walk from the smokehouse to Mara's store takes maybe five minutes — south past the elder's post, east around the well, and there's the red door. The village is fully awake now: a woman carrying a yoke of water-jars, two men arguing about ditch-work at the corner of the terraced lane, a cart horse standing in the shade looking philosophical. The morning light is flat and bright and clean. You push the red door open and step in.

turns.player: Ask Mara if she has a nice bottle of wine, and any imported fruits.  The stranger the better.
turns.summary: Silas asks Mara for wine and exotic fruit — she produces a resin-dark mountain wine and sealed dried starfruit from before the pass closed.
turns.narration:
Mara looks up from the ledger with the same unhurried attention she gives everything that comes through her red door. You ask about wine — a good bottle — and whatever imported fruit she might have squirreled away. The stranger the better.

She doesn't answer immediately. She closes the ledger on one finger to hold her place, turns, and surveys the back shelves with the same methodical sweep she'd give any inventory question. "Wine, yes. One bottle — came up on the last trader's cart from the lower valley, Adaptor settlement south of the Disputed Zone. They press from a dark mountain grape. Strong. Tastes like resin and something you can't quite name." She reaches under the counter and sets it on the wood: dark glass, sealed with wax gone slightly tacky in the spring warmth, no label.

For fruit, she tilts her head. "Not fresh. Mid-winter I had candied persimmons from the Heights — traded out fast." She disappears behind the shelving partition for a moment, and when she comes back she's carrying a small cloth packet, folded and tied. "Dried starfruit. Came through the pass circuit before it closed — three winters back, maybe four. Still good. I kept them sealed." She sets the packet beside the bottle. "The resin wine and fruit that's older than most of what I stock. Strange enough for you?"

Huginn, somewhere just outside the door, makes no comment. Mara glances toward the sound and then back to you with that same practical expression.

turns.player: Before you buy anything, mention that you're heading to the forge this afternoon — the wine and fruit are for Pell. Watch how Mara reads that.
turns.summary: Silas tells Mara the wine and fruit are a gift for Pell — Mara takes it in without a word out of place and names the price.
turns.narration:
You set your hands on the counter and tell Mara plainly: the wine and the fruit are for Pell. You're heading to the forge this afternoon, and you'd rather arrive with something in hand than not.

Mara doesn't move for a moment. Her finger stays in the ledger. She looks at the bottle, then at you, and then — not quite a smile, but a small, decisive settling of her expression, the way a number comes out right. "Pell," she repeats, as if logging it. "She's had that whetstone three weeks and never came to collect it. I gave it to you instead." A brief pause. "I think she'll appreciate this more than the stone anyway."

She sets the bottle and the starfruit packet a little closer to your side of the counter — a clear declaration of ownership. Then she opens the ledger properly. "Eight cord-weight for the wine. Three for the fruit. I'll take it off your Aldric balance if that's easier, or coin now — your choice." Her pen is already at the margin, ready either way.

turns.player: I'll pay her out of pocket - then ask if there's anything else she knows about Pell that might help in your wooing... What kind of things does Pell like?
turns.summary: Silas pays for the wine and starfruit, then draws quiet intelligence on Pell from Mara — who gives it plainly, with a warning attached.
turns.narration:
You count out eleven cord-weight and set it on the counter in a neat stack. Mara picks up each disc with the same unhurried competence she brings to everything — counts them without looking, drops them in the drawer, marks a line in the ledger. Done.

Then you lean on the counter slightly and ask, keeping it easy: is there anything Mara knows about Pell that might be worth knowing? What does she actually like?

Mara's pen doesn't stop moving. She caps the ink, sets the pen in the groove, and looks at you with an expression that is not unkind but is entirely clear-eyed. "That's a question I'd be careful about," she says. "Not because I won't answer — I will — but because Pell reads a person who asks around about her the same way she reads a blade that's been sharpened by someone else. She wants to know if it was done right." A beat. "So take what I say as background, not as a shortcut."

She begins to close the ledger. "She likes work that is actually finished. Not almost done, not good enough — done and honest about what it cost. She'll sit with you over a long job longer than she'll sit with you over a short meal. The wine is right: she drinks slowly and she notices the taste. The fruit —" a brief pause, a slight upward movement at one corner of her mouth — "she grew up before the pass closed. She'll recognize those and she won't expect them. That's a better thing than something expensive." The ledger closes. "One more. She doesn't like being anticipated. Give her room to come to conclusions herself and she'll trust you for it. Try to land her somewhere and she'll feel the current under the conversation." She picks up her pen again. "That's what I know. It's enough."

turns.player: Ask one follow-up: does Pell have anyone — or has she, recently? Better to know before you walk in.
turns.summary: Mara tells Silas that Pell is unattached — a tinker from the heights, three years back, ended quietly. Warns him not to over-engineer the approach.
turns.narration:
Mara's expression doesn't shift much — a small compression at one corner of her mouth, the kind that precedes honesty rather than deflection.

"Not currently," she says, in the same tone she'd use to report stock levels. "There was someone, maybe three years back — a tinker from the heights who came through twice a year. It ran quiet and steady for a while. Then it didn't. He stopped coming through." She straightens a jar on the shelf behind her with one knuckle, a purely habitual gesture. "I don't know if it ended clean or not. Pell didn't say and I didn't ask. She's not the kind you ask twice."

She turns back to you, and there's a slight narrowing to her attention — not unfriendly, but precise. "What I'll tell you, for free: she doesn't hold a torch. She just moves at her own pace and doesn't apologize for it. If you go in there trying to read the room before you say a thing, she'll know you're doing it." A short pause. "Wine and starfruit is good. Showing up with your hands busy and your mouth quiet is better. Let the afternoon be what it is."

Huginn makes a sound from the doorstep outside — not a word, just a low, settled croak, the kind it makes when it has been waiting long enough and finds the wait unremarkable. The forge is across the village. The fire will be ready by early afternoon, if Pell runs true.

turns.player: Head to the forge now — clean up from the hunt, arrive early, let the afternoon settle on its own terms.
turns.summary: Silas cleans up from the hunt at the forge, gifts set by the door, fire building in the south pit.
turns.narration:
You thank Mara, tuck the wine and the starfruit packet under one arm, and step out into the morning. Huginn is on the eave-peg above the door, watching a dog cross the lane with the focused indifference of something that has outlasted every dog it has ever seen. It drops onto your shoulder without ceremony as you turn south.

The forge is a ten-minute walk — past the well, down the south lane, past the elder's post with its hand-chalked notice board. The air is cool still, spring light slant and clean through the upper gardens, and the smell of fresh-turned earth follows you most of the way before the coal smoke from Pell's fire takes over. She said she'd have it ready. She was not wrong: a thin column of gray-white rises from behind the forge wall as you come through the gate, and the metallic warmth of a young fire reaches you at the door.

Pell is at the near bench with her back to you, one boot off, doing something with the lacing. She doesn't look up. 'Water's in the bucket,' she says. 'Soap's on the ledge. Apron's on the hook if you need it.' The instructions land like she wrote them before you arrived — practical, unhurried, no ceremony. The south pit has a small neat fire, not yet working heat but building toward it. The whetstone you gave her is on the bench where you last saw it, the surface newly wiped.

You set the wine and the starfruit near the door where they won't catch smoke, strip down to the useful layers, and make use of the bucket. The hunt smell comes off in stages — river mud, green bark, the specific copper note of a kill — and by the time you're done the water is opaque and Huginn has relocated to the ridge-beam above the pit, watching the fire the way it watches most things: as if it knows what comes next and is content to let it happen.

turns.player: Set the wine and starfruit on the bench now — let Pell see them without comment and watch where it goes.
turns.summary: Silas sets the wine and starfruit on the bench. Pell clocks them — the mountain wine, the old pass-circuit knot — and waits.
turns.narration:
You pick up the wine and the starfruit packet from near the door and set them on the bench — not thrust forward, not announced, just placed where she'll see them when she turns. The dark glass of the bottle catches a dull gleam from the building fire. The cloth-wrapped packet sits beside it, squared up out of habit.

Pell's still relacing the boot. Her hands slow for just a moment — one beat — then resume. She doesn't look up right away. When she does, it's a quick, flat inventory of the objects: bottle, packet, the wax seal on the bottle's neck, the old-style knotting on the cloth wrap. Her eyes come back to her laces.

"Mountain wine," she says. Not a question. A pause. "And that knot on the cloth is the old pass circuit pattern." Another pause, shorter. "Haven't seen that in a while."

She ties off the boot and sets it down. She doesn't reach for either thing. She looks at you — the same look she had on the scrub oak ridge when she named what the rune-channel did, flat and direct and not without warmth — and waits, the way she waits for a piece of iron to tell her what it needs.

turns.player: I figured I bring some thing as old as you are... lol... no, really I asked for the weirdest Mara had because you said you liked weird and I wanted to get the image of me rutting around like a bull out of your head.
turns.summary: Silas names the gifts plainly; Pell receives the deflection, sets the bull down, and opens the afternoon.
turns.narration:
The line lands the way an honest thing does — a little ungainly, entirely real. Pell's mouth doesn't do much with it at first. She sets the boot down on the floor with a quiet thud, both feet flat, and looks at the wine and the packet for a moment as if reassessing them in light of what you just said.

Then the corner of her mouth moves. Not a full smile — something drier than that, earned rather than given.

"Old as me," she says, and picks up the starfruit packet, turning the pass-circuit knot between two fingers. "Three winters at least, if I'm reading the cord right." She sets it back down. "And you remembered the weird part." She glances up at you — a quick, direct look, not unkind. "That's either careful or lucky. I can't tell which yet."

She reaches for the bottle next, reads the wax seal with her thumb, and doesn't open it. Just holds it for a moment with the flat competence she applies to everything, then sets it on the bench beside the whetstone with a small, deliberate movement that means *these stay here*. "The bull's gone," she says, matter-of-fact, the slight edge in it that means she's actually putting it down rather than performing putting it down. "You got him. That's what the afternoon is."

Huginn shifts on the ridge-beam, rearranges a wing, and settles again. The south pit fire has built into something real now — the air in the forge has gone from cool and coal-smelling to warm and coal-smelling, which is a different thing. The tools on the wall have taken on a dull, orange-sided gleam.

Pell tilts her head toward the bench. "You've got an hour before the iron's worth working. What do you want to do with it?"

turns.player: The wine and starfruit can wait for a bit more... til after we word the forge and learn things... but for the next hour - I could help you wash up a bit more, and you could check my chest for any wounds and put ointment on them.
turns.summary: Silas and Pell tend each other's hunt-scrapes at the forge — clean and practical, the afternoon now genuinely open.
turns.narration:
The fire in the south pit has found its rhythm — orange-sided heat that makes the wall tools gleam and the far corners soft. Huginn has settled deep into the ridge-beam, a dark shape with one eye half-open, entirely unbothered.

You set the wine and starfruit to the side of the bench, out of the way but not gone. There's time for those. You tell Pell plainly: you've got some scrub-oak country on you from the hunt, probably a scratch or two on your chest from the gorse, and she's been up since pre-dawn on rough ground. Easier to start clean before the iron gets interesting.

She doesn't make it a thing. She nods, pulls the water bucket closer, and you work through it practically — she takes the cloth and does your back and shoulders while you work the rest, and you find the two places the gorse got you across the lower ribs, shallow and already-dried, nothing urgent. When you turn she looks them over without fuss, presses the edge of one with a thumb to check it, and finds the small tin of drawing salve on the shelf above the bench without asking whether you want it. Her hands are a craftsperson's hands, direct and economical. She says *two days and they'll be nothing* and means it, the way she'd assess a dull edge.

You do hers in turn — a bruised forearm from the gorse, a red patch on one knee from the ridge ground. She holds still for the salve the same way she holds still for everything: with the absolute patience of someone who has been through harder things and isn't going to perform the minor ones.

By the time you're both back in clean shirts and the water bucket is set aside, the fire has climbed another degree toward working heat, and the afternoon is genuinely open. The wine and starfruit are still on the bench. The whetstone is beside them. Huginn has closed the second eye.

turns.player: Lay the Deathbound Runic Spear on the bench — look at the misfired binding together
turns.summary: The Deathbound Runic Spear laid on the bench — Pell reads the marks and names the misfire plainly.
turns.narration:
You lift the Deathbound Runic Spear from where it leans and lay it flat across the bench — shaft overhanging one end, the wide iron blade pointing toward the far wall. The south pit fire does the rest: orange light runs clean along the rune-channels in the iron, and the same light catches the etched marks in the shaft, the Huginn-thread where the wax and pitch-ink dried into the grain.

Pell leans in without being invited. She doesn't touch it yet. She reads it the way she'd read any piece of ironwork — starting at the socket throat where shaft meets steel, tracking the binding at the collar, then following the rune-channels up the blade's center to the cutting edge. Her expression stays flat and attentive, the same face she gives a piece she hasn't decided about yet.

"This is mine," she says, fingertip settling on the maker's mark pressed into the iron just above the collar. She says it without pride or alarm — just identification. "You put it here on purpose." Not quite a question. She glances up at you briefly, then back to the blade. "And it closed. At the kill moment, it just — closed." She lifts her hand away and straightens. "Show me what it was supposed to do."

turns.player: I will show you what it was supposed to do (stand close to her while you explain the runes, their alignment and general intent to channel pale work through the spear to tap the life of what the spear is used against)... but I'm just as interested in what your maker's mark is to you, describe it, and tell me about what it felt like at the moment of the kill. (silently invite Huginn close to watch over our work and point things out we might miss)
turns.summary: Silas teaches Pell the rune-binding's intent; she names her maker's mark and what the kill felt like; Huginn flags the shallow transverse as the site of the stall.
turns.narration:
You pull the spear closer between you and start at the socket throat — the point where your work and Pell's meet. Your voice stays low and even, almost instructional, because that is what this is: you are teaching, and the forge is the right room for it.

The channels run from the collar-binding up the flat of the blade in a branching path — two main lines with a shorter transverse that closes the circuit at the cross-guard shoulder. You trace them with one finger without touching the iron, and you explain it plainly: the intent was to let the spear read the life in what it strikes. Not harvest it, not trap it — read it, the way a hand reads a pulse. Palework through iron, routed by the runes, meant to register the moment of ending so you could know what you were ending. A kind of witnessing built into the blade. "The transverse here is the latch," you say. "It closes the circuit at the moment of deep contact — when the iron has gone past the edge of a thing and into what it is. That's when the channel was supposed to open."

Pell listens with her arms folded, not at the elbows but at the wrists — the posture of someone containing a question until they're sure they have the right one. When you finish the channel description she unfolds one hand and points to the maker's mark — a small double-stamp at the base of the blade, two offset rectangles pressed into the iron when it was still hot enough to take a mark. "That's mine," she says. Not pride, not modesty. Identification. "I press it when a piece is finished. When it's done becoming what it is." She looks at the mark and is quiet for a moment. "At the kill — I felt it close. Not through the spear. Through my hand. Like pressing a stamp and having the metal push back."

Above you, Huginn drops from the ridge-beam without sound — an unhurried arc to the end of the bench, where it lands and tilts its head once at the rune-channels. Not close enough to the iron to suggest anything, just present, at the edge of the firelight. Its eye moves to the transverse line and holds there long enough to be a comment.

You follow its attention. The transverse is where the circuit stalled. The branching lines in the blade carried the Palework intent fine — the wax took the inscription cleanly and the pitch-ink set into the grain without shrinkage. But the transverse is shallow. Not damaged, not malformed — just not deep enough to fully close at the speed of a kill-moment. The Order-Sense reading is quiet but definite: the maker's mark wasn't the problem. The maker's mark was the one thing that held. The shallow transverse is what couldn't complete the circuit before the channel sealed itself in protection of what it recognized as unfinished work. "Your mark read as still becoming," you tell her, as directly as you can manage. "The binding protected it. The cut was supposed to open the channel — but the transverse couldn't close fast enough at the kill, so the binding held everything in instead of passing it through." You look at the shallow line. "The spear worked. It just didn't get the instruction it needed to do the last part."

turns.player: Show her what the channel was meant to register — use Palework on something already concluded nearby (the banked coals, the salve tin, the spent rune-wax) so she can see the ability itself before it goes back into the iron.
turns.summary: Silas demonstrated Palework on the spent rune-wax so Pell could see the ability itself before it goes back into the blade.
turns.narration:
You pick up the spent stub of rune-wax from the bench — the one that went into the Huginn-thread two nights ago, now half the length it was, the surface dulled to grey. A reasonable object. Already concluded.

You hold it in your open palm and let Palework in without ceremony — not the blade, not the binding, just this small finished thing. What comes back is quiet and exact: the wax remembers the heat of the pitch-ink going into it, the press of your thumb, the moment the grip loosened because the work was done. An ending that was also a completion. The two are different, and Palework reads them both cleanly. You don't pull on it — you let it pass through your hand like water remembering which way it ran.

Pell is watching your face more than the wax. You keep it open so she can see what you're doing — no effort, no strain, just attention directed at what is already finished. Huginn shifts on the bench-end and angles its head toward your palm; not a comment, just orientation. Then you set the wax down and look at her.

"That," you say, "is what was supposed to happen at the kill — the spear reading the ending rather than me holding it separate. The rune-channels were meant to carry this back through the iron. The transverse closes the circuit." You touch the shallow line in the blade with one finger — still not pressing, just indicating. "It's too shallow to close at that speed. The channel opened and found nowhere to land."

turns.player: I'd like to work together on modifying the spear - we seems to be linked ourselves through it... Let's make a plan after each doing our own assessments and sharing what we've found - Huginn will help.  Will you make a plan with me Pell?
turns.summary: Pell agrees to plan the spear repair; both assess the transverse fault together while Huginn attends.
turns.narration:
You set the spent rune-wax stub down and look at Pell directly. "Will you make a plan with me?" She doesn't answer immediately — she's still watching the blade, the transverse line you indicated, the place where the channel found nowhere to go. Then she straightens and looks back at you with the same expression she uses for a piece of iron she's decided to keep working.

"Yes," she says. That's all.

Huginn shifts at the bench-end — a single step closer along the wood, head angling to bring the spear's full length into view. Not commentary. Attendance. Pell notices the movement and doesn't remark on it, which tells you something about how quickly she's stopped being startled by Huginn.

She takes her own first turn with it: fingers along the socket throat, thumb tracing the collar-binding you pressed there two nights ago, then up to the transverse line. She holds there. "The mark is mine," she says, not for the first time, but this time it sounds like she's placing it — deciding what it means that her mark is in the thing rather than just on it. "And the fault is in the transverse. Too shallow — it couldn't close at kill-speed. So the channel had nowhere to land, and the binding held rather than passed." She looks up. "That's the what. What I want to know is the how of fixing it. Does the rune-line need deepening, or does it need rewriting, or does it need something from me again — the mark re-seated, the way it was seated the first time?"

Huginn tilts its head toward the shallow transverse and holds. The timing is not coincidence. Pell looks at the bird, then back at the blade, and says nothing — but her thumb has already moved back to the fault line, pressing gently into the groove to feel how little depth it has.

turns.player: GAMBIT — Upgrade the Deathbound Runic Spear
turns.summary: The Deathbound Runic Spear upgraded through a full night's work — circuit deepened, maker's mark re-seated, memory-capacitor installed rough at the collar.
turns.narration:
The repair takes most of the night.

You start at the south end of the bench with the spear full-length between you and Pell, Huginn at the far end keeping the whole picture. The first hour is the honest part: you read the rune-structure as it is — Order-Sense tracing the branching channels from socket to cross-guard, Pell's thumb tracing the physical iron beneath — and you find it together. The transverse is shallow, but more than that: it was pressed at the wrong moment. The wax went in while the pitch was cooling, not while it was still fluid. The groove took the marks but not the depth. The channel is legible; it is not load-bearing. That much resolves cleanly. You can see where the runes need to go, and why.

The hard part is Pell. You ask her to re-seat her maker's mark — not re-stamp the iron, but reclaim the intention that belongs in it. 'Still becoming' was what the binding read, which means she was inside the piece when she pressed it. That is not wrong; it is just unfinished. She stands over the spear for a long quiet stretch with her punch in hand, and when she drives it down it is not quite centered. Close. The mark lands, the binding receives it — you feel the circuit tighten fractionally through the runes — but the re-seat is slightly off-true, and Order-Sense confirms it when you verify: the mark is present, it is hers, and there is a hair of variance in the axis that will make the Palework channel run just slightly oblique. Not broken. Not clean. You name it to her without softening it; she nods once and says, 'I can live with oblique.' Then she picks up the chasing chisel and deepens the transverse herself, reading from your tracing on the blade, and this part goes better than it should — a rerolled moment where the chisel finds the right angle on the first pass and the groove opens to three times its old depth in a single clean cut. The channel closes when you test it with the spent rune-wax against the iron: the wax reads, the transverse holds, the circuit is whole.

The memory-capacitor is the last piece and the messiest. The augment goes into the socket collar — a length of wax cord you press into the collar-groove with pitch-ink, a second binding intended to let the spear hold the shape of what it has struck rather than releasing it at the moment of the kill. It would make the death-register legible later, outside the kill-moment's narrow window. It seats, the pitch takes, the collar-groove closes around it — but when you name the attunement through Order-Sense, working the rune-lines into a ritual inventory of the spear's own structure, the capacitor threads slightly loose at the north quarter of the collar. It will hold under ordinary use. Under the shock of a kill, particularly a hard-body strike, the thread may slip. The spear is upgraded; the upgrade is rough. The death-register is now available before and after the kill moment, not only during — but the collar wants finishing work, and you both know it.

By the time you set the blade down Huginn has gone still. The fire in the south pit has coaled to a steady orange low. Outside the lane is dark and absolutely quiet. Pell wipes the chisel and sets it on the rack and looks at the spear for a long moment without speaking. Then: 'What does oblique cost you, in practice?'

turns.player: Use Order-Sense to check whether the capacitor's loose north quarter can be pressed tighter while the pitch is still slightly warm — a small correction window, if it exists.
turns.summary: Order-Sense found the warm pitch — the collar's north quarter pressed flush before it set.
turns.narration:
You lay two fingers on the collar without pressing — just touching the iron near the north quarter seam, where the capacitor sits slightly canted in its bed. Order-Sense comes in quiet and narrow, the way it reads small things: not the whole spear, just this joint, just the pitch in the gap.

The pitch is still warm. Barely. There's a window — maybe a quarter-hour before it stiffens past the point of correction. The Order-Sense reads the gap's geometry cleanly: the north quarter is canted about two degrees off its seat, and the pitch hasn't fully bridged the void on the inside edge. If you press now, slow and even, it will close. If you wait until morning it will set crooked and stay that way.

You say it plain: "Still workable. North edge, light even pressure — don't rush it." Pell is already moving, her thumb finding the edge of the collar without being shown where. She presses with the heel of her thumb — not a push, just weight, steady and patient — and you watch the faint gleam of pitch squeeze to the surface at the gap's mouth. Huginn leans forward an inch, eye on the seam. Nobody says anything. Thirty seconds, then Pell lifts her thumb and wipes it on her apron.

The north quarter sits flush.

turns.player: Use Deathsense on the finished spear — feel whether the death-register now reads cleanly end to end.
turns.summary: Deathsense confirms the spear's death-register runs clean end to end — the repair holds.
turns.narration:
You set one hand flat on the shaft just below the collar and let Deathsense come in quiet — not a push, just an opening. The forge resists it a little; this is a place of making, and Deathsense reads endings. The fire, the iron, the bench worked until dawn — all of it is alive with the hum of work completed rather than work concluded, and the ability has to find its footing against that grain.

It finds it. The death-register runs the length of the spear in one clean line — socket to cross-guard, no stall, no fold-back at the transverse. The channel you deepened reads load-bearing now: the signal moves through it the way current moves through a properly bedded joint, not around or under but *through*. The maker's mark is present and seated. The capacitor at the collar catches the register's tail and holds it — a reservoir rather than a terminus. The slight oblique axis Pell named is there, a hair of variance in the impression, but it doesn't interrupt the flow. It reads like a signature reads: the person's, unmistakably, not a copy of someone else's.

Pell is watching your face rather than the spear. You let the sense go and look up. "It reads clean end to end," you tell her. "The register runs through the transverse, catches in the capacitor. The variance in your mark doesn't interrupt it — it *is* it. The spear knows whose hand made it." Huginn settles back from the bench without comment, which is, from Huginn, a form of confirmation.

turns.player: The mark being oblique means that she's made room for something - could be something she's found in herself... and it could be someone she's found.  Either way, life is that - and death is part of life - so this spear, Memory, is our memory.  Open the wine and hand her some starfruit.
turns.summary: Silas named the repaired spear Memory, opened the wine and starfruit, and shared them with Pell at dawn.
turns.narration:
You pick up the wine and work the wax seal loose with your thumbnail — it gives with a soft crack, and the smell that comes out is dark and resinous, faintly sweet, the kind of thing that should not exist this far from the mountains where it was pressed. You pour two measures into the forge cups — the ones Pell keeps on the shelf beside the drawing salve, tin-lipped and plain — and set the bottle down with care.

"The mark being oblique," you say, and you are not explaining the rune-work now, "means you made room. For something found in yourself. Or someone found." You open the starfruit packet as you say it — the cloth falls back and the smell is older still, dry and faintly tropical, a scent that belongs to a pass that was closed before either of you came into your work. You set a piece near her cup without ceremony. "Either way, that's what life is. And death is part of it." You touch the spear's shaft once with two fingers. "So this is Memory. That's what we made tonight."

Pell takes the cup. She doesn't drink immediately — she looks at the starfruit for a moment with an expression that is not grief and not quite recognition, but something between them. She picks up the piece and turns it over in her fingers, then sets it on her lower lip and bites. Her eyes go somewhere that is not this room. When she comes back she says, quietly: "I haven't seen these since the pass closed." She doesn't say anything else about that. She drinks.

Huginn shifts on its perch — a single small adjustment, wings settling — and is still again. The fire is low orange and the light under the door is pale and growing. The three of you sit in the forge's particular quiet, which is the quiet of a night's work finished and a name given, and the wine tastes of resin and something you still cannot name.

turns.player: What do you think caused the mark to land oblique?
turns.summary: Pell explains the oblique stamp — the iron told her hand where to go; she's never had a piece do that before.
turns.narration:
Pell turns the cup in both hands, looking at it rather than you. The starfruit taste is still on her, you can tell — something in the set of her jaw that isn't quite back yet.

"Oblique." She says the word like she's testing its weight. "I've been making the double-stamp for eleven years. You press the first face, feel the metal settle, then you press the second exactly over it. Every piece I've ever made — axe-heads, gate-hinges, collar-rings — the mark lands true. It's the last thing I do. By then I know the piece well enough that my hand knows where to go." She takes a slow drink of the wine. "That spear — when the bull went down and I felt the binding close through my palm, I pressed the stamp twice the same as always. But I already knew, before I lifted my hand, that it had landed wrong. Not a slip. Not a tremor. I pressed where the iron said to go, and the iron said to go there."

She is quiet for a moment. Huginn shifts on its perch, a small adjustment, wings flat.

"So I think," Pell says, without quite looking at you, "that the piece needed more room than I usually leave. And the piece knew that. And my hand listened to the piece." She picks up a sliver of starfruit, holds it between two fingers. "I've never had a piece tell my hand where to go before." Not distressed about it. Just naming a first time, plainly, the way she names a measurement.

turns.player: Tell her that's not the piece overriding her — that's her craft deep enough that the piece and the hand are the same conversation. Ask if she wants to understand what happened there more fully.
turns.summary: Silas named what happened in Pell's hand — craft deep enough that piece and hand speak the same language. Pell accepted the framing and said plainly she wants to understand it.
turns.narration:
"That's not the piece overriding your hand," you say, and you mean it plainly, the way you'd name a true thing at a forge bench at dawn — no ceremony, just the fact of it. "That's your craft deep enough that the piece and the hand are the same conversation. The iron didn't tell you something foreign. It told you something you already knew, in a language you've been building for eleven years."

Pell is quiet for a moment. The low orange of the south pit fire moves in the cup she's holding. Huginn shifts on its perch — one slow, deliberate resettlement of weight, wings flat — and then goes still again. Outside, the light under the door has gone from pale gray to something thinner and more definite, the particular quality of early-spring morning just before the valley warms.

"Understand it more fully," she repeats. Not a question exactly — more like she's checking the shape of the offer. Her eyes come up from the cup and find yours, and there's something careful in them, the look of a person who does very precise work and knows what it costs when precision is asked for in the wrong direction. "You mean the way you read the spear. What you were doing with grain and register and the death-sense of it." A beat. "Yes. I want to understand it. Not what you did to the iron — what happened in my hand when I pressed the mark."

The whetstone is still on the bench between you, next to the spent rune-wax stub and the open wine. The morning is young and neither of you has slept, but the question is awake.

turns.player: Use Order-Sense on Pell herself — lightly, with her awareness and consent — to read the structure of what her craft has become. Show her what the pattern looks like from outside it.
turns.summary: Order-Sense reached the edges of Pell's craft but not the heart — the moment the iron spoke lives between hand and metal, not in any readable structure.
turns.narration:
You settle into the familiar reach of Order-Sense — the light touch, the listening posture, the way the pattern beneath a thing surfaces when you stop pressing for it. You have her consent; she's watching you with that same careful attention she gave the spear all night.

But the lattice here is thin, and what's thinner still is the substrate of craft itself — the structure you're reaching for isn't a crowd's schedule or a ritual's hidden rule. It's a person's eleven years of accumulated knowing, and that is not the same kind of pattern. You find the edges of it: the economy of her movements, the way she holds a tool slightly differently when she's working iron she trusts. But the deeper grammar — the grain of what happened when her hand and the metal became one conversation — slides away from you. The ability is not failing; it's meeting the right kind of resistance. A person's learned body-knowledge doesn't answer the Lattice the way a system does. It is not made of rules. It is made of repetition, and repetition is not structure — not this kind.

You surface cleanly. No lurch, no cost beyond the energy already spent. You meet her eyes and tell her the truth: you could read the edges — the economy, the sureness — but the heart of it, the moment the iron spoke, that's not a pattern Order-Sense can reach. It lives somewhere between your hand and the metal, and it doesn't have a form outside of doing it. "I think," you say, "the only way to understand it is to be at a bench with a piece in front of you."

turns.player: Ask her to walk you through the double-stamp on a piece of scrap iron — her hands, not her words — and use Deathsense quietly to feel whether Memory's register shifts while she's doing it.
turns.summary: Pell demonstrated the double-stamp on scrap iron; Deathsense caught a faint sympathetic resonance between her hands and Memory while she worked.
turns.narration:
You set your cup down on the bench and gesture toward the scrap pile in the corner — a few offcuts and a collar-blank Pell hasn't sorted yet. "Not your words," you say. "Your hands."

She looks at you a moment, then sets her own cup down and picks up the collar-blank. The movement is immediate, unhurried. She turns the piece once in her left hand — reading it, though she wouldn't call it that — and then takes up the double-stamp punch from the rack without looking for it. Her body knows where it lives. She positions the blank on the near horn of the anvil, seats the punch, and strikes. Once, clean. Then she feels the iron settle — you can see it in how she waits, the breath between strikes — and brings the punch down a second time exactly over the first. The mark lands true.

You hold Deathsense lightly while she works, conserving it — not reaching, just attending. It costs you almost nothing to listen. Memory's register is steady on the bench beside you, its clean end-to-end hum unchanged. But while Pell's hands are on the collar-blank, working that familiar sequence she has done a thousand times, you catch something at the edge: a faint sympathetic resonance between her and the spear. Not signal, not call. More like two instruments tuned to the same room. The moment she lifts the punch it fades back to nothing, and Memory's register settles again to its own clean tone.

Pell sets the blank down. "That's the double-stamp," she says. Then she looks at you, reading your face the way she reads iron. "Something moved."

*You feel it settle into you — a new steadiness. (Level 5: +1 attunement, +5 reserves, and growth to choose — a sub-attribute point and a skill point.)*

*✦ A new portrait captures who you've become.*

turns.player: Hand her Memory and ask her to do the double-stamp again — this time with the spear in her hands instead of scrap.
turns.summary: Pell struck the double-stamp on Memory itself — the resonance between her hands and the spear ran truer than scrap iron ever gave.
turns.narration:
You lift Memory from the bench and hold it out to her, grip first. No explanation — the gesture says it plainly enough.

Pell takes the spear with both hands, and you watch the adjustment happen: she's held tools her whole life, but a spear is not a tool, and this one has a night's worth of work in it that she put there herself. She turns it once, finding the balance point without searching for it — the way she turned the collar-blank, same economy — and then she moves to the anvil horn and positions the collar's north quarter under the double-stamp punch. The punch goes to the brass ring she installed last night, and she seats it. Waits one breath.

The strike lands true. Then the second, the breath between them just as measured as before.

You hold Deathsense very lightly — CONSERVE, the barest aperture, no press — and what comes back is not quiet. The sympathetic resonance you caught on the scrap iron is here again, but stronger: her hands, the spear, and something in the iron that is already listening. Memory's death-register does not shift or falter. It steadies. As if the piece knows whose mark is in it and recognizes the weight of the hand that put it there. The resonance runs between them for the length of her working and no longer, and then it is gone the moment she lifts the punch. Clean.

She sets Memory down on the anvil with deliberate care and looks at her own hands for a moment. Then at you. Outside, the first birds of actual morning are starting up.

turns.player: Talk through the whole thing with her, and also talk about how much you've enjoyed today... and want to keep enjoying days like this as you discover these kinds of things together... what does she think of you?  Are you weird enough for her?
turns.summary: Silas and Pell talked plainly at dawn — she said she'd like to keep finding out who he is.
turns.narration:
You pick up your cup and settle back against the bench — not going anywhere, the morning outside just beginning to assert itself under the door. The forge fire has coaled down to something comfortable, and the smell of pitch and wine and old starfruit is what the room is made of right now.

"Today was good," you say, and you mean all of it: the cold pre-dawn, Corvin's clean strike, Huginn attending the open animal in the sedge. The night at the bench with the spear between you. The wine. This. "I want more days like it. That's — I'm saying that plainly, because I'd rather be plain than clever."

Pell is quiet for a moment. She turns her cup in both hands — the same reading movement she gave the collar-blank, the spear, everything she's deciding about. Then: "You brought me starfruit from a pass that's been closed since before I had a forge." A beat. "And you stayed up all night fixing something that isn't even yours, because you wanted to understand it." She sets the cup down on the bench and looks at you directly. "You read iron like a person and you have a bird that knows when things are ending. 'Weird enough' is — " She makes a short sound that is almost a laugh. "That's a generous way to put what you are."

Huginn, from its perch, produces a single flat croak. Pell glances at it. "Right," she says, as though it confirmed something. She looks back at you. "I don't know what you are or where you're going. But I'd like to keep finding out. That answer your question?"

turns.player: Tell her you're glad you didn't fall for the first woman you met in this villiage (the cookhouse woman), but you're pretty sure you've fallen hard for the strong - capable - gorgeous creature before you.  Move toward her and make physical contact - telling her plainly what you want.
turns.summary: Silas told Pell plainly he'd fallen for her; she heard him, and held his wrist.
turns.narration:
You set your cup on the bench and close the distance — not fast, not tentative, just the honest movement of a man who knows what he wants and has decided to say so out loud.

"When I walked into this village," you tell her, "the first woman I met looked at Huginn and all but shut a door in my face." A corner of your mouth lifts. "I'm glad that's how it went. Because I'd have missed this." You reach out and take her hand where it rests on the anvil horn — both of yours around one of hers, the same way she holds a piece to read it. Her palm is warm and calloused and absolutely sure of itself. "You're strong and capable and gorgeous, and somewhere between the pre-dawn cold and the last of this wine I fell fairly hard for you. I'm not being clever about it. I'm telling you plainly, because that's the only way I know how to mean it."

Pell looks at where your hands are, then at your face. The forge-light is low and gold, and she doesn't move away. What she does instead is turn her hand in yours — the same economy she brings to every piece she handles — and close her fingers around your wrist. A grip, not a hold. Reading.

"Cookhouse woman," she says, like she's filing that away. Then, quieter: "I heard you." She doesn't move toward you or away — she stays exactly where she is, hand around your wrist, and looks at you steadily in the low light. "You should sleep before you're back on a road. But — not yet."

Huginn makes a single sound from its perch: not a croak, not quite a word, but something that lands in the room like punctuation. Pell glances at it once, then back at you. The river whetstone is still on the bench where it's been all night, unclaimed.

turns.player: Stay — the morning can wait. Lean in and kiss her.
turns.summary: Silas kissed Pell at the forge bench at dawn — she stayed, said 'alright', and the morning opened around them.
turns.narration:
You close the last of the distance.

Her hand tightens around your wrist a half-second before your mouth finds hers — not stopping you, just marking that she felt it coming and chose to stay still. The kiss is unhurried, the way she does everything: no performance in it, just the plain fact of it, her free hand coming up to the front of your shirt and not pulling you closer, not pushing, just resting there like a hand on a piece she's reading. She tastes of resin wine and old starfruit. The forge fire ticks low behind you. Huginn makes no sound at all, which is its own kind of comment.

When you pull back she doesn't move away. Her eyes open — that direct, unhurried look she gives iron before she decides what it needs. "Alright," she says, quietly, and there is something that might be a smile at one corner of her mouth. Not a conclusion. More like a first reading.

Memory rests on the anvil horn between you, the collar flush, the double-stamp clean in the brass. The river whetstone is still on the bench, unofficially hers. The morning is pressing gold under the door now, full and unhurried, and Millbrook is waking up around the forge — wheels on stone, someone calling across the lane, the ordinary machinery of a day beginning. You have a provisioning debt to Aldric, one outstanding thread on the river scout, and a spear that finally knows its own name. The night's work is finished. What the morning holds is yours to choose.

*✦ Your bond with Huginn deepens into something new: **The Attended End**.*

turns.player: You forge iron with confidence - do you want to be forged by me?
turns.summary: Pell received the forge-and-fire question honestly — answered it with craft-logic and held on.
turns.narration:
The question lands in the forge-quiet the way a well-struck tool lands — no excess, no rebound. Just the clean fact of contact.

Pell doesn't answer immediately. She's still holding your wrist, the front of your shirt still in her other hand, and she looks at you the way she looked at Memory after the double-stamp: measuring the register, not the surface. Her thumb moves once against the inside of your wrist — not a stroke, just a press, reading pulse the way she reads tension in cooling iron.

"That's a dangerous thing to offer," she says at last. Her voice is low, entirely unhurried. "Forging changes what a thing is. Not always into something better. Sometimes just into something different that can't go back." She doesn't let go of your wrist. "And the smith doesn't always know what she's making until it's done."

Huginn shifts on its perch. One small weight-transfer, entirely silent. Pell glances at it — just once, the way you check a fire — then back to you. The half-smile is still there, but something behind it has gone more serious, the way heat goes deeper when you hold the piece longer. "Ask me again," she says quietly, "when you've decided you're alright with not knowing what comes out of the fire."

turns.player: Tell her you've already decided — you've stood in worse fires, and you walked toward this one on purpose.
turns.summary: Silas's declaration of certainty lands wrong — Pell names the difference between his readiness and hers, and footsteps approach the forge door.
turns.narration:
The words come out right. That's the problem.

You say it plainly — that you've already decided, that you've stood in worse fires and walked toward this one on purpose, and every word of it is true. You feel it as you say it: the certainty, the clean line of having chosen. It should land.

Pell's thumb stops moving against your wrist. The half-smile that was there a moment ago has gone somewhere she hasn't brought back yet. She looks at you for a long beat — that same unhurried measurement, the kind she gives iron that surprises her — and then something in her face does a quiet thing, not closing exactly, but settling back into itself.

"You've decided," she repeats. Not mocking. Turning it over. She releases the front of your shirt, lets her hand rest flat against your chest for one moment before dropping to her side. Her other hand still has your wrist, but the grip has changed — less a hold, more a contact. "I believe you. I even think it's true." A beat. "But I'm not iron, Silas. I don't care that you're sure. I care what the fire does over time."

Huginn shifts its weight on the perch — a small adjustment, unhurried, perfectly timed. Pell glances at it once, then back at you. She hasn't stepped away. The morning is fully outside the door now, gold light pressing the gap under the threshold, and somewhere beyond it someone is moving — a heavy step on the south lane gravel, quick and purposeful, heading toward the forge.

turns.player: You're right. I didn't mean to skip the fire - I meant I'm willing to be in it, with you.
turns.summary: Silas corrects the register — willing to be in the fire with her, not ahead of it. Pell hears it. Three pragmatic knocks at the forge door.
turns.narration:
The footsteps outside slow — someone hesitating at the gravel's edge, not yet at the door.

Pell hears it too. Her eyes stay on yours.

You don't rush to fill the space. You let the correction land the way it deserves to: plainly, without backing off the weight of what you said before, just setting it right. *I'm willing to be in it, with you.* The difference is small in words and large in everything else — the fire changes you both, or it changes neither, and you said so.

Her thumb resumes against the inside of your wrist. Once. The hand at your shirt doesn't move, but the tension in her fingers shifts — reading something different now.

"That's better," she says, very quietly. Not a verdict. Not even quite approval. Just the sound of something finding its correct register, the way a joint clicks into seating and you feel it before you hear it. She looks at you a moment longer — that unhurried measurement — and then her attention moves with deliberate care toward the door, which has not opened yet but will. Her grip on your wrist becomes a light touch, and then she lets go; not pulling away, just releasing, the way you set down a thing you mean to pick up again.

The footsteps settle. A knock — three knocks, the pragmatic kind, not a visitor who wants to be invited, a person who has arrived and is informing you of the fact.

Huginn turns its head toward the door and says nothing, which is a comment.

turns.player: Let Pell handle the door — watch how she moves on her own ground
turns.summary: A farmer arrives at dawn needing an iron sleeve for a cracked irrigation valve — brittle threading since the water changed.
turns.narration:
You step back — not far, just enough to give her room — and watch.

Pell moves the way she moves at the bench: no wasted motion, no self-consciousness. She wipes her right hand on her apron once, a habit that has nothing to do with the door and everything to do with transitioning between states of work. She picks up the river whetstone from the bench and sets it on the shelf above the bellows — not tucking it away, just getting it off the work surface — and then she crosses to the door and draws the latch.

Morning light spills in, full and pale-gold, and standing in it is a woman maybe ten years older than Pell, broad-shouldered, with dirt at her knees and a coil of split-reed tubing under one arm. She takes in the forge interior with the economy of someone who has been here before, her gaze touching the coaled fire, the bench marks, the spent rune-wax stub, and then you — and Huginn on the rafter, who gets a beat of flat attention before she looks back to Pell.

"I know it's early," the woman says, not apologetically. "The lower field intake valve cracked overnight. The reed's holding it for now." She lifts the coil slightly — showing Pell, not explaining to her. "I need a sleeve. Iron, or bronze if you have off-cuts."

Pell glances at the coil, then at her fire. "Give me half an hour," she says. "The pit's coaled low — I'll have to bring it back up."

The woman nods, her eyes moving back to you once more — not hostile, just noting what she's noting. "Valve failure in two fields since the water went strange," she says, to Pell but audible to anyone in the room. "Iron's going brittle at the threading. Maren says it's the river, but Maren says everything's the river."

Huginn shifts on the rafter. One quiet, deliberate readjustment of weight, the way he does when something has just become relevant.

turns.player: Stay back — let Pell manage her work, watch and listen. You can use Order-Sense quietly on the cracked tubing she brought in to read what the water has been doing to it.
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the cracked irrigation tubing — the gray-green deposit is targeting iron contact points, not the reed itself.
turns.narration:
You stay back and let Pell work. The farmer — name unknown, dirt at her knees, the coil of split-reed tubing still under her arm — gives her estimate and nods at the half-hour. She's already describing the valve: iron threaded sleeve, east-channel intake, second field from the road. 'Iron went brittle overnight,' she says. 'Just crumbled at the threading. We lost the lower intake yesterday. Fen's is the same this morning.' Pell asks one question — wall thickness on the original — and moves to her iron rack without ceremony.

You let your eye rest on the cracked reed tubing. Quietly, economically, you let Order-Sense open — not wide, just enough to read the design the tube is part of. The substrate here is thin and you feel it immediately, the power requiring more intent for less reach, like trying to see through river-ice. But the tubing is concrete, physical, already broken, and the place is used to practical reading. What you get is clear enough: the inside surface of the reed carries a faint mineral crust, gray-green at the joints — the same color the river runs on bad mornings. It's not uniform. The crust is thicker at the threading interfaces, where iron meets reed, and the reed itself has stayed sound. The damage isn't in the plant material. It's at every iron contact point, working inward from the water-facing surface. Whatever is in the river is selecting for iron.

Huginn shifts on the rafter above you. Not agitated — just repositioning to see the tube better. The farmer notices, tracks the bird for a moment, then looks back at Pell like she's chosen not to have an opinion about it. Pell sets a blank sleeve-length on the bench and picks up her calipers without looking up.

turns.player: Say nothing to the farmer yet — wait until she's gone, then tell Pell what you found. This is information that could alarm people if it spreads wrong.
turns.summary: Silas waits for the farmer to leave, then tells Pell the gray-green deposit is targeting iron specifically — two east-channel valves gone since the water changed.
turns.narration:
The farmer doesn't stay long. Pell pulls a sleeve blank from the iron rack, holds it against a caliper, sets it back, pulls a second — the work of thirty seconds, unhurried and exact. She gives the farmer a time: half an hour, come back when the bell rings the second quarter. The farmer nods, tucks the split-reed tubing back under her arm, and steps out into the pale-gold morning without ceremony.

The forge door stays open. Birdsong. The low orange of the south pit fire. Huginn shifts on the rafter, resettling one wing, and does not look away from the door the farmer just left through.

You wait until the footsteps have moved off the gravel and onto dirt before you speak.

"It's not the reed," you say. "The gray-green deposit is in there, at every iron contact point — threading, join, the interface where the sleeve seats. It's working inward from the water-facing surface. The reed itself is fine. The iron isn't." You keep your voice level, practical — the same register Pell uses for wall thickness and bore diameter. "Two valves in the east channel since the water changed. If that's the pattern, there are more. Every iron fitting the river water touches."

Pell has the sleeve blank on the bench and is scribing a cut line without looking up. She doesn't stop working, but there's a quality to her stillness — the stillness of someone filing away something that confirms what they already suspected rather than hearing it for the first time.

"Fen's was the second," she says. It is not a question. She scores the mark, sets the scribe down. "Two fields, one morning." She picks up her tongs.

Huginn makes a short sound from the rafter — one syllable, low, and then nothing.

turns.player: Ask Pell if she wants help with this and if she wants to head north with me after, or if I should go alone?  We need to get some info for the authorities.
turns.summary: Pell agrees to walk north after the sleeve job — she knows both failed valve sites and names the east-channel intake as a starting point for gathering evidence on the iron contamination.
turns.narration:
Pell scribes the second cut line on the sleeve blank without looking up. 'Help with the sleeve?' She sets the scriber down. 'It's a half-hour job. I've done it a hundred times.' Not a refusal — a clarification. She picks up the blank and holds it to the fire's light, checking the line. 'But the contamination — yes. That I'd want another pair of eyes on.'

She sets the blank down and turns to face you properly, leaning a hip against the bench. 'North. You mean the east channel itself, or further?' She reads something in your face and answers her own question. 'Further.' A breath. 'I know where the two failed valves are.
