Fen's is maybe twenty minutes north of the upper meadow — the east channel bends there and the intake sits low. If the deposit is working inward from the iron, the valve housing will show us exactly how long it's been building.'

Huginn makes a sound from the rafter — not quite agreement, but timed like one. Pell glances up at it, a reflex she's developed without seeming to notice she has. 'The elder panel needs to know about iron specifically,' she says. 'Mara Wells's ledger will show when the first valve parts started failing. That's a date.' She straightens, picks up her tongs. 'Let me finish the farmer's sleeve. Half an hour. Then we go.' She tilts her head at Memory on the anvil horn. 'Bring that. If we find what I think we'll find, you'll want it in hand.'

turns.player: Agree — and while Pell works the sleeve, pull a Preserved Trail Parcel from the pack and eat something before the walk. You've been up all night.
turns.summary: Silas eats a trail parcel while Pell works the farmer's sleeve — the last quiet before they walk north.
turns.narration:
You agree, and while the forge settles into the clean rhythm of Pell's work — scriber, caliper, the first ring of hammer on iron — you pull one of Aldric's trail parcels from your pack and sit on the low stool near the door.

The parcel is cloth-wrapped and knotted the way Aldric does everything: practically, without waste. You work the knot loose and peel it back. Dried river perch and salted pork, dense and a little sharp with the cold. You eat slowly, watching the morning come through the open door — pale gold on the south lane stones, a dog moving somewhere two properties over, the bell tower still quiet. Huginn drops from the rafter to the anvil horn and stands over Memory for a moment, then moves to the bench edge nearest you and watches you chew with the expression it always has, which is patience.

Pell doesn't look up from the sleeve work, but she speaks into the iron. 'You should have eaten hours ago.' It isn't a reprimand. It's the same register she uses when she notices something that ought to be corrected — factual, without heat. She drives a half-punch into the blank's edge to seat the cut line and sets it over the south pit to warm. 'Bell rings the second quarter, we go.' The sleeve isn't done yet, but the shape of the next half-hour is fixed and she's already moving inside it.

The parcel is about half gone when you feel the edge come back — not much, just the difference between a body running on nothing and a body running on something. Outside, the lane is brightening. Memory sits on the anvil horn, capacitor flush at the collar, and Huginn has not moved from the bench edge, and the east channel is twenty minutes north of the upper meadow, and the bell hasn't rung yet.

turns.player: Stay quiet and watch Pell work the sleeve — you've been up all night with her and there's something in just letting the craft be the craft for half an hour.
turns.summary: Pell finishes the sleeve job in quiet craft. She places Memory in Silas's hands and they prepare to walk north.
turns.narration:
The sleeve blank takes shape under Pell's hands in the unhurried way that competence makes look like ease. She works the punch with the heel of her palm, not the hammer — two measured taps, a quarter-turn, two more — and the iron opens along the line she scribed without complaint. Huginn watches from the anvil horn, one foot tucked, head angled. Memory catches the fire's light along the capacitor collar.

You sit on the low stool and let it be. The south pit fire breathes. A rooster sounds off somewhere past the well and is not answered. Pell holds the blank up, checks the mouth against the caliper face, sets it back to the fire. She does not look over at you. She does not need to. The shared quiet after a full night of close work has a different texture than ordinary silence — not empty, just used.

The bell tower rings the second quarter while the sleeve is still on the fire. Pell counts two beats after the last note, pulls it, and sets it to cool on the stone ledge. The whole job has taken twenty-six minutes. She peels her gloves off, tucks them under her arm, and moves the blank to the door-step for the farmer to collect.

'Ready when you are,' she says, not turning around — already moving toward the peg where her coat hangs. The river whetstone is still on the shelf above the bellows. Memory is still on the anvil horn. She lifts it, weighs it once in her palm, and sets it into your hands without ceremony. 'Bring this. If there's iron to read, you'll want it close.'

turns.player: Stop at Mara's store on the way north — the ledger may name the date the first iron valve failed, and a five-minute detour is worth a confirmed timeline.
turns.summary: Mara's ledger confirms the timeline: four sleeve failures in three weeks, starting World-day minus-eighteen at Fen's intake.
turns.narration:
The south lane is quiet at this hour — dew still on the stones, the cookhouse smoke just starting to thicken. You and Pell walk in step, Memory easy in your grip, Huginn riding your left shoulder with the companionable weight of something that has nowhere else to be. Mara's store is three minutes from the forge, red door shut but not latched; the lamp inside is already lit.

Mara looks up from her ledger when you push the door open — registers the two of you, the spear, the hour — and nods once. No preamble. 'Early start,' she says, which is not a question.

Pell stays in the doorway while you ask about the east-channel valve dates. Mara turns two pages without hesitation, runs a finger down a column of small, dense script, and stops. 'Fen's intake.' She turns the ledger to face you without lifting it from the counter. The entry is brief: a materials note for a replacement sleeve blank, a date, and Fen's name initialed in the margin. World-day minus-eighteen — roughly three weeks before today. 'I sold Fen the blank. She came back four days later to say it hadn't seated and she'd had to cap the line.' Mara closes the ledger. 'The second?'

'The farmer's — east furlong,' you say.

Mara opens a different log. A beat. 'World-day minus-nine. Also a sleeve blank. Also came back.' She looks up. 'I've sold four sleeve blanks in three weeks. Normal is two a season.' She says it the way she says everything — flat, precise, without alarm — but she holds your gaze a half-second longer than inventory warrants. 'You're going to see someone about this.'

'We are,' Pell says from the doorway.

Mara nods and lifts the ledger closed. She tears a small page from a loose notepad on the counter — two dates, two names, four blanks total — and sets it at the edge for you to take. No charge named. No explanation offered.

turns.player: Ask Mara if she knows who Dara Holt is — the senior east-channel irrigator — and whether Mara thinks she should know about this before or after you've seen the site.
turns.summary: Mara named Dara Holt — senior east-channel irrigator, house with blue gate-post on the way north — and advised seeing the site before briefing her.
turns.narration:
The question lands without preamble. Mara's chin lifts a fraction — not surprise, recognition.

"Dara Holt." She says the name the way you'd set down a tool you know well. "Senior irrigator, east channel, twenty-two years. She lives at the bend before the upper meadow — the house with the blue gate-post, you'll pass it on the way." She pauses, glancing at the note page she tore for you, then at Pell in the doorway. "After. She'll want to see it herself before she hears about it. You tell her first and she'll be halfway there before you've finished the sentence — and she'll miss something because she's hurrying."

Pell makes a small sound from the doorway — not quite agreement, but close.

Mara tucks the ledger under the counter with a flat pat. "Dara's practical. She won't panic, and she won't let politics run ahead of the evidence. But she has to be the one standing at the site." A beat. "She and Fen share a water-right on that east bend. If Fen's intake failed first, she may already have her suspicions. Don't be surprised if she's been there recently."

You pick up the valve failure timeline note from the counter edge. Four dates, two names, Mara's clean hand. The lamp flickers once as a door somewhere on the south lane opens and closes.

Outside, the morning is coming on. The blue gate-post Mara described is somewhere between here and the site.

turns.player: Head out and let Pell set the pace — she knows this ground better than you do.
turns.summary: Silas and Pell leave Millbrook on the north track — Dara Holt's house passed without stopping, the east channel audible ahead.
turns.narration:
You step out of Mara's store behind Pell, and she takes the lead without ceremony — a small adjustment of the coat, a check of the sky, and she's moving. Her pace is the pace of someone who has walked this path in mud and frost and dry summer both, and she doesn't modify it for company. You fall in half a step behind and to her right, Memory easy in the crook of your arm, the Valve Failure Timeline note folded into your breast pocket.

The village thins out quickly. Past the well, past the last of the garden terraces, the lane narrows to a packed-earth track between fieldstone walls. The dew is still on everything — fence-posts dark with it, the grass along the verge bent low. Huginn rides your shoulder without comment, watching the hedgerow as you pass. The morning smells of wet soil and last year's straw and, faintly, something else underneath it — a thread of metal on the back of the tongue, barely there. You notice it and say nothing.

Pell points out the bend without looking back. "Blue gate-post — that one." A house set back from the track, gate-post painted the blue of old work, woodsmoke already lifting from the chimney. Dara Holt's. She's awake. Pell doesn't slow. "After," she says, which is everything Mara said in a single word.

The track rises gently north of the bend, the terraced gardens giving way to rough meadow and willow scrub. The east channel runs somewhere to the right, audible before it's visible — a steady talk of moving water that sounds, this morning, just slightly wrong.

turns.player: When you reach the intake site, use Palework to read the failed valve housing directly — what ended here, and when, and what the rot's front looks like now.
turns.summary: Palework reads Fen's failed intake — iron dissolution, three crust layers, process older than the ledger by thirty days minimum, active edge at the collar throat.
turns.narration:
The east channel bends here — a slow curve of cut stone and packed earth where the water once ran clear and fast. Now it moves gray-green and sluggish, the surface catching the pale morning light in a way that doesn't quite resolve into reflection. The intake sits low in the bank: an iron-collared sleeve set into the stone, the valve housing rusted and seized, a green-gray crust thick at every point where the iron meets the water.

Pell crouches without being asked and runs her thumb across the crust — not testing it, just measuring it. 'Housing's gone,' she says. 'Collar too, probably.' She looks at you. 'Read it.'

You pass Memory to your left hand and crouch beside her, laying the fingers of your right hand flat against the valve housing — and Palework opens. Not dramatically. It is a forensic thing, quiet and specific: the rot explains itself the way a wound explains itself to someone who knows what to look for.

What ended here: the iron's integrity. Not a crack, not a joint failure — a dissolution, steady and patient, working inward from the water-facing surface exactly as you named to Pell at the smokehouse. The deposit isn't fouling the valve — it's consuming it, replacing iron with something that looks structural until you test it and it gives.

When: further back than Mara's ledger. The housing shows three distinct crusting layers — the outer two have the gray-green character of the current contamination, but the innermost layer is older, darker, more settled. World-day minus-eighteen is when the valve failed. The process started earlier — thirty days minimum, possibly longer.

The rot's front: still moving. The active edge is at the collar throat — the point where the iron transitions to the stone sleeve. The stone is unaffected. Whatever this is, it targets iron specifically and stops at stone, which means the channel walls are holding, and the intake housing is the furthest point of advance on this site.

Huginn drops from your shoulder to the bank edge and stands there, looking at the water. He does not croak. His silence has its usual timing.

Pell is watching your face. 'Well?'

turns.player: Mark the three crust layers visually — scrape a small section clean with the Belt Knife so you have a physical record to show Dara Holt — then tell Pell what you found.
turns.summary: Palework findings reported to Pell — iron-specific rot, three layers, thirty-day head start; knife-scraped section preserved for Dara Holt.
turns.narration:
The belt knife's edge comes off the hip clean. You pick a section of housing just above the waterline — away from the active front, where the crust is thick enough to show all three layers without disturbing what's still working — and scrape a strip clean with three short strokes. The layers come off in sequence: the outermost gray-green and waxy, the middle the same color but drier and more friable, and the innermost a dark reddish-brown that crumbles at the edge like old mortar. Iron oxide underneath, the base metal already thinned. You use the knife tip to cut a shallow bracket around the exposed section so the layers stay readable, then wipe the blade on the grass.

"Three layers," you tell Pell. "Outermost is fresh — still wet, still active. Middle is maybe three weeks old, which matches Mara's ledger. But the bottom layer is older — thirty days minimum before the first failure, maybe more. Whatever started this was in the water before anyone was counting sleeve blanks." You gesture at the collar throat, where the active front sits just under the stone transition. "The rot is targeting iron specifically. The stone is clean. This isn't fouling from debris or sediment — it's the water itself doing something to the metal."

Pell is quiet for a moment. Her thumb is still on the housing, and she presses it once, testing the depth of the seized valve through the crust. "Stone unaffected," she repeats, like she's filing it. She looks upstream — the gray-green water coming slow around the east bend, the willows on the far bank with their feet in it. "That's not a natural rot pattern. Stone should go first in acid water." She stands, brushing her knee. "Dara needs to see this section. Don't cover it back up."

Huginn hasn't moved from the bank edge. He's looking upstream, and the timing of his stillness is, as always, a comment.

turns.player: Follow the channel upstream a short way — see if the crust thickens or where the water transitions from gray-green to clear.
turns.summary: Upstream trace confirms crust thickens with distance — color deepens toward the willow bend and the upper reach.
turns.narration:
You stand and walk the bank north, Memory loose in your left hand. The channel cuts close to the slope here, the stone coping mossy on the shaded side, and you move slow enough to watch the waterline. The crust on the iron collar at Fen's intake was three layers deep and old. Fifty yards upstream it is four — you can see it clearly where the channel wall carries a thin iron banding-strip at the waterline, the same gray-green buildup, slightly thicker on the north-facing stone. The deposit is not uniform: it follows the iron. The stone beside each band is clean, the mortar between coping blocks untouched.

A hundred yards further the gray-green color in the water itself deepens, just slightly — a shift from the dilute morning tint you've been walking beside to something with a bit more body to it, closer to the color of old copper. The channel here runs through a cut section where the bank rises on both sides, and the current slows in the shade. The transition is not a line; it is a gradient, and you are walking upstream into it.

Pell comes up beside you without hurrying. She looks at the banding-strip deposit for a moment, then at the water. 'Thickening,' she says. It isn't a question. She tips her chin north toward where the channel disappears around a long willow-bank curve. 'That bend is the last one before the channel comes off the high meadow feed. Past the willows, you're into the upper reach — that's Dara's jurisdiction, properly.' She studies the water color another moment. 'She needs to see this section too. Not just the intake.'

Huginn has followed the bank at his own pace and is now ten yards ahead of you both, sitting on the coping stone at the edge of the willow-shade, looking upstream. His stillness is the timed kind.

turns.player: Use Order-Sense on the channel itself — see the whole design this water is part of, and where the deposit pattern points.
turns.summary: Order-Sense fails in the thin substrate — the channel reads as stone and water, not design. Huginn holds position at the willow edge.
turns.narration:
You set your hand against the coping stone and reach for Order-Sense — a restrained reach, conserved, the way you'd test weight before committing to a lift. The lattice here is thin, the substrate barely a suggestion under the cut-bank clay, and you feel the ability find almost nothing to anchor to. Concrete ground, concrete water, concrete iron: the channel does not want to be a design. It wants to be a ditch.

You get fragments. A shape that might be a network — east channel branching from something above, something feeding something else — but the connections dissolve before you can trace them. The deposit pattern you've read with your eyes stays more legible than anything Order-Sense can give you here. The channel wall. The iron banding. The way the crust thickens as the water runs from south to north. That much you have from walking, not from craft.

Pell is watching you from two steps back. She reads the result without asking. 'Nothing useful?'

'The lattice is thin here,' you say. 'The channel doesn't read as a system. Just stone and water.' You straighten and look north toward Huginn, still perched on the coping at the willow-shade edge, ten yards ahead, angled upstream. He has not moved. Whatever the Order-Sense could not give you, he is already looking at it.

turns.player: Walk to where Huginn is sitting — the willow bend, the edge of Dara's upper reach — and see what he's looking at upstream
turns.summary: Silas reaches the willow bend with Huginn — the copper-green water and continuous iron deposit confirm the source lies upstream, past Dara's boundary.
turns.narration:
The willow bend is ten yards. You cover it in a dozen steps, Memory's butt-end tracking the coping as you go, and crouch beside Huginn on the stone.

He does not move. He is looking north.

The channel here bends left and the willows lean over it from the far bank, trailing last year's dead fingers in the current. Past the bend the water is visible for thirty yards before another curve takes it out of sight — and the color is wrong. Not the dilute gray-green you have been reading since Fen's intake. Deeper. A definite copper-green, the color of old bronze left in rain, sitting in the current like something dissolved rather than suspended. The iron banding-strips on this section of coping are barely visible beneath the deposit: not four layers but a continuous mat, gray-green at the waterline and rust-brown above it where the water has retreated in dry spells and left a tidemark.

Huginn turns one eye toward you, then back north. He makes no sound. The timing of it is a comment.

Pell arrives at your shoulder a moment later, boots quiet on the stone. She looks at the water, then at the deposit on the coping, then at you — a short measured look that says she sees what you see and is waiting to hear what you make of it. Somewhere upstream, past that second curve, the source is doing whatever it is doing. The willow branches move. The water does not smell of iron here — it smells of something older, something the tongue does not have a word for. Not rot. Not clean.

turns.player: Cross the willow bend and follow the deposit upstream — see how far the source is and what it looks like at closer range. Dara's territory, uninvited, but the evidence is thickening.
turns.summary: Silas crosses into Dara's upper reach — iron-mat thickens continuously, and a buried amber light briefly wakes in the bedrock shelf before going still.
turns.narration:
You step across the willow bend without ceremony — one foot on the coping, one on the far bank, Memory braced across your body. Pell follows without a word. Huginn lifts from the stone and banks left, dropping ahead along the water line, and you take that as permission enough.

The deposit north of the bend is continuous iron-mat, not the discrete banding you've been reading since Fen's intake. Every coping block carries it at the waterline — rust-brown above the tideline, gray-green where the water still touches, and the smell is older here: metal and something underneath the metal, the way a tool left in a cave smells different from a tool left in rain. You walk slowly, Memory's butt-end tapping coping stones, and count the distance in paces. Thirty. Fifty. The copper-green deepens with every bend.

At seventy paces north of the willow, where the channel narrows and the cut-bank rises to shoulder height, the current runs across a flat shelf of exposed stone — and there, just below the waterline, something is moving. Not water-movement. Not current-ripple. A thin line of light, the color of old amber, threading along a crack in the bedrock shelf like a buried lamp seen through thick glass. It pulses once — or seems to — and then is still. The water flowing over it carries no trace. The shelf itself is unremarkable stone; the coping above it is rusted the same as everywhere else. Only that line, that one quiet amber thread, briefly present.

Huginn lands on the cut-bank rim above the shelf and stares down at it. He does not croak. His head tilts — the tilting he does when he is attending something — and after a moment he looks away north, as if that settles it.

The iron-mat thickens again twenty paces further on. Whatever is upstream is not far.

turns.player: Stop here and use Palework on the bedrock shelf where that amber light ran — read what the stone is part of, what ended or is ending in it.
turns.summary: Palework reads the bedrock shelf — the amber light is an ancient ended thing breathing out through a recent crack; the crack itself is new.
turns.narration:
You kneel on the bedrock shelf and press your palm flat against the stone beside the hairline crack — not Memory's tip, just your hand, the old reading posture. The cast is conserved, a narrow thread rather than a sweep: Palework has something to anchor to here where Order-Sense found nothing. Bedrock holds endings differently than cut-bank clay. It's been doing it for longer.

What you find is not rot in the stone. The stone is fine. What you find is that something *beneath* the stone has already ended — a long time ago, at a depth you can't precisely name — and the crack is where that ending is still breathing out. Not actively dying. Finished. But the finished thing is large enough that its finishing is ongoing: an exhalation across years, not minutes. The amber light you saw was exactly that — the pressure of something concluded, venting through the only gap the bedrock offers it. The iron in the water is downstream of this. Not the same process, but not unrelated — the stone's finished thing and the channel's active rot share a direction, if not a cause.

There's one detail that sits wrong. The ending below the shelf is very old, but the crack is not. The crack is recent — a season, maybe less. Whatever broke the stone open to let the old thing breathe, that happened close to now.

Pell has come up beside you. She reads the posture, the flat palm, the pause. She says nothing, but she's stopped moving.

turns.player: Use Deathsense on the crack itself — read what made it, how long it has, and whether it's still widening.
turns.summary: Deathsense confirms the crack is stable — not widening, pressure equalized — but the cause of the initial shift remains unread.
turns.narration:
You keep your palm on the stone and shift the cast — same contact point, different question. Palework read what was below; now you ask Deathsense what the crack itself is doing.

The place resists it. Not hostility — more like stone that has held one story for a very long time and does not easily admit a second reading. You work with it rather than against it, keeping the cast narrow, economical: a thread instead of a sweep. What comes back is thin but clean.

The crack is not dying. It is a pressure-release — a seam that opened when something below shifted, and it opened recently, within the last season as Palework already told you. Deathsense adds one thing Palework could not: the crack is stable. It is not widening. Whatever pressure opened it has equalized, at least for now. The ending below breathes out through it at a fixed rate, like a bellows that has found its resting position. Nothing in the crack's own structure is accelerating toward failure. This is not a fissure about to open further.

What Deathsense cannot confirm — and the stone refuses to yield — is what made it shift in the first place. The cause is upstream of what the crack can tell you. Something moved below, or was moved, and the stone responded. That question points deeper and further north, past what you can read from here with your hand on one shelf.

Huginn turns his head above you, once, toward the north bend. Pell has not moved.

turns.player: Tell Pell what Deathsense found — stable crack, pressure equalized, cause upstream — and ask what she makes of it from a smith's read on the iron.
turns.summary: Pell reads the coping iron as received, not leached — and names the crack's origin as a decision, not age.
turns.narration:
You straighten up from the shelf and wipe your palm on your thigh. "Crack's not moving," you say, keeping your voice low — not for secrecy, just for the stone's register. "Pressure below equalized when it opened. Something shifted upstream and this was where the valley let it out. The shift is done but the cause isn't — the cause is still up there."

Pell has been reading your posture the whole time. She steps forward now, crouches at the waterline, and runs two fingers along the edge of the crack the way you've watched her run a file along a collar seam — not touching the crack itself, just reading the stone to either side of it. Her thumb presses the iron-mat above the tideline. She rolls a flake between her fingers, looks at it, lets it fall.

"Iron's not leaching from the coping," she says. "The coping's *receiving* it — you can feel the difference when you press it, the way a bead of solder sits different on a cold join than a hot one. Whatever's coming up through that crack is already carrying the iron when it arrives." She stands, knocking the rust-flake from her hand. "And if the pressure equalized when the crack opened, then it opened because something below changed. Not age — age cracks stone slowly. This was a *decision*." She doesn't say it ominously. She says it the way she'd note an irregular hammer-mark in a piece — a shape that means a person was involved, whether they meant it or not.

Huginn shifts on the cut-bank rim above you. Once. His head stays turned north.

turns.player: Follow Huginn north — he's been attending the north bend since before the cast
turns.summary: Huginn leads north to a wider reach — old column stain behind separated stone facing predates the contamination event entirely.
turns.narration:
The copper-green water runs quieter past the bedrock shelf than it did at the willow bend — the channel narrows here, and the cut banks press in close enough that you can hear the current working against the iron-mat below the tideline. Huginn has not moved from the cut-bank rim. His head is still turned north, that unhurried attention he gives to things that are not quite finished yet.

You follow it.

The channel bends left forty paces on, and the bend opens into a wider reach — a low shelf of exposed stone on the west bank, the east bank a tangle of alder root and iron-dark sediment. The iron-mat on the coping here is thicker than anything you've seen downstream. Not a trace, not a stain — a continuous rust-brown crust above the tideline, gray-green where the water touches it, and between the two a sharp boundary line as clean as a forge-edge. Whatever is carrying iron into this water, it is doing it evenly, in volume, and it has been doing it long enough to build in visible layers.

At the far end of the shelf, a section of old stone facing has pulled away from the bank — not collapsed, just separated, the way a fitted course shifts when the ground beneath it moves. Behind the gap, visible even from here: a darker stain, older than the crust outside it, running down the exposed face in a column roughly the width of your hand. Not new discoloration. Old. Pre-existing. Whatever is below this reach has been breathing out through this bank longer than the current contamination event.

Pell stops beside you without being asked. She looks at the separated facing, then at the column stain, then at the crust line, and she does not say anything for a moment.

"That stain is older than the valve failures," she says finally. "Older than Mara's ledger. Older than the gray-green, maybe." She looks at you. "Something was here before the crack."

turns.player: Use Palework on the column stain — this is older material than the crack; press the reading there and see what ended in it, and when.
turns.summary: Palework on the column stain — old, concluded, enormous: a slow release over years, predating the contamination. Something vast drained through this bank long before the valley's water ran gray.
turns.narration:
You press your palm flat against the column stain and send Palework in shallow — a conserve cast, narrow thread, asking only what ended here and when.

The stain answers, but not completely. What comes through is old: older than the gray-green water, older than the valve failures, older than anything Mara's ledger could touch. The material you're reading is not iron-dissolution. It is something that seeped, slowly, over a long time — not a catastrophic end but a slow release, like a held breath let out over years rather than minutes. The ending underneath it is enormous and absolutely concluded. Whatever it was, it is not dying now. It finished a long time ago and this stain is the residue of that finishing, the dark watermark left on stone when something vast drained away.

What you cannot reach is the shape of it — what it was, what form it held, what caused it to end. Palework gives you the fact of the conclusion without the story behind it. The stain predates the contamination by years, possibly decades. The column is not a cause. It is evidence of a prior tenant.

Pell watches you from two paces back, not crowding the read. When you lift your hand she speaks without preamble: "That's not the same material as the waterline crust." She's been looking at where the stain edges meet the newer iron-mat. "Different direction. That came down through the stone, not up through the water."

turns.player: Ask Pell what she makes of 'came down through the stone' — she's reading the material direction; let her run with it
turns.summary: Pell reads the column stain's direction — source is above and behind the bank, not from the river; a buried structure draining down through the hillside, older than the contamination.
turns.narration:
You turn from the stain and look at Pell. "Came down through the stone," you say. "Not up from the water. What does that mean to you?"

She doesn't answer immediately. She's still crouched at the boundary where the column stain's edge meets the newer iron-mat crust — one finger tracing the line without touching it, the way she distinguishes a weld seam from a crack without committing to either. Then she rocks back on her heels and tilts her head at the facing stone lying on the bank beside you. "Stone holds direction," she says. "Water pushes up and out — you can read the grain of what it leaves behind. This one ran down and in, through the body of the bank, not through the channel face. That means the source was above and behind it." She taps the exposed cut. "Not the river. Something in the hillside. Or under it."

Huginn shifts on the cut-bank rim above — one step, then still again, head still north.

Pell straightens and wipes her hand on her apron, a habitual motion. "Old site drainage, maybe. A seep from a buried structure — the kind that runs clean for years until the structure settles and the path widens." She says it matter-of-factly, the way she'd diagnose a flue leak. "The stain predates the gray-green water. So whatever is up there moved first — and the river situation followed after." She looks at you directly. "Which means the crack in the shelf isn't the source. It's a symptom. The source is higher up, and it's behind stone."

Huginn takes two more steps north along the rim, deliberate as a finger pointing.

turns.player: Follow Huginn north — press further upstream and see what's behind the next section of bank
turns.summary: Huginn leads thirty paces further north — dressed rubble stones in the sedge, a buried coursed structure visible as a settling-seam in the hillside above.
turns.narration:
Huginn doesn't wait. He takes three deliberate hops north along the cut-bank rim, then lifts — a short flat glide over the channel — and lands on a root-spur jutting from the east bank, fifteen paces further upstream. His head turns back once, confirming you're following, then swings north again.

You move along the west bank with Memory in hand, Pell a step behind you. The iron-mat thickens as you go, not gradually but in a step — like crossing a threshold on the riverbed. The copper-green tint in the water deepens here too, pulling toward something more amber at the edges where the current slows against the bank. Thirty paces past the column stain, the exposed bedrock shelf ends and gives way to packed earth and old stone rubble — not natural scree, but the kind of fall that happens when something built stops being maintained. Dressed stones, maybe a hand's width each, sitting in the sedge at the base of the bank like they rolled there and stopped. They are not river-rounded.

Pell crouches at the nearest one without being asked. She turns it over, looks at the face, sets it down. "Coursed," she says. "Someone laid this." She straightens and looks at the bank above. The hillside here runs steeper than it should, and about ten feet up, the earth has a long seam in it — not a crack, not exposed stone, but a line where the soil settles differently, where the grass grows in two slightly different shades of green. A buried edge. The shape of something beneath.

Huginn is on the root-spur above the rubble stones, not moving. His eye is on the seam in the hillside, not on you.

turns.player: Before I use order-sense... i turn to Pell with a big grin - Date #2 is going swimmingly!  I'm glad we're here together!
turns.summary: Silas grins at Pell over the buried ruins — she gives him the corner of her mouth and tells him to keep his eyes on the seam.
turns.narration:
You turn from the rubble stones with Memory still in hand and catch Pell's eye — the grin comes out before you can think better of it. "Date two," you say, "and we've found buried ruins. Swimmingly."

She looks at you for a moment. Then the corner of her mouth moves — not a full smile, the kind she'd deny if asked, but real enough. She glances down at the dressed stones half-sunk in the sedge, then back at you. "Most people walk upriver for the view," she says. "You find infrastructure."

Huginn, on his root-spur, does not look at either of you. He is attending the settling-seam with the focused patience of something that has attended a great many things and knows which ones matter. His silence has the shape of a comment.

Pell steps past you and crouches at the nearest rubble stone, turning it to check the far face. The motion is the same one she uses on a piece of iron — not dismissive, just first things first. "Keep grinning," she says, without looking up. "But I want to know what's behind that seam."

turns.player: Use Order-Sense on the buried structure — read the whole design, including the parts not yet exposed
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the buried structure — a sealed channel-house, pre-Transition, two closed rooms, one aperture breached from within.
turns.narration:
You close your hand around Memory's shaft and let the craft run quiet — not a push, just a lean. The substrate is thin here; you feel it immediately, the lattice grudging, but the design has enough mass behind it that it surfaces anyway, the way a buried wall will show itself in a drought when the grass dies back in a line.

The buried structure comes through in outline, not in detail: a channel-house. Low, rectangular, older than anything in living memory — three rooms, or three functions, it's hard to say which. The courses you can see in the sedge are the northwest corner. The main run of the building goes into the hillside at an angle, following the slope, ending somewhere behind and above the settling-seam. Two apertures, sealed. One of them — the lower one, roughly where the seam is widest — has been breached. Not broken open from outside. Settled open from within, the way a body relaxes after it is finished.

What you cannot read is what it was for. The design tells you the shape and the intent of the shape — water management, yes, but older than the valley's current channels, older than the sleeve-blank fittings, older than Mara's ledger and Dara's twenty-two years combined. The closed rooms hold something the structure was built around. Order-Sense gives you the skeleton; it won't give you the marrow.

Pell is watching your face. Huginn has not moved from the root-spur.

turns.player: Tell Pell what Order-Sense found — channel-house, two sealed rooms, the lower aperture breached from within, not from outside. Ask what a smith reads in that.
turns.summary: Silas tells Pell what Order-Sense found; she reads the breach-from-within as a valve — deliberate design, not failure.
turns.narration:
Pell listens without interrupting.

You lay it out plainly: the channel-house below the hillside, three rooms or functions, the northwest corner exposed here in the sedge. Main run going in at an angle, following the slope. Two apertures — both sealed, sealed deliberately, the kind of closure that is meant to hold. And the lower one, the one closest to the river-level, breached from within. Not forced from outside, not collapsed by settling or water pressure. Opened from the inside, against the seal.

She's quiet for a moment after you finish. Then she crouches beside the nearest rubble stone and runs her thumb along the dressed face of it — the flat diagnostic touch you've seen her use on worked iron when she's reading something she doesn't want to rush. "From within," she repeats, not a question. She looks up at the settling-seam ten feet up the hillside, then back down at the coursed stones. "A smith reads that as a valve. You design a seal to hold — you put it in from the outside so there's no mechanism to fail on the inside. If it opens from within, either something was left in there with a key, or the design always intended it to open when a condition was met." She stands, brushing sediment from her thumb. "Neither of those is an accident." Her eyes track north along the buried structure's line. "The two sealed rooms — if this is a channel-house, they're either storage or they're chambers around something the builders didn't want the water touching directly." She pauses. "Or something they didn't want touching the water."

Huginn shifts once on his root-spur — a small adjustment, wings held close. He is still attending the settling-seam. He has not looked away from it since you arrived.

turns.player: So... you're strong, capable, beautiful, AND intelligent... quite a combo there Ms Marsh.
turns.summary: Silas flatters Pell at the ruins — she deflects with a word, but her ears say otherwise.
turns.narration:
You say it with the grin still on your face — "So. You're strong, capable, beautiful, and intelligent. Quite a combo, Ms. Marsh."

Pell doesn't look up from the rubble stone. Her thumb is still working the dressed face of it, reading the grain. A beat passes — the kind she chooses. Then: "Weir." Flat, final, as if that is the entire verdict.

But her ears have gone red. You can see it plainly in the early-spring light through the alders.

Huginn shifts his weight on the root-spur. One claw, then the other. He does not look at you. He does not look at Pell. He is attending the settling-seam with total, pointed dedication, and if his silence has a quality right now it is the quality of a very old thing that has decided this particular development is not its business, and means to hold that position.

turns.player: Ask Pell what she'd need to safely probe the lower aperture — her read, her call on risk.
turns.summary: Pell names the conditions for reading the aperture — clear the face first, then ask Huginn what he sees.
turns.narration:
The question lands easy. Pell finally looks up from the rubble stone, thumb still resting on the dressed face of it, and turns to read the settling-seam again — the full length of it, top to bottom, the widest point marking where the lower aperture gave.

"Probe is the wrong word for it," she says. "You don't probe a valve. You read the condition it's in, first. Then you decide whether opening it is yours to do." She stands, brushing grit from her knee. "What I'd need: a way to clear the sedge and loose fill from the face without shifting the coursework above it. A hand-length of exposed stone on either side of the breach. Light — not direct sun, it'll wash the grain. And time. You don't rush dressed stone."

She glances at Memory, then at your belt knife. "The kit you've got can clear the face. Takes maybe half an hour, careful work. What I can't tell you until the face is clear is whether the breach already finished its job or whether it's mid-process. Those read differently." A pause. "And I'd want to know what Huginn thinks before we touch anything."

She says it without inflection — what Huginn thinks — the way she'd say what the flue draft tells you before you open a sealed chimney. Three days ago she wouldn't have said it at all.

Huginn has not moved from the root-spur. His eye is on the settling-seam. His stillness has a texture to it — not waiting, exactly. Attending.

turns.player: Start clearing the face — belt knife and careful hands, sedge and loose fill only, nothing that shifts the coursework
turns.summary: Lower aperture face cleared — dressed ashlar frame exposed, seating-piece moved inward, inner darkness dry.
turns.narration:
The sedge comes away in dry handfuls — root-mats shallow here, no deep grip, the fill beneath them mostly loose river-silt and old leaf-duff that someone packed in years ago with no great care for permanence. You work the belt knife flat, slipping the tip under each clump and lifting rather than cutting, keeping the blade parallel to the coursework so nothing catches and drags. The fill layers beneath are distinct: top half dry and pale, bottom half darker, damp-compressed against the stone face — the difference in color marks where the waterline sat, season after season, before the hillside started to move.

Pell doesn't help, and she doesn't hover. She stands to your left at a useful distance, watching the coursework above the breach, tracking the settling-seam for any shift that would mean the weight is redistributing. Huginn has not moved from the root-spur. His eye is on the aperture face, the way it is always on the thing that is actually ending — not the work, not you, the thing itself.

Two feet of stone come clear. Then four. The lower aperture opens to you: a rectangular frame of dressed ashlar, no wider than a man's shoulders, no taller than chest height. The breach at its center is not a break — the stone hasn't failed. There is a gap where a seating-piece has moved inward, pushed back into the channel-house rather than out. The face around it is stained in the familiar copper-green iron-mat, thicker at the lower corners, tapering up. The inner darkness past the gap is dry — no water sound, no drip.

Pell steps up beside you. She reads the stone frame for a moment — the dressed faces, the ashlar courses, the way the seating-piece sits skewed in its channel — and then, without comment, she looks at Huginn.

turns.player: Ask Huginn what he sees — let him attend the aperture and read what it holds before anyone touches the seating-piece.
turns.summary: Huginn attends the cleared aperture and reads what's inside as old and finished — not dangerous, but present. Pell defers the next move to Silas.
turns.narration: Huginn reads the aperture — old, concluded, not ending. Pell names his attendance as the clearest permission she knows.

turns.player: Ask Pell to read the frame condition now — her full assessment before the seating-piece moves an inch.
turns.summary: Pell reads the aperture frame: sound joints, dry stone, clean channel — the structure has already made its move. Go in, or fetch Dara Holt first.
turns.narration:
You step back half a pace and let Pell have the frame. She moves into the space you've cleared with the particular economy of someone who learned a long time ago that the thing you're reading doesn't need you crowding it. Both hands go to the stone — not the seating-piece, the ashlar itself — thumbs pressing the lower corners of the frame where the iron-mat staining is thickest.

She works in silence for a minute. Reading the grain, running a thumbnail along the dressed face, checking the reveal depth on both jambs. When she speaks she doesn't look up.

"Frame's sound. Whoever built this knew their courses — the breach didn't come from stone failure, the joints are still tight except at the seating-piece channel." Her thumb traces the channel itself, the inch-wide slot the seating-piece rides in. "Channel's clean inside. No debris forcing it, no calcium bridging. The piece moved on its own — either something pushed from within or the trigger condition released it. Either way, the structure above is not in danger from what's in there. It has already made its move."

She straightens and steps to the right jamb, pressing her palm flat against the face. "Stone is dry. No hydrostatic load. Whatever those sealed rooms hold, they are not full of water." She glances at Huginn once — brief, factual — and then back to you. "Old, concluded, not ending. That's your read, that's my read. The seating-piece is still seated — skewed, but seated. You can shift it without collapsing the reveal, if you want what's inside. Or you leave it and you go fetch Dara Holt and you show her all of this before anyone puts a hand on it." A pause. "Both are legitimate. I am not choosing for you."

turns.player: Shift the seating-piece and see what's inside
turns.summary: Seating-piece shifted — the sealed chamber opens. Two rooms, dry stone, three iron ring-hooks, empty recesses, and a second aperture leading deeper.
turns.narration:
You get your hands on the seating-piece — both palms flat against the dressed face, fingers finding the channel edges Pell already identified. The stone is dry and cool, no hydrostatic resistance, no give-and-take with trapped water. You push laterally, the way the channel wants it, and the piece moves. Not easily — there's a century of mineral adhesion along the base-seam — but it moves without fighting you, grinding inward another four inches along its own track before the resistance drops and it simply stops, seated at the channel's inner lip like it was always meant to sit there.

The gap opens to shoulder-width. The smell comes first: mineral, dry, with something underneath it like old iron and sealed clay — not rot, not water, not anything alive. Cool air pushes outward against your face, the kind of still-cool that means a space untouched by seasons. Then the light makes sense of what's inside.

Two rooms, as Order-Sense read. The near one is a single chamber, maybe eight feet deep and six wide — dressed ashlar all the way round, the courses as tight as the frame outside. The floor is dry stone flags. Along the far wall, three iron ring-hooks are set into the courses at chest height, evenly spaced, and below each one a shallow rectangular recess, palm-width and palm-deep, cut directly into the stone. The recesses are empty. On the left wall, a second aperture — narrower, barely shoulder-width — leads into the second room, and whatever's beyond it is dark and still. No water sound. No drip. The column stain you read from outside runs up the right-hand wall and across the ceiling in a dark stripe, and it is old: the mineral crust on it is thick and even, decades at minimum. This room has been holding that stain without complaint for a very long time.

Huginn drops from the root-spur and lands on the cleared threshold stone, weight forward, head tilted. One eye on the ring-hooks. One eye on the second aperture. The bird is unhurried, which is its version of permission.

turns.player: Step into the near room and read the second aperture with Deathsense — what's ending in the deeper chamber, if anything
turns.summary: Silas reads the second aperture with Deathsense — the deeper chamber holds nothing ending, only something long concluded.
turns.narration:
You step across the threshold stone — Huginn shifts his weight but stays where he is, one eye tracking you — and into the near chamber. The floor is cold under your boots, dry flags, no give. The smell is old mineral and something else underneath: not rot, not moisture, just the particular stillness of a space that has been sealed long enough to forget air moves.

You face the second aperture. It's narrower than the one you came through, roughly dressed at the edges where the first is precise, as if cut in a hurry or cut by different hands. You set Memory's butt on the stone beside you and open Deathsense — not wide, not urgent. Conserve. You're asking a question, not forcing an answer. The ability comes up the way a slow breath does: deliberate, steady, without waste.

The read comes back plain. Nothing in the deeper chamber is ending. Nothing living, nothing active, nothing in the late stage of any kind of conclusion. The space beyond that aperture has already finished whatever it was going to finish — years ago, or decades, or longer. It is not dying. It is not dying toward anything. It is simply on the far side of an ending that already occurred. The Deathsense finds no trajectory to read, no cause underway, no pivot point where intervention would matter. It is a room that is done.

Pell is at the jamb behind you, not crowding. You can hear her breathing settle when you don't immediately say anything alarming. Huginn makes a single low sound from the threshold — not a warning, just acknowledgment, the way he marks a thing he already knew.

turns.player: Use Order-Sense on the near chamber first — read the whole design these ring-hooks and recesses belong to before going deeper
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the near chamber — a staging space for tools or instruments, regularly accessed, deliberately emptied.
turns.narration:
You don't force it. Three energy drawn, no more — a conserved read, the way you'd feel a lock's mechanism rather than test it with your shoulder. Order-Sense opens across the near chamber and you let it settle.

The design comes in pieces, not all at once. The ring-hooks read immediately: mounting points for something long, heavy, and meant to be accessed quickly — the recesses below aren't storage, they're rest-positions for whatever hung above. Poles, or rods. The spacing is deliberate, the cut precise, and the design is older than the channel infrastructure outside by at least a generation. Whatever this room served, it was here before Dara Holt's east channel. Before the valve. The ring-hooks and the second aperture belong to the same design — this room was a staging space, not a storage space. The things that hung here were carried through that inner aperture and used somewhere beyond it.

The substrate thins almost immediately. You feel the lattice starve out at the edges of the read, the design going soft where it meets the hill's deeper stone — you get the shape, not the full picture. But the shape is enough. Whatever this installation was for, it was accessed regularly, carried in and carried out, and then one day it wasn't. The recesses are clean-empty, not broken-empty. The things that hung here were removed on purpose.

Pell hasn't moved from the jamb. She reads your stillness correctly. "Something?" One word, level.

turns.player: Tell Pell what Order-Sense found — staging space, not storage. Whatever hung here was carried through the inner aperture and used beyond it. Ask if she's seen anything like it in other buried structures.
turns.summary: Silas reports the staging-space read to Pell; she names matched-pair carrying and recalls a similar site; Huginn walks to the center and faces the second aperture.
turns.narration:
You turn toward the jamb and find Pell already watching you — not impatient, just ready. 'Staging space,' you say. 'Not storage. The hooks held something long — rods or poles, carried through there regularly and brought back.' You nod toward the second aperture. 'The recesses are rest-positions, not mounts. Whatever they used in here, they brought it out when they were done. Every time. The room was emptied on purpose.' You pause, letting her read what that means the same way you did. 'Design's older than your east channel by a generation, maybe two. This was here before any of the infrastructure outside.'

Pell pushes off the jamb and comes across the threshold — one step, no more, getting a feel for the floor before she commits further. She looks at the ring-hooks with the same attention she gives a joint she hasn't seen the technique for: not suspicion, just the particular stillness of someone running calculations. 'Carrying poles,' she says, half to herself. 'Or rods with load in them. The hook-spacing says matched pairs — whatever they carried, they carried two at a time.' She moves to the nearest recess and crouches, running a thumb along the cut edges. 'Clean work. Not fast work. Someone cared that these didn't splinter whatever they were resting.' She looks up. 'I've seen one other like this. Deeper valley — old drainage site, before the Transition, stone-flagged cellar under a field barn. Ring-hooks, flagged floor, nothing else. Locals called it the wash-room.' She stands. 'Nothing was washed in it.'

Huginn drops from the threshold stone and walks — actually walks, four deliberate steps on the stone flags — to the center of the near chamber. He stops under the middle ring-hook, looks up at it once, and then looks at the second aperture. He does not croak. He does not look back at you. Pell watches him and says nothing for a moment. Then, quiet: 'So. We go through, or we don't go through.'

turns.player: Go through the second aperture — Memory in hand, Huginn already looking that way.
turns.summary: Silas steps through the second aperture into a larger chamber — worn foot-path, low ring-hook with a cradle-recess, a grid of pressed marks in the ashlar, and Huginn attending the far wall.
turns.narration:
You take Memory from the flags and step through.

The second aperture is tighter than the first — you angle the spear shaft to clear the rough-dressed edges, and your shoulder brushes stone on the left side. Then you're through, and the chamber opens around you.

It's larger than the near room. Not vast — maybe eight paces across, ten deep — but the ceiling lifts a full arm's length higher, and the air is different: drier, if anything, with a faint mineral edge that sits at the back of the throat. The flags continue underfoot, the same cut and spacing, but they have been worn slightly in two parallel lines that run from the aperture you just stepped through to the far wall. Foot traffic, repeated, over a long time. People came here and knew exactly where they were going.

The far wall holds something you did not expect: a third ring-hook, centered, lower than the three in the near chamber — set at hip height rather than chest height — and below it a recess wider and deeper than any of the ones behind you. Not a rest-position. A seat, or a cradle. To the left of the hook, at roughly shoulder height, a series of shallow marks have been pressed or struck into the ashlar: not runes, not script, but a regular grid of small squares, some filled, some empty, in a pattern you cannot immediately read. To the right: nothing. Just clean dressed stone and the column stain bleeding down from the ceiling, thicker here than in the near chamber, darkest at the top.

Huginn comes through behind you without a sound and walks directly to the center of the worn path. He stops at the far end of it — three feet from the cradle-recess — and stands very still, looking at the low ring-hook the way he looked at the bedrock crack this morning. Not a warning. Attending.

turns.player: Read the deeper chamber with Order-Sense — the grid of marks, the cradle-recess, the low ring-hook — what design does this room belong to?
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the deeper chamber — a fixed-purpose room, not a staging space; the object in the cradle-recess did not leave; the grid is a calibration surface, unfinished.
turns.narration:
The three energy leaves you quiet and precise — a conserved read, no wider than the room demands.

The design comes back in a single coherent shape, and it is not what the near chamber suggested. That room was a staging space, functional and repeated — objects in, objects out, the logic of a tool-chest. This room is something else. The worn parallel lines in the flags are not transit marks; they are stand-here marks, two positions, fixed and deliberate, where the same two people stood the same way for a very long time. The low ring-hook with its wide cradle-recess was not a rest-position between uses — it was the use. Whatever sat in that cradle sat there and worked from it, or was worked upon, and the design makes no provision for moving it elsewhere. The object this room was built around did not leave.

The grid of pressed marks reads last, and it takes a moment to resolve. Not a record, not a map — a calibration surface. The filled and empty squares are a binary register: some state tracked in increments, each mark pressed when a condition was met or changed. The design assumes whoever read it would know the key. You don't. But the architecture of the grid is clear enough — it runs left to right, oldest to newest, and the rightmost column is about two-thirds filled. Whatever it was tracking, it was not finished when this room was last used.

Huginn has not moved. He is three feet from the cradle-recess and he is attending it the way he attended the bedrock crack this morning — patient, unhurried, and entirely certain.

turns.player: Call Pell through — she should see the stand-marks and the grid before you touch anything
turns.summary: Pell enters the deeper chamber and reads the stand-marks as a two-person fixed operation — asks what the grid calibrated against.
turns.narration:
Pell's name carries easily through the aperture — the ashlar catches it and gives it back once, clean.

She comes through without hesitation, angling her shoulders the same way you did, and straightens in the larger chamber. Her eyes do what a practical person's eyes always do in an unfamiliar space: ceiling first, floor second, walls after. Then she finds the worn stand-marks in the flags, and she stops.

'There.' She crosses to the nearer of the two positions and puts her boot toe to the worn stone — not testing it, just occupying it, the way she'd stand at a forge station to read the angles. Her gaze tracks forward to the low ring-hook, then left to the grid. 'Two-person operation. Same stance, same facing, every time.' She looks back at you over her shoulder. 'You'd have to be consistent about it. Exact. Same spot, or the read comes out different.' She hasn't touched the grid, but she's studied it the way she studies a joint before she cuts — taking in the geometry of it. 'What was it calibrating against? There's no instrument here. No table. Nothing to put beside it and compare.'

Huginn has not moved from his position facing the cradle-recess. He is attending it the way he attended the bedrock crack and the open bull — that quality of patience that is not waiting for something to happen but recognizing that something already did.

Pell glances at the bird, then at you. She doesn't say anything about him. She's learned that silence around Huginn is the more honest response.

turns.player: Read the cradle-recess with Palework — it held something; what does the stone remember of it?
turns.summary: Palework reads the cradle-recess — the object left with purpose, not abandonment; its field leached iron from the stone beneath over years of use.
turns.narration:
You crouch at the cradle-recess and press two fingers to its stone. Three energy, no more — a conserved read, the question kept small.

The recess remembers contact. Not the object itself — Palework reads endings, and whatever sat here did not die here, only departed — but the stone holds the record of long pressure: a shape pressed into the cradle over years, maybe decades, of the same weight in the same position. Wide at the base, narrowing as it rose. Dense. The iron content in the stone directly beneath the cradle is fractionally different from the surrounding flags — leached, over time, by something with its own field. Not biological. Not quite mechanical either, by any category you can name cleanly.

What ended here was *use*. The object was placed, operated, and removed — many times, always the same way, always by two people standing at those marks behind you. The last time it was removed, it was not brought back. The recess has been empty long enough that the ending of its use reads as thoroughly concluded — no near-ending, no imminent return. Whatever it was, it left with purpose, not abandonment.

Pell is watching from the nearer stand-position. 'Anything?' She keeps her voice even, but she hasn't moved her boot from the worn stone.

turns.player: Try the farther stand-position — step to it, face the cradle-recess, and use Order-Sense from that exact fixed point. Maybe the design reads differently from where the operators stood.
turns.summary: Order-Sense from the farther stand-position — the chamber has paired operator/confirmer roles; the grid records states, not measurements; the calibration key left with the object.
turns.narration:
You step to the farther stand-position and let your boot find the worn stone. It receives you — not welcome exactly, more like a groove that accepts the key it was cut for. The fit is right. You face the cradle-recess, Memory loose in your right hand, and open Order-Sense conserved and narrow: three energy, no wider than the room asks.

The read comes back differently. Not more — not richer detail or greater range — but oriented. From this position the cradle-recess stops being an object in the room and becomes the room's axis. The whole chamber resolves around it: the grid to your left isn't a wall-surface, it's a face you read from here, the way a smith reads a finished edge from the grip end, not the blade end. The two stand-positions are paired input and verification — whoever stood at the nearer position operated; whoever stood here confirmed. The design has an error-checking step built in.

The grid's blank fields resolve a fraction more clearly from this angle too. Not enough to read what it measured — the calibration key is still absent, stripped with the object — but you can see the filled marks are grouped, not random. A running count, or a series of states. The object in the cradle had modes, and someone stood exactly where you're standing now to record which mode it was in.

Pell hasn't moved from her stand-position. She's watching you with the same attention she gives a piece of iron that's telling her something she hasn't named yet. Huginn is still three feet from the cradle-recess, attending the ring-hook like he's keeping an eye on something that might decide to move.

turns.player: Tell Pell what the second position reveals — operator and confirmer, the grid records states, the design had an error-check built in
turns.summary: Silas reports the operator/confirmer design to Pell; she names the error-check and gestures toward the valve sites.
turns.narration:
You straighten from the farther stand-position and look across the chamber at Pell.

'Two roles,' you say. 'The person here—' you tap the worn stone with your heel '—reads the grid and calls the state. The person at your position confirms it. The grid doesn't measure anything against an outside standard. It records what the object was doing — modes, conditions, something like that. The calibration key left with the object, so whoever took it could still use the grid to verify states on the far end.' You pause, letting the geometry of it settle. 'Someone stood in that exact spot and checked your work. Every time.'

Pell doesn't move. Her boot toe is still on the nearer worn stone, her gaze on the low ring-hook. She's quiet the way she's quiet at the forge when a piece tells her something she didn't expect — absorbing it, not resisting it.

'Error-check,' she says finally. It isn't a question. 'Built into the floor.' Her eyes go to the grid. 'So whatever that object did — whatever it was doing here — they didn't trust a single reading of it.'

Huginn turns his head from the ring-hook to you, then back. Once.

The chamber holds that. The faint mineral smell, the ceiling higher than it needs to be, the worn parallel paths in the flags going nowhere now that the object is gone. The grid's binary marks wait at shoulder height to your left — filled and empty, grouped — and you understand them well enough now to know you're reading an account ledger for something that no longer exists in this room. Whatever was seated in that cradle-recess, someone counted its states carefully, with a witness, for long enough to wear the stone.

Pell looks at you across the chamber. 'The valve sites,' she says. Her voice is level but there's something moving under it — the same thing that moved in her when she named the column stain's direction. 'Tell me you're thinking what I'm thinking.'

turns.player: Step to the ring-hook and use The Attended End — what is concluding here, and how long has it had?
turns.summary: The Attended End finds the cradle-recess's function concluded generations ago — the room's job ended; Huginn flags the grid.
turns.narration:
The ring-hook is iron, and old — not Pell's work, not anyone's work you can name. You rest two fingers on it, Memory's haft against your shoulder, and let The Attended End open just enough to ask the question.

It answers sideways.

Not the hook itself — the hook is not ending, has not been ending, will not end on any timeline you can feel. What answers is the *function* it held. Something was last set in this cradle a long time ago — longer than the current contamination, longer than the valley's living memory of the east channel — and the role the room was built around concluded then. Not destroyed. Concluded. The way a watch-post concludes when the threat it watched for either arrived or passed: the purpose burned down to nothing, and the room has been waiting since in the particular stillness of things that finished and were not told.

How long? Marrow's sense of it comes back uncertain at the far end, which means *generations* — more than forty years, less than two hundred. The room did not die. Its job did.

Huginn turns his head from the ring-hook to you and holds the look for a moment, then shifts it to the grid of pressed marks on the left wall. One beat. A timing, not a performance.

Pell has not moved from the nearer stand-position. Her eyes are on your hand at the hook, reading the read.

turns.player: Read the grid — press The Attended End against a filled mark and ask what state it recorded last
turns.summary: The Attended End reads the filled marks — three consecutive stable-state confirmations; the object was removed at completion, not in crisis.
turns.narration:
You move left from the ring-hook and press two fingers against the nearest filled mark in the grid — a small depression, smooth at the center from repeated contact, darker than the empty ones around it. You don't ease into The Attended End. You push it, hard, the way you'd lean your whole weight on a door you need to open now.

The mark answers in kind.

What comes back is not a measurement. It is a state — the last state recorded before the object in the cradle-recess was removed. The mark holds a condition, the way a body holds the shape of the thing that broke it: the object was present, seated fully, field-active at its working intensity, and whatever it was reporting — to the operator at your position, to the confirmer at Pell's — was a number between two limits. Not high. Not low. Stable. The grid recorded that stability, and then the recording stopped, and the mark has been holding that last read ever since, the way a lamp holds heat after the flame is out. You can feel the stillness of it under your fingers: not dormant, not dead. Finished. The job it was asked to do was done, and done clean.

You pull your hand back and look at the row above — two more filled marks, both in the same cluster, both carrying the same flavor. Not a progression. Three confirmations of the same steady state. The object was checked, found stable, checked again, found stable, and on the third check someone lifted it from the cradle and walked out. No emergency. No failure. A completed reading followed by deliberate removal.

Pell has not moved. Her boot is still on the nearer stone, her arms loose at her sides, watching your face rather than your hand.

'Stable,' you say. 'Three consecutive confirmations of the same state — whatever the object was doing, it was doing it right. Then someone took it out cleanly.' You look at the marks again. 'This wasn't a failure. Someone was satisfied with what they saw and took it with them.'

turns.player: Walk the full grid — count the filled marks, note how they cluster, and see whether the pattern maps to anything you've seen in rune-work or channel geometry
turns.summary: Full grid walk — four verification cycles mapped, three-beat state-grammar identified; the object was managed on a schedule, not abandoned.
turns.narration:
You move along the grid methodically, two fingers trailing from mark to mark without pressing — reading the pattern the way you'd read a rune-sequence on worked bone, counting clusters before you count individual marks.

There are thirty-one filled marks in total. They fall into four distinct groups, separated by two or three empty rows — not random scatter, not a continuous measurement run. Four groups, each tight, each with its own internal repetition. The first group holds seven marks, the second nine, the third nine again, the fourth six. The last three in the fourth group are the ones you already pressed: the clean completion cluster, the stable-state confirmation before removal. But the earlier groups don't read as confirmations of the same thing. The spacing is different — tighter within each cluster, longer between clusters, as if each group was recorded over a compressed period and then left to rest before the next. Not daily logs. Episodes.

The clusters map. Not to channel geometry — not directly — but to something close in structure to the rune-sequences you use for state-binding: a mark for condition-present, a mark for condition-held, a mark for condition-checked. The three-mark rhythm inside each cluster. It's not the same grammar — it's older, and it works in stone rather than iron — but the logic is the same logic. Someone was running a verification cycle: the object enters a state, holds it, gets checked. Four cycles, separated by silence. The fourth cycle ended with the object being removed in good standing. The first three ended with the object still in the cradle.

You step back and look at the whole grid. Thirty-one marks. Four episodes. The same three-beat structure in each. And the gap between the first episode and the fourth is longer than the gap between the sleeve blank failures and now — considerably longer. Whatever this room was tracking, it tracked it across time and across multiple states, and someone was here for each one.

'Four runs,' you say aloud, not turning from the grid. 'Same structure each time — condition present, condition held, condition checked. Three beats per cycle. The last cycle ended with a clean removal.' You do turn then, and look at Pell. 'The first three cycles — the object stayed. Someone came back for it three separate times, checked it, and left it running. The fourth time, they took it.' You tap the valve timeline note in your breast pocket without pulling it out. 'I want to know how old the first cluster is. Because if someone was cycling through this room on a schedule, and the schedule stopped, the sleeve blank failures might not be about the object at all — they might be about whatever the object was managing losing its manager.'

turns.player: You've mapped what the room can tell you — report the full picture to Pell and move: the valve sites are waiting, and Dara Holt will want this framing before she makes any decisions about the east channel
turns.summary: Silas reports the full grid picture to Pell — scheduled management, clean removal, post-removal failures — and the party moves toward the valve sites.
turns.narration:
You step back from the grid and turn to face Pell at the nearer stand-position, letting your fingers drop. The count is already ordered in your head — no need to rehearse it.

"Four episodes," you say. "Thirty-one marks total, clustered in four groups — seven, nine, nine, six. Each group runs the same three beats: condition present, condition held, condition checked. The last three marks in the fourth group are a clean completion sequence. Whatever sat in that cradle was confirmed stable, then removed on purpose. Not abandoned, not broken. Someone came here, did the work, and took the device out when the work was done." You pause. "The episodes weren't continuous — there are gaps in the pattern. Managed operation, scheduled. Someone was coming back at intervals. When they stopped coming, the room stopped recording."

Pell's boot lifts off the nearer stone. She does not ask a clarifying question — she was confirming what she already half-suspected, and the confirmation fits. "So the device was functional when it left," she says, not quite to you. "And the sleeve blank failures started after the removal. Not before."

"After," you confirm. "Whatever the device was managing — the channel pressure, the filtration load, the valve seating — it managed it cleanly for however long it ran. When someone took it out, the system had nothing to pace against. The blanks started failing within weeks." You glance at Huginn, who has finally shifted — one slow quarter-turn, attention moving from the grid to the aperture behind you as though the chamber has said what it has to say. You take that as agreement. "Dara Holt needs to hear this before she makes any decisions about the east channel. Let's go find the valve sites first — I want to see what the ground looks like before I tell her what's below it."

turns.player: Pause at the aperture threshold and look back at the chamber — note down the interval question in plain terms on the Valve Failure Timeline note before the detail fades
turns.summary: Silas notes the interval question on the timeline note before leaving the deeper chamber.
turns.narration:
You pause at the second aperture and turn back once more, letting your eyes settle on the chamber as a whole — the worn flags, the four-clustered grid, the empty cradle-recess, the column stain ascending to the ceiling corner. The detail is fresh but the kind of fresh that fades once you're moving on uneven ground.

You pull the Valve Failure Timeline note from your breast pocket — Mara's torn page, her clean hand — and press it against the door jamb. There's enough morning light threading through the near chamber from the outer gap. You write small in the margin, using the belt knife's butt to anchor the paper flat:

*Four episodes. Gaps between clusters — uneven. Who came back, and how often? Device removed at completion — where did it go?*

That's the question the room won't answer. The grid records what the object did; it records nothing about the hand that set it or the hand that lifted it out. Someone was returning on a schedule, and then they stopped. Whether they stopped because the work was finished or because they couldn't come back is the thing you cannot read from marks in stone.

You fold the note back into your breast pocket. Huginn is already through the aperture, a dark shape on the near-chamber flags, head angled toward the outer light. Pell stands a step behind you, boot off the nearer stand-position at last, waiting without impatience.

"Written," you say. She nods once — not at you, at the fact of it — and moves toward the light.

turns.player: Ask Pell the interval question as you walk out — she read the stain from deposit grain; she may have an instinct about the return schedule
turns.summary: Pell reads the stain bands as seasonal return cycles — the device was checked when conditions demanded it, not by calendar; the party moves north toward Fen's intake.
turns.narration:
The near chamber light is thin but real after the deeper room's dark — your eyes adjust as you step through behind Huginn, who is already posted on the flagstone nearest the outer gap, head canted toward the morning.

Pell is a half-step ahead of you, moving without hurry. You ask it plainly, as you walk: the gaps between the four clusters were uneven — does she have a feel for what kind of schedule would produce that? Someone returning to confirm a device's state, but not on a fixed calendar. The deposit grain, the stain's layers — she reads accumulation the way you read a grid.

She doesn't stop walking, but she does slow. "Seasonal," she says, after a moment. "Not weekly, not monthly-fixed. The deposit grain on that stain reads three distinct bands above the old bleed-line — two winters, maybe three. If someone was coming back to run those episodes, they weren't running them on a count. They were coming when the conditions told them to." Her boot finds the outer threshold and she steps through into the pale morning without looking down. "Which means whatever the device was measuring, it had a natural cycle. You check it when the cycle is due, not when the calendar says."
