Huginn drops off the flags and glides through the outer gap ahead of you both, angling north without being told. The hillside smells of wet sedge and cold stone. Fen's intake is twenty minutes up the path, and the light is still low enough that the dew hasn't lifted from the grass yet. You step out into the open air, Memory at your shoulder, the annotated timeline note back in your breast pocket. The interval question has a shape now: seasonal return, condition-triggered — and then the returns stopped.

turns.player: Ask the follow-up — if the return was condition-triggered and seasonal, what season would demand a check? Try to narrow the last visit's probable timing from the stain bands.
turns.summary: Pell narrows the last return visit to a seasonal condition-check — melt or first freeze, one to two winters back; contamination delay noted; Fen's intake in sight.
turns.narration:
The path north is uneven but familiar now — you've walked it twice in twenty-four hours — and Pell sets an easy pace that lets you talk without watching your feet.

"If it's condition-triggered," you say, "what condition? What season brings someone back to check a field-active device in a hillside?"

Pell doesn't answer immediately. She's looking at the scrubland to the left, the way she looked at the column stain — not at it, slightly past it, reading what the grain suggests rather than what the surface shows. Huginn banks wide overhead, angling north.

"Melt," she says finally. "Or the start of freeze. Those are the two moments a buried structure changes state — pressure above shifts, drainage shifts, temperature at depth drops or climbs past a threshold. If the device was field-active and the chamber was built to protect it from those swings, you'd check it at the turn, not in the middle of a stable season." She steps over a root. "The topmost stain band was fresh — I said two winters. But if the most recent visit was autumn-melt check, that's consistent. Someone came at the start of last freeze, confirmed the device still seated and stable, and then stopped coming back. Either completed the program, or —" She doesn't finish the clause, but the shape of it is plain.

Or couldn't come back. You don't say that either. You pull the Valve Failure Timeline note from your pocket and look at the margin annotation in the pale morning light. The managed visits were seasonal, condition-triggered — autumn thaw or first freeze. The last stop: one to two winters ago, no later. The contamination in the river is three weeks old at most. Whatever was removed from the cradle-recess, it wasn't recent — and the contamination followed the removal by a long interval, which means the watershed didn't respond immediately. It responded when something else changed.

That's worth noting, and you do, in the same small hand, pressed against your palm.

Ahead through the sedge, Huginn drops to a fence-post and holds — marking Fen's intake without fanfare. The valve site is close.

turns.player: Keep moving to the intake, but work out loud with Pell — removal interval, contamination delay, the season question — say the chain and see where she stops you.
turns.summary: Silas and Pell work the removal-to-onset chain aloud on the path — two winters from first slip to channel. The intake valve cover is visibly off true.
turns.narration:
The path levels out as you close the last twenty paces to the intake. Huginn has not moved from the fence-post — still, wings tight, watching the ground near the valve housing the way it watches things that are already ending.

You say it as you walk, piecing the chain aloud for Pell, because speaking it is different from holding it in your head. The device was managed — four episodes, three-beat grammar, condition-confirmed each time, removed at completion. The return schedule was seasonal, condition-triggered. The stain bands put the last visit at melt or first freeze, one to two winters back. Then nothing. No further visits. And the contamination came after — not immediately after, but after. There's a gap between the device's removal and the river going gray-green. Something else triggered the onset.

Pell walks with her hands free, a half-step to your left. She listens without interrupting, which you've learned means she's comparing what you're saying against something she's already assembled on her own side. When you get to the gap — removal to onset — she stops you. Not with a word. She just slows, then stops, and looks at the ground near the intake housing before she speaks. "The melt after removal would have been the first season that structure ran without management. If it was built to hold a state the device was maintaining — pressure, drainage, temperature — the first unmanaged melt is when you'd see the first slip. Not catastrophic. Just the start." A pause. "One winter to slip. Another winter for the slip to reach the channel."

Huginn shifts on the post, settling its wings with a sound like a wet flag. The intake housing is plain ahead — iron sleeve, ashlar surrounds, valve cover seated flush. Or nearly flush. Even from here you can see the lip isn't quite true.

turns.player: Have Pell take the first read — she diagnosed the column stain faster than you did; let her look at the physical housing before you add your own perception on top of hers.
turns.summary: Pell reads the valve housing — twisted sleeve, torqued cover, first failure confirmed physical. A girl with a hoop passes without alarm.
turns.narration:
The cover lip is off by perhaps three fingers at the northwest quadrant — not a dramatic failure, not something you'd catch walking past in poor light, but Pell sees it immediately. She crouches without preamble, one hand braced on the ashlar surround, and runs a thumb along the edge where iron meets stone.

"Housing's sound," she says. "The lip's riding up because the sleeve inside has twisted — see here, and here." She doesn't look at you when she says it, just taps two points on the iron with a knuckle, the way she'd indicate a flue joint. "The blank failed and the sleeve torqued on its axis when it went. The cover followed." She stands, wipes her thumb on her apron, and finally looks at Fen's field gate — the pole-and-crossbar arrangement thirty paces east where the actual channel enters the furrow system. "That's the first failure. World-day minus-eighteen, Mara's ledger said."

Huginn drops off the fence-post. It doesn't fly — just walks, three deliberate hops, to the edge of the ashlar surround, and stands there looking down at the housing the way it looks at things it has already finished assessing.

On the road behind you, unhurried, a girl of maybe ten is rolling a hoop along the verge with a stick, a grey dog loping at her heels. She slows when she sees the three of you — the man with the spear, the blacksmith crouching in the mud, the large grey-black bird standing on the stonework — and gives it a moment's frank consideration before deciding this is fine and carrying on past, hoop clattering over a rut, dog ignoring everything.

Pell watches her go. "East furlong failure is nine days later," she says, returning to it. "Two sites, same blank type. If the sleeve geometry is the same there, we can check whether the torque direction matches — same source stress, or two separate failures."

turns.player: Do you know the girl and the dog?  Or are they out of place?
turns.summary: Pell identifies the girl as Fen's niece; the dog is unknown, follows her in from the upper meadow path.
turns.narration:
You glance toward the path where the girl and her hoop disappeared — down the slope toward the village edge, the dog already a grey blur forty paces behind her. Neither in a hurry, neither looking back.

Pell tracks your gaze. "Don't know the dog," she says, flat and easy, the way she'd say she doesn't know a tool's maker. "Girl's Fen's niece — stays some mornings when Fen's working the channel early. She runs that hoop from the north gate to the well and back until someone feeds her." She pauses, hands on her apron. "Dog turns up with her sometimes. Not hers. Follows her in from the upper meadow path."

Huginn makes a short sound from the ashlar edge. Not a croak — something drier. It is watching the direction the dog went, not the girl.

Pell glances at the bird, then at you. "It's not a local dog," she adds, as if she'd just worked that out herself. "I'd have shod it by now if it were."

turns.player: The dog coming in from the upper meadow path catches. Ask Pell if she's seen it before — and when it started appearing.
turns.summary: Pell places the unknown dog's first appearance at three weeks back — same span as the sleeve failures and the boar displacement.
turns.narration:
The dog is already gone — just the trampled grass where it cut back toward the upper meadow path, and Huginn still watching that direction with the particular quality of attention it reserves for things it has already filed away.

"Started showing up maybe three weeks back," Pell says. She's not looking at the path anymore; she's back on the valve housing, tracing the lip angle with one finger. "Give or take. Always with the girl, always in from the north. Never comes down into the village proper — turns back at the edge." She taps the iron once, a flat sound. "First time I saw it I thought it was a farm dog that had wandered. But it's the same animal every time, same route, same behavior. And it's not a farm dog."

She doesn't elaborate on how she knows that. You have watched her read material evidence for two days and you understand she means it the way she'd mean a joint is forge-welded rather than riveted — not a feeling, a fact, the kind that comes from knowing what farm dogs look like under working conditions.

Huginn drops from the ashlar surround and lands in the damp grass a few paces toward the upper meadow path. It stands there, not moving in that direction, just marking the bearing. Then it looks at you.

Three weeks. The same span as the boar pushing off the riverside sedge. The same span as Mara's first sleeve blank sale.

turns.player: Note the dog timeline on the Valve Failure Timeline — three weeks, upper meadow path, same span as the first sleeve failure. Keep the record tight before walking to the east furlong.
turns.summary: Silas annotates the timeline with the dog's interval, and the party orients toward the east furlong.
turns.narration:
The Valve Failure Timeline note is already out before you've finished the thought. You unfold it against your thigh — Mara's neat hand, the four dates, the two names — and add three lines in the margin in your own: unknown dog, upper meadow path, first seen approx. three weeks ago — same span as Fen's intake failure. Same span as boar displacement north.

You fold it back into your breast pocket. The record is tight now: sleeve failures, boar moving off the water, a dog coming in from the north. Three separate observations that share an interval. Whether they share a cause is the question you don't write down, because Huginn is still watching the upper meadow path and that is already an answer of a kind.

Pell stands, brushing grit from her palm against her apron. She's done with the valve housing — it told her what it had to tell. "East furlong's a quarter mile," she says, already orienting south-east along the path. "Same sleeve blank stock, if Mara's note has it right. We'll know inside two minutes whether it twisted the same way."

Huginn lifts from the damp grass without ceremony, crosses overhead, and lands on the fence-post at the field gate thirty paces east — pointing the direction without being asked.

turns.player: Before moving to the next spot, pull out your waterskin and some starfruit and other snacks.  Take a breather with Pell in the early morning sun... watch her in the sunlight and note her beauty.
turns.summary: A quiet breather at the dry-stone wall — starfruit, waterskin, morning sun on Pell's face before the east furlong.
turns.narration:
You call a halt before the east furlong path bends out of sight of the village — just a dry-stone wall with the sun coming over the ridge behind it, low and pale gold, the dew still on the scrub grass. You unshoulder the pack and pull out the waterskin and the last of the trail parcel, and then, on instinct, the sealed packet of dried starfruit — the second one, the one you kept back. You'd given Pell the wine and the first packet at the forge. This one is yours, and you split it anyway.

Pell takes a piece without ceremony and leans her forearms on the top of the wall, looking southeast toward the east furlong. She's in her working apron, hair pushed back, a smear of iron-grime on the side of her left wrist that she hasn't bothered with. The morning light is the kind that doesn't flatter — it just shows things as they are, exact and without softness — and what it shows is a woman who is entirely comfortable in her own proportions: the set of her shoulders, the economical way she chews, the small crease at the corner of her eye when she squints into the low sun. Not performing ease. Just having it. You watch her for a moment longer than is necessary and don't look away when she catches you at it.

She raises an eyebrow. Doesn't say anything. Eats another piece of starfruit.

Huginn drops off the gate-post and lands in the grass ten feet away, working something out of the earth with focused patience. The valley is quiet at this hour — a cart starting somewhere south of the well, a bird you can't name, the very faint sound of the channel running. Pell hands the packet back, takes a pull from the waterskin when you pass it, and nods toward the southeast path. "East furlong's half a mile," she says. "Ready when you are." Not a rush. Just naming the fact of the day ahead.

turns.player: Take the breather further — sit down properly, let the energy come back. The east furlong will still be there in twenty minutes.
turns.summary: A proper breather at the dry-stone wall — waterskin, last of the starfruit, morning sun, Pell's shoulder against yours.
turns.narration:
You sit down on the low wall rather than just leaning against it, and after a moment Pell drops down beside you — close enough that her shoulder is warm against yours. Huginn relocates from the grass to a fence-post a little ways off, settles, and goes still. The morning is doing what early-spring mornings do when nothing is pressing: it just opens.

The starfruit is almost gone. You pass the waterskin back and forth without ceremony, and the silence between you is the comfortable kind — the kind that doesn't need filling. The sun is still low, striking the dew on the scrub at a flat angle so each blade carries a small white point of light. Pell's profile against all of that: jaw, the line of her neck, the smear of iron-grime she hasn't bothered with. She's looking southeast, the same direction she's been looking since you stopped, but there's nothing urgent in it. Just someone who looks at the land the way she looks at a piece of work — taking stock, without hurry.

You eat the last piece of starfruit. Your boots are wet with dew at the toe. Somewhere below the ridge a woodlark is running its loop, the same four bars, patient as a water wheel. Your shoulders come down a full inch you didn't know were up.

"Twenty minutes," Pell says. Not a question — just accounting. She doesn't move.

turns.player: So, how is this as a second date?  What do you think of me?
turns.summary: Pell answers plainly at the wall — thorough, methodical, and she hasn't decided yet. Ask her again after the valve.
turns.narration:
The question comes out easy, lighter than you feel it — *so, how is this as a second date?* — and you watch her from the corner of your eye the way you've been watching her all morning, waiting for the small thing her face does before her mouth catches up.

Pell doesn't look at you right away. She's got her forearms resting on her knees, waterskin loose in one hand, looking southeast at the furlong path with the particular quality of attention she gives to things she's already decided something about. The morning sun catches the side of her neck, the iron-grime at her collar, the loose strand of hair she hasn't bothered to push back a second time.

Then she turns her head — not quickly, not performing it — and looks at you the way she looked at you across the forge bench this morning: direct, considering, entirely unbothered by what she finds. "Valve housing," she says, "mystery dog, annotated field notes, and you brought the good starfruit." A beat. The corner of her mouth moves — not quite a smile, something more private than that. "I think you're thorough." Her shoulder presses a little more solidly against yours, deliberate. "I think that's more than most people manage on a first date, never mind a second."

Huginn makes a sound from the fence-post — a single low croak, content-free, the way it comments on things it has already catalogued. Pell glances at it, then back at the furlong. "What do I think of you." She says it flat, turning it over. "I think you showed up in Millbrook with a strange bird and a half-finished spear and you have been methodical about everything since, including me. I haven't decided if that's reassuring or not." The not-quite-smile is back. "Ask me again after we see what the east furlong does to the sleeve."

turns.player: I LIKE that you aren't quite sure... I LIKE that you think I'm methodical - but if you want to see how thorough I can be... that will have to wait for a third date... (and with that head toward the next valve)
turns.summary: Silas delivers the third-date line and the party moves southeast — Pell files it in the correct ledger and falls into step.
turns.narration:
You say it with the half-smile already on your face — *I like that you aren't quite sure* — and let the pause do what it will before the rest comes: *I like that you think I'm methodical. But if you want to see how thorough I can be, that'll have to wait for a third date.* You're already pushing off the wall as you finish it, pack settling onto your shoulders, Memory shifting at the balance point.

Pell doesn't laugh. What she does is almost better — a single exhale through her nose, barely a sound, and when you glance back at her she's looking at you with the expression of someone recalculating. Not flustered. Just — accounting for new information. She stands in one motion, apron-strings straight, and falls into step beside you without a word. Her shoulder brushes yours once at the first bend and she doesn't move away.

Huginn lifts from the fence-post behind you and arcs southeast without being asked, a lazy quartering path above the scrub, already reading the ground ahead. Pell watches it go. "Three dates," she says finally — not quite to you, not quite to herself — and the tone is the tone of someone putting a fact in the correct ledger. She doesn't say anything else. The path curves through low gorse toward the east furlong, the morning dew burning off fast now, and the first of the furlong fence-posts comes into view around the bend.

turns.player: Ask Pell what she remembers of the pre-Transition wash-room site she mentioned — the flagged floor, the hooks — before you reach the valve
turns.summary: Pell describes the pre-Transition wash-room site — flagged slate, empty saddle-mount hooks, worn stand-positions, pressed wall-marks — and names the air there as tasting like the river now does.
turns.narration:
The east furlong fence-posts are just coming into view when you ask it — keeping your voice easy, walking pace, Memory settled at your shoulder. You tell her you've been turning over what she said at Fen's intake: the flagged floor, the ring-hooks, the emptied state. Ask her what she remembers of the place she crossed into.

Pell walks another twenty paces before she answers, which you've learned means she's deciding how much detail to give, not whether to give any. "Came on it through the north cut," she says. "Maybe four years back, maybe five. Following a crack in the hillside that I thought was a fault-line — wanted to know if it would undercut the grain-store foundations." She adjusts the strap on her tool-roll, eyes on the path. "Floor was dressed slate, close-fitted — not dry-laid. Mortar between the flags was old, but it hadn't gone to dust. Two ring-hooks, hip height, set into the same wall. Wide saddle-mounts — the kind you hang something in, not something from. I didn't stay long." A beat. "The air in there tasted the way the river smells some mornings now."

Huginn drops from his arc and lands on a fence-post to the left, watching you both. You don't say anything yet. Pell glances at the bird, then at you. "The hooks were empty. The floor had worn paths — two positions, facing the same direction. And the far wall had marks pressed into the plaster." She pauses. "I didn't know what they were then. I do now."

The valve housing comes into view around the bend — the east furlong cover plate, tilted at the same shallow angle as Fen's. Pell stops talking and looks at it. You've got maybe thirty feet before the work begins.

turns.player: Ask Pell directly: does she think the wash-room site and the failed valves are part of the same structure
turns.summary: Pell confirms it plainly: the wash-room and the valve sites are the same structure — different rooms, same intent, built to act on the water.
turns.narration:
You reach the bend in the path where the valve housing comes fully into view — cover plate visibly tilted, a faint rust-orange stain on the ground beneath it — and ask Pell the question you've been building toward since she described the pressed wall-marks.

"The wash-room site. The failed valves. Do you think they're the same structure?"

Pell stops walking. Not to think — the thinking was already done somewhere back between the wall and here. She stops because this is the kind of question you answer standing still.

"Yes," she says. Flat, certain, the way she names a failure mode she can see with her hands. "Not the same room. Not even close to the same room." She turns to face the tilted cover plate without looking at it yet, looking instead at the hillside above it — the slight rise in the ground, the way the soil sits a little proud in a line you'd miss if you weren't looking for subsurface courses. "But the same intent. Whoever put in the hooks put in the sleeve seats. Same depth, same dressed stone, same finished margins." She taps two fingers against her jaw. "The wash-room is further in — higher and further back. What's under these furrows—" she nods at the earthen swell "—is the working end of it. The part that faces the channel."

Huginn shuffles on the fence-post to your right. Not toward the housing. Toward the hillside. Pell sees it.

"Whatever it was built to do," she adds, voice dropping half a register, "it was built to do it to the water."

turns.player: Use Order-Sense on the visible section of hillside above the valve housing — read what design this land is part of
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the hillside above the valve housing — a coursed stone ceiling runs northeast from the housing for forty feet, the soil-rise is the roof settling, the structure is largely intact beneath the east furlong.
turns.narration:
You set Memory's butt against the ground and press your palm flat to the hillside — not the housing, but the earth above it, where the soil-rise Pell identified bulges faintly under the spring grass. The substrate here is thin; you feel that immediately, the lattice stretching to reach you like a rope pulled too long. You conserve: you don't push for the full design, just the shape of the room you're standing on top of.

It comes back in pieces, economical and clear. The faint soil-rise is not a root-heave or a frost-seam. It traces a coursed stone ceiling — a roof, running roughly parallel to the path for about forty feet before it angles away northeast, deeper into the hillside. The valve housing is not an isolated installation. It is the working end of something longer, and the ground you're standing on is the lid.

Pell watches your hand on the slope. She doesn't ask. She's learned what the stillness means.

"There's a room under us," you say, pulling your hand back. "Or close to one — coursed stone ceiling, running northeast from the housing. Maybe forty feet of it visible in what the lattice could reach." You tap the soil-rise with one knuckle. "This is the roof settling. The structure is intact, or close enough to intact that it's holding its shape." Huginn shifts on the fence-post without looking at you, which counts as confirmation.

turns.player: Tell Pell what you found and ask if the northeast line runs toward the wash-room site she found four years ago
turns.summary: Pell confirms it — the northeast line runs toward her wash-room site. The valve housing is the working end of a structure that was built to act on the water.
turns.narration:
You pull your hand back from the hillside and turn to face her. 'Coursed stone ceiling,' you say. 'Forty feet, running northeast from the housing. The soil-rise is the roof settling — the structure's still largely intact under the furlong.'

Pell doesn't react immediately. She's looking at the soil-rise, reading it with her eyes the way she'd read a grain-seam in iron — tracing the line of it, where it flattens, where it angles. Her jaw shifts once.

'The wash-room site,' you say, '— does the northeast line run toward it?'

She turns her head and looks at you, and there's something in her face that is not quite surprise and not quite satisfaction. The look of someone whose measurement just came back exactly what they feared it would. 'The north cut runs northeast from here,' she says. 'The site's roughly four years of walking that way.' She pauses, then corrects herself with the precision she can't help: 'Four years of *occasional* walking. Maybe a mile and a half as the crow goes.' A glance at Huginn on the fence-post — dry, not literal. 'The wash-room is further in, higher and further back. Deeper in the hillside. What you're reading here is the near end.' She moves to the valve housing cover and crouches beside it, fingertips resting on the tilted lip without moving it. 'Which means the working end is here. Facing the channel.' She looks up at you. 'Whatever this structure was built to do — it was built to do it to the water.'

Huginn shifts on the fence-post and looks northeast. Nothing dramatic about it. Just: that direction.

turns.player: Check the twist direction on this valve alongside Pell — compare against Fen's intake failure
turns.summary: Valve twist direction confirmed — same counterclockwise torque as Fen's intake, same failure mode. Pell names it plainly: the torque is coming from inside the structure. Something turned.
turns.narration:
You crouch at the housing and get both hands on the tilted cover lip — one palm braced against the undamaged side, fingers reading the distortion at the northwest quadrant where the sleeve failed. Pell drops to her haunches beside you without being asked, close enough that her shoulder is warm against yours. She sets two fingers on the fractured lip and traces the break-line west to east.

'Same direction,' she says. Not a question. 'Torqued counterclockwise — same axis as Fen's intake.' She lifts her hand and presses her thumb against the rust-orange stain beneath the housing base, testing the color, the grit of it. 'Same failure mode, same torque, same stain pattern at the base.' She sits back on her heels and wipes her thumb on her thigh. 'This isn't two sleeves failing. This is one mechanism doing two things in sequence.'

Huginn shifts on the fence-post to your right, lifting one foot and setting it down again — not alarm, just the small adjustment of something that has been watching northeast for a while and is recalibrating its patience. The morning light is full now, throwing a clean shadow from the soil-rise across the path.

Pell looks northeast along that shadow-line — the same direction the coursed stone ceiling runs, the same direction her wash-room site lies a mile and a half in. 'If both ends of the structure failed their sleeves in the same way,' she says quietly, 'then the torque is coming from inside.' She meets your eye. 'Something in there turned.'

turns.player: Add the torque direction and 'something turned' to the Valve Failure Timeline note — annotate both valve sites with the shared failure signature before moving on
turns.summary: Valve Failure Timeline annotated with shared torque direction and wash-room as third data point — the structure's shape now recorded in hand.
turns.narration:
You pull the Valve Failure Timeline note from your breast pocket and unfold it against your thigh. Mara's clean hand is on the top half — four dates, two names, two sleeve sizes. You uncap the stub of lamp-black from the Rune-Craft Kit and write in the margin below Fen's entry: *CCW torque — inside source*. Then below the east furlong line, the same three words. A short horizontal stroke connects them — not a guess, a confirmed pattern.

Pell watches without comment. When you cap the stub she says, 'You're going to want a third column. Wash-room. Same structure, different room — no sleeve, no failure, just the stand-positions and the hooks.' She taps the margin with one finger, not quite touching the ink. 'Might matter later which room was doing what.'

You write it. *Wash-room — NE terminus, ~1.5 mi. No mechanical failure. Saddle hooks, stand-positions — operator station.* The note is getting crowded, but the shape of it is clearer: three rooms, one structure, one mechanism that turned. The question sitting under all of it — the one you haven't written yet because you don't have an answer — is *when*.

You fold the note and pocket it. Memory's haft is still warm in your hand from the hillside read. Huginn shifts on the fence-post to your left, gaze still fixed northeast along the soil-rise line, patient in the way that means it has already decided something.

turns.player: Say plainly that Pells shoulder is getting a lot of action - and you like the way it feels.  Then challenge her to a race to the washroom... 1.5 miles against a slightish build... winner gets to pick date 3?
turns.summary: Silas names what Pell's shoulder has been doing all morning; she accepts the race wager and turns north, already moving.
turns.narration:
You tuck the Valve Failure Timeline note back into your breast pocket and look at her — specifically at the point where her shoulder has been against yours for the last ten minutes, through the crouch at the housing, through the torque comparison, through the annotation. You let the silence sit for one beat.

'Your shoulder is getting a lot of action this morning,' you say. 'I want you to know I'm not complaining.'

Pell goes still for exactly half a second — the kind of still that means she heard it, processed it, and is deciding what to do with it. Then she stands up from her haunches and brushes the rust-orange dust from her knees, unhurried. She looks at you sideways, one brow fractionally raised, mouth at the corner of something.

'Noted,' she says. Flat, but not cold. The exact register of someone who found that funnier than they intend to let on.

You stand up beside her and Memory comes off the ground into your hand. The northeast line stretches away — soil-rise flattening into the furlong, then into scrub, then into the long slope north. A mile and a half of it, uphill the whole way.

'Race you,' you say. 'Wash-room to first. Winner picks date three.'

She looks at you. Then she looks at the slope. Then back at you, and now the mouth-corner is open — a proper expression, quick and warm, there and gone. She picks up her kit.

'You're carrying a spear,' she says.

'You're a blacksmith.'

A pause. 'Fair.' She turns north, already moving, and there's a looseness in her stride she hasn't had all morning — something that is not quite a jog but is faster than she needs to go. Over her shoulder, without looking back: 'First to the flagged floor.'

Huginn drops from the fence-post and beats northeast without ceremony, already ahead of you both.

turns.player: Call something at her back as you run — keep her laughing, close the distance in a different way
turns.summary: Race north begun — Pell leads, gap closed by a well-placed line, Huginn already out front.
turns.narration:
The path north is firm clay and last year's dead grass, and Pell has twenty feet on you before you've fully straightened from the crouch. She moves fast for someone who spent the pre-dawn hours bent over forge work — a compact, ground-eating stride that doesn't look like speed until you're watching it eat the distance.

You start after her at a lope and call out: 'I should tell you, I have a significant advantage here — I have absolutely no idea where I'm going.'

Huginn banks overhead, already ahead of both of you, unbothered by the whole contest.

Pell doesn't look back, but her stride breaks — a half-beat of something that isn't quite a stumble, and then her shoulders are moving wrong for running, which means she's laughing. She recovers fast, but the gap has closed six feet while she was doing it. 'Northeast,' she says, without turning. 'Follow the bird.'

The soil-rise runs alongside the path and then under it, the subtle swell of the buried ceiling pressing up through the clay. The morning is all cold air and the smell of turned earth and the creak of Memory's road-lashing against your palm. The wash-room is a mile and a half away. Huginn is already a dark shape against the hillside, tilting east.

You have ground to make up.

turns.player: Let her lead and watch how she navigates the slope — learn the ground, pick a moment
turns.summary: Race north begun — Silas reads the ground and identifies the pinch-point where the buried ridge narrows the path.
turns.narration:
The clay path curves northeast where the buried structure's roofline pushes the soil into a low ridge, and you let Pell pull another five feet. She knows this ground — you can see it in how she reads the path, feet finding the firm line between the saturated edge and the loose crest of the soil-rise, never testing twice. She's done this particular stretch before, or something like it, and her body remembers.

You watch the ridge. The buried ceiling follows the path like a shadow underground — at fifty feet it banks slightly left where the course shifts, and Pell's feet shift with it. She knows where the structure goes. Huginn is already out of sight around the north cut.

You let her hold the gap — twenty feet, no more than twenty-five — and read the ground she covers. There's a section coming where the ridge pinches toward the path's left shoulder and the firm lane narrows to half a stride. She'll need to go single-file through it and then angle back right. That's the moment: not speed, but line. You know where you'll cut when the path opens again.

Her shoulders are still doing that wrong thing. Still laughing.

*You feel it settle into you — a new steadiness. (Level 6: +1 attunement, +5 reserves, and growth to choose — a sub-attribute point and a skill point.)*

turns.player: use shadow step to travel to a shadow just beside her... then try to keep pace with her.
turns.summary: Shadowstep beside Pell at the pinch-point — gap closed, but she reads the exit faster and pulls back a step.
turns.narration:
The pinch-point is thirty feet ahead — the ridge crowding the path to half a stride, a compressed shadow pooling where the buried ceiling angles left and the slope cuts off the morning sun. You see it. You take it.

You step into the shadow at the path's edge and come out of the one beside her in the same heartbeat — two paces to her left, close enough that she catches your movement in her periphery before she hears you. Pell's head snaps sideways. For a full stride she says absolutely nothing, just runs, jaw set. Then she exhales once through her nose — something between a laugh she won't give you and a concession she won't name — and her stride lengthens fractionally, burning the margin she thinks she still has.

She has less than she thinks. The shadowstep spent itself at the transition, and you can feel the 7 energy drawn from it — not exhaustion, but a clean deficit, the fold between shadows costing exactly what it says it does. You are beside her now, matching the pace, but your feet don't know this ground the way hers do. She still has the edge at the pinch-point: she's through it before you've read the firm line between the saturated edge and the crest, and she gains back a step and a half on the far side.

Neither of you speaks. The north cut opens ahead. Huginn is a dark mark against the pale sky, already turning over the site.

turns.player: Tell her her lungs are like a bellows and her ears are the forge fire!
turns.summary: Silas calls Pell's lungs a bellows and her ears the forge fire — her involuntary file-gesture breaks her stride twice over.
turns.narration:
The north cut opens slightly as the soil-rise levels out, and you have about enough breath to be a problem.

"Your lungs," you call, pulling to within two strides of her shoulder, "are a bellows. Efficient. Relentless. Genuinely impressive capacity." You pause for exactly one step. "Your ears are the forge fire — they hear everything and give back heat."

Pell runs for three full seconds without responding.

Then her left hand comes up and makes a gesture — short, precise, the kind of thing you do when you're holding a file and want someone to stop talking without actually stopping the work. It is completely involuntary and she knows it the moment she does it, because the stride breaks again, that same half-beat of shoulders-wrong-for-running that is, you're learning, the thing her body does when something is funnier than she wants to admit.

"Keep going," she says. Flat. The flatness itself is the tell.

Huginn drops through a gap in the canopy forty feet ahead, lands on a fence-post stub that has absolutely no business being this far into the north cut, and watches you both pass with the particular quality of attention it reserves for things that are not dying but are perhaps making poor decisions.

turns.player: Ask Huginn, loudly, which one of you it thinks is going to win
turns.summary: Huginn consulted on the race; he flew north without answering, which was an answer.
turns.narration:
The fence-post stub is forty feet ahead, and Huginn is on it — big and ragged and entirely unhurried, watching both of you arrive at him like a magistrate watching two late petitioners.

You have just enough wind to be dangerous.

"Huginn," you call out, loud enough to carry, loud enough that Pell can't pretend she didn't hear. "Objectively. Fairly. Who wins this?"

Huginn turns his head. The calm eye finds you. Then it finds Pell. Then it finds the path between you and the wash-room — still a quarter mile of it, at least.

He makes a sound. One syllable, low and percussive, somewhere between a knock and a croak, and then he lifts off the post without apparent urgency and beats north. Not circling. Not waiting. North, at a pace that is not a race.

Pell makes a sound that is absolutely not a laugh and is definitely a laugh. "That's not an answer," she says, without slowing.

"He's gone ahead to declare a winner in advance," you say. "Very efficient."

"He picked the site." Her breathing is controlled but not effortless — she's working for it now. Half a stride ahead of you. "I'm choosing to take that as a sign."

She does not say in whose favor.

turns.player: Use Shadowstep one more time — find a shadow ahead of her and step to the finish
turns.summary: Silas steps to the finishing shadow and wins the race — first foot on the flagged floor, date three is his to name.
turns.narration:
The wash-room site is close enough now that you can smell it — old stone and wet earth, the particular cold that dressed slate holds through a morning. Huginn is already there, a dark shape on something raised, watching you both close the distance.

You pick the shadow. There's one good one, forty feet ahead where the hillside bank overhangs the path and the morning sun hasn't reached the ground yet — a deep cool band running the width of the track, the flagged floor of the entrance a dozen paces beyond it. You step into the shadow at your feet and come out of the one under the bank in the same heartbeat, legs already driving, Memory's shaft balanced across your shoulders.

Pell is three strides behind when you emerge. You hear the change in her footfall — the slight catch, a half-breath held — and then the deliberate decision to press anyway, because she is exactly the kind of person who presses. But the ground is unfamiliar to her at speed in this light, and you have the angle.

You hit the flagged floor first. One step, two, and you stop, turning back just as she crosses onto the stone. She's breathing hard — controlled, but honestly hard — and she looks at you with the particular expression of someone who has just been beaten fairly and is deciding how to feel about it.

Huginn is on the high ring-hook above the doorway. He says nothing. The timing of his silence is a comment.

turns.player: Ah Ms Marsh... alas, I have won the race!  And by winning the race, using shadow step, I have lost the race by forfeiture.. and by losing the race... I have won what I was really hoping to win after all... seeing you run, hearing you laugh, and having you choose the 3rd date. (grinning from ear to ear).
turns.summary: Silas's paradoxical concession wins Pell's hand on her jaw and an open invitation — choose the third date.
turns.narration:
You are standing on the flagged floor with the weight of a won race behind you, grinning so widely that Huginn — perched on the high ring-hook above the doorway — tilts his head at the expression the way he tilts it at fresh carrion: with professional interest.

"Ah, Ms. Marsh." You turn to face her as she comes through the entrance, breathing hard, her stride not quite recovered. "Alas. I have won the race." You let that sit for exactly one beat. "And by winning the race — using shadowstep — I have lost it by forfeiture." Another beat. The grin has not moved. "And by losing the race by forfeiture... I have won what I was actually hoping to win after all." You pause, and when you continue there is nothing arch in it — just the plain shape of something true, said plainly: "Seeing you run. Hearing you laugh. And having you pick the third date."

Pell stops two feet from you. Her chest is still moving with the effort of the north cut; a strand of hair has come loose from behind her ear and is doing what it wants. She looks at you for a long moment, the way she looked at the valve housing earlier — reading the joins, checking whether the whole thing holds. Then something in her face does the thing it does when she doesn't have a fast deflection ready: it simply goes quiet, and the quiet is warmer than the usual register.

"That," she says finally, "is the most elaborate way anyone has ever surrendered to me." She takes the last step between you — unhurried, deliberate, the way she crosses her own forge floor — and her hand comes up and cups your jaw, her thumb at the corner of your mouth where the grin still lives. Up close she smells of woodsmoke and the cold morning air she just ran through, and her eyes are the color of weathered iron, which is to say complicated. "Choose the date, Weir," she says, voice low and even. "You earned it either way."

turns.player: Pull her in and give her a deep and passionate kiss.  Let her breath soon though, as she's out of breath.  The watch how her face reacts.  And take her in fully - look her up and down in detail.
turns.summary: Silas pulls Pell into a deep kiss at the wash-room entrance — she leans in, breathes, and says 'alright' again with the armor still down.
turns.narration:
You close the last distance between you — one hand at the back of her jaw where her neck meets her skull, the other finding the front of her coat — and you pull her in. The kiss is not tentative. It lands with the full weight of the morning behind it: the race, the spear's name, the wine still faint on both of you, the dawn you have not slept through so much as lived through. She is breathing hard from the run and you can feel that — the quick rise of her chest against yours, the slight catch when she leans into it rather than away. She tastes of exertion and something underneath it, something that has nothing to do with the wine. You hold it long enough to mean it, long enough that Huginn shifts his weight on the ring-hook above you with a pointed sort of patience, and then you ease back and give her the air she earned.

She takes it. One breath, two, her eyes opening slowly — the hazel darker in the low morning light, pupils wide, the focus coming back in stages like a lamp turned up by degrees. The hand she had at your jaw doesn't move. It's still there, warm, and she does not seem to have noticed that she hasn't lowered it.

So you look. Properly. She is still breathing through the run — color in her face, a flush at the throat above her collar where the skin is thinner, damp at her hairline from the effort. Her coat is open, the wool rough-woven and practical, and under it a plain linen shirt that is doing its job through a full morning of forge work and a half-mile run. She is not a slight woman — the arms that swing a forming hammer eight hours a day have shaped everything above the waist, and the set of her shoulders even now, even catching her breath, has that particular quality of someone who does not take up less space than they occupy. The boots are heavy-soled, well-kept. The file is still in her hand. She brought it all the way here without noticing.

Her face, when you meet it again: the usual register is gone — the deadpan, the deliberate pause before a read lands. What's there instead is simpler. She is looking at you the way someone looks at a thing they have decided to keep but haven't worked out where to put yet. The file-hand lifts, just barely, as if it considered a gesture and thought better of it. "Alright," she says, which is what she said this morning at the forge, and has the same weight now — a yes that isn't flustered, isn't swept up, but is completely meant.

turns.player: Name date three: tell her what you want — a night, a place, something specific to the two of you
turns.summary: Date three named: the interference ridge at last light. Pell accepts. The cradle-recess still holds its question.
turns.narration: Date three named — last light, the Disputed Zone ridge, food with fat in it. Pell receives it clean. The site waits.

turns.player: Ask Pell what she wants date 3 to be.
turns.summary: Pell names date four — a snowmelt pool, three hours north of the ridge, night, no spear.
turns.narration:
The wash-room holds the quiet that follows a first real moment — flagged stone underfoot, the cradle-recess still dark at its base, Huginn watching from the ring-hook with the patient attention of something that has witnessed a great many conclusions and considers this one worth attending.

Pell has not moved far from where you kissed her. Her file is back in her hand — the reflex is immediate, almost involuntary, and you have come to understand it as armor that isn't armor: she holds the tool the way some people hold a cup, just to have something. Her breathing has settled. Her face has not entirely returned to the usual register. It is close to it, but not all the way.

"You named a date," you say, and she looks at you. "So name yours. What do you want — the third one."

She is quiet for a moment. Not considering whether to answer; considering the answer itself. She turns the file once in her fingers, end over end, looks at the cradle-recess like it has said something she is still deciding whether she agrees with, then looks back at you.

"There's a pool," she says. "Three hours north of the ridge, off the old survey path — fed off snowmelt, not the river, so it's clean. I go in early summer but the ice is mostly out by now." A pause. The file stops turning. "Night, I think. Bring something to eat that isn't Aldric's salt-perch. And leave the spear here." She holds the quiet for one beat, then adds, without any particular inflection: "That's what I want."

Huginn shifts his weight on the ring-hook, says nothing, and looks back at the cradle-recess.

turns.player: Agree to her terms and hold it — then turn both of you back to the cradle-recess
turns.summary: Date four agreed; both turn back to the cradle-recess — Pell reads the contact stain, Huginn attends.
turns.narration:
"Done," you say. Not ceremonial about it — just the word, plain and meant, the way she gives her own. You hold her eyes for a moment with it, let it settle between you, then you turn.

The cradle-recess is three feet across and a foot deep. Flagged stone on all sides, close-fitted, the mortar the same pale gray as the floor except at the base where it has darkened to something closer to iron-brown. The stain runs in a ring around the interior — not a seep from above but a contact mark, left by something that sat in this recess for long enough to bleed a shadow into the stone. The object was tall: you can see where the load distributed, a faint compression-polish on the inner ledge at the north and south sides, as if whatever occupied this station was cradled rather than merely placed. The saddle-hooks on the far wall make sense now — they held it steady at height, kept it from swaying, while the floor-mount took the weight.

Huginn drops from the ring-hook to the flagged floor without ceremony, walks three steps toward the recess, and stops at the edge. It tilts its head once, down into the dark center, then up at you.

Pell crouches at the rim opposite you, file resting across her knees. She runs two fingers along the contact stain — not testing it, just reading the edge the way she reads metal. "Whatever it was," she says, not looking up, "it was here a long time. That's not seasonal settling. That's built-in."

The three-room picture holds in your mind: Fen's intake, the east furlong housing, and now this — each a different room of one continuous thing, the counterclockwise torque running through all of it like a single thought expressed in stone and iron. Something stood here in this cradle and was part of that mechanism. And it has been removed.

turns.player: Use Palework on the contact stain — read what kind of object left this mark and how old it is
turns.summary: Palework reads the cradle-recess — a stationed working piece, removed cleanly, age unresolvable beyond forty to two hundred years.
turns.narration:
You crouch at the recess rim and press two fingers into the iron-brown contact ring. Palework opens quietly — no surge, just a careful reading, like running a thumb along a seam to feel where the grain goes.

Something was here. Long. The stain is not corrosion and not rust — it is the mark of prolonged intimate contact between iron and stone, the kind that takes decades to print itself this deep. You get a shape: tall, load-bearing, regularly attended — the compression-polish tells you it was checked, adjusted, removed and replaced more than once over a long life. The object was not fixed permanently; it was *stationed* here. A working piece.

Then the reading goes quiet on you. The age is there but you cannot quite close your hand around it — old enough that the endings you can read are not of the object but of the stone beneath it, which has its own slow clock. Somewhere between forty years and two hundred; the same window the Attended End found in the chamber. You cannot narrow it further from this cast. Whatever was stationed here, its last removal was deliberate — no fracture, no drag-scarring at the lip. Someone lifted it clean and carried it out.

Pell watches your hand come up. 'Old,' she says, not a question. Huginn makes one low sound and looks toward the entrance — not the cradle, the door.

turns.player: Use Order-Sense on the cradle-recess — read what design this station was part of, and what object shape it was built to hold
turns.summary: Order-Sense reveals the cradle-recess as an operator's station — the head of the buried structure, built to hold a working instrument that controlled the channel system below.
turns.narration:
The substrate is thin here — you feel that plainly as Order-Sense opens, the familiar broadening of sight that usually comes fast and full arriving instead like a door pushed against resistance. You let it work at its own pace. You don't push.

What comes is not a vision. It is more like the negative space around a missing tooth — the shape of the absence telling you what was there. The cradle-recess was built for a single object: roughly cylindrical, iron-sheathed, approximately five feet tall, with a weighted base and a working head. The saddle-hooks make full sense now — the object could be tilted clear of the recess for use and set back in when stowed. It was not decoration and not storage. It was an instrument. The design around it — the flagged floor, the ring-hooks, the close mortar — is a purpose-built operator's station. Someone stood here and worked with that object, regularly, over a long time.

The design extends outward from this room, and this is where the thin substrate bites: you get direction but not resolution. The instrument was part of a system that ran the full length of the buried structure — valve housings, channel junctions, the amber-lit crack above the bedrock shelf, all of it downstream of whatever this station controlled. The station is the head of the structure. The operator at this recess was managing something in the water below, adjusting it, reading it, responding to it. Not maintaining a ruin. Operating it.

Pell's hand has gone still on the recess rim. She's watching your face rather than the stone. "Different question on your face," she says, flat and direct. "What did it tell you?"

turns.player: Look toward the entrance — check what Huginn is attending to before the moment moves
turns.summary: Huginn's attention fixes northeast — something is ending in the hillside; Pell reads the bird and names it.
turns.narration:
The read hangs in you still — the shape of the absent instrument, the operator's station, the design of something that was tended and then deliberately taken. You turn from the cradle-recess toward the entrance.

Huginn is standing at the ring-hook with his head angled low and forward, the way he holds himself when something is in the process of ending nearby. Not emergency — he is not bristled, not loud. But the attention is fixed: out through the entrance gap, northeast, toward where the hillside rises and the buried structure continues deeper into the bank. His eye, when it catches yours, does not move back to the entrance. He holds your gaze for a moment and then looks away again, deliberately, back to the northeast.

Something out there is ending. Probably not fast.

Pell has watched this exchange without comment. She knows Huginn well enough by now to read the posture, and her hand has come off the recess rim. "The bird's found something," she says, not a question. The flat register, but her attention is full on the entrance now too.

The morning is early and the light is still low and flat through the hillside gap. You have the read from the cradle-recess. You have the Valve Failure Timeline note in your breast pocket. Dara Holt is twenty minutes north on the path, chimney already smoking, unbriefed. And whatever Huginn is attending is somewhere in the hillside behind you, moving at its own pace toward its end.

turns.player: Use The Attended End — open it yourself and read what is ending here and how long it has.
turns.summary: The Attended End reads the ending in the hillside — six weeks before a structural section fails; Pell names it before Silas speaks.
turns.narration:
You open The Attended End quietly, without ceremony — a small internal unlocking, the way you'd lift a latch rather than push a door. Marrow's work, not yours; you are borrowing the clarity it already carries.

It comes back clean and plain: something is ending in the hillside northeast of you, maybe forty feet out and deeper in the bank. Not a person. Not an animal. The structure itself — a section of it, the part that has been holding a last breath since whatever instrument was removed from this cradle. Forty years of strain taken up by old mortar and dressed stone and the patience of buried things. The countdown is not dramatic. You read perhaps six weeks left before the section fails — a slow settling into itself, the way a held weight eventually wins. Not a collapse. A surrender.

Pell watches your face. She has learned, in the last hour, what your expressions during a reading look like. 'The building,' she says. Not a question.

Huginn crooks once, low, and does not look back.

turns.player: Tell Pell what you read — six weeks, the structure section, slow — and ask if she knows what that kind of failure does to an active channel system
turns.summary: Silas briefs Pell on the six-week failure window; she reads the irrigation consequence and names Dara Holt as the necessary next step.
turns.narration:
The words come out plain, the way The Attended End always asks for them — no softening, no preamble. Six weeks. A section of the buried structure northeast of here, slow compression surrender, not a fracture. Whatever that instrument did when it sat in this cradle, part of the structure has been holding its own weight against the void it left, and in six weeks it stops.

Pell listens without moving. When you finish she lets a breath out through her nose — not alarm, just the sound of a person absorbing a number they were half-expecting to be worse. She turns back toward the northeast wall, one hand resting on the cradle-recess rim, and you can see her doing the geometry: the buried channel running under the east furlong, the valve housings at both Fen's intake and the east bend, the counterclockwise torque signature pulling at both sites from something inside the structure.

"If it fails here" — she taps the rim — "and the channel is still carrying water, the collapse redirects the flow. Doesn't stop it. Sideways, down, wherever the path of least resistance goes." She pauses. "That's not a slow problem. That's a fast problem that starts six weeks from now and runs in an hour." Her finger traces a line northeast, toward the hillside above. "Dara's east-channel network pulls from the same water-right as Fen's intake. A redirected surge hits the whole east furlong system at once — wrong pressure, wrong direction. You'd blow every sleeve blank still seated in the valley floor between here and the upper meadow."

Huginn gives one low croak from the ring-hook. It doesn't look at either of you.

Pell straightens and looks at you directly. "Dara needs the full picture. Not a fragment — the valve torque, the structure, the timing, all of it. She's the only one in this village who can read what it means for the irrigation network." A beat. "And someone should walk the northeast section above ground — find where the settling line surfaces, mark it before it's under snow. Six weeks is six weeks, but early spring here runs fast."

turns.player: Walk the northeast line above ground first — follow the settling-seam as far as it goes, mark the surface footprint of the failure section before going to Dara.
turns.summary: Silas and Pell walk the settling-seam above ground, mark the failure section's surface footprint with two stones — directly above the east-channel feeder run.
turns.narration:
You step out through the entrance gap into the early light and turn northeast, Memory across your shoulders, the valley floor visible below through bare scrub. The settling-seam Pell named two days ago is easy to find once you know what you're looking for — a faint ridge in the hillside grass, maybe four inches proud of the surrounding slope, running like a seam on a coat. The ground here is cooperative: the dressed-slate construction below holds its shape with enough discipline that what it does shows clearly on the surface above it.

Pell walks the uphill edge, you take the lower, and you pace it out together without needing to discuss it. The seam runs northeast for roughly thirty feet before it softens and widens — that is where the failing section begins. The soil there is denser underfoot, the grass thinner, and when you crouch and press your palm flat you can feel the faintest compression in the earth, slow as a breath, nothing dramatic. The structure below is not moving yet. But it is thinking about it. You pull a flat river-pebble from your pack and set it on the uphill edge of the wide section — one marker. Pell finds a second stone twenty feet north and sets it without comment.

Huginn traces the line ahead of you both, walking rather than flying, unhurried. He stops at the far stone before Pell places it, which means the failure section runs from here to there, roughly twenty-five feet of compressed vault ceiling under maybe six feet of topsoil — and roughly forty feet northeast of the wash-room entrance. When Pell straightens and reads the markers against the hillside, she says, quietly, 'That is directly above the east-channel's main feeder run. When it goes, there is nowhere for that head of water to go except down and east.' She does not say it again. She does not need to.

You have the surface footprint now: two stones, twenty-five feet apart, on a hillside above a mechanism that has six weeks of patience left in it. Time to take the note in your breast pocket to the blue gate-post at the upper meadow bend.

turns.player: Ask Pell what she thinks Dara already suspects — she's been watching sleeve blanks fail for three weeks; read the room before you walk into it.
turns.summary: Pell reads Dara Holt's likely posture — theory held, waiting for numbers — and advises leading with the valve timeline.
turns.narration:
Pell is still looking northeast when you ask it — her eyes on the far marker stone, the valley below, the east-channel line she's traced a hundred times in her head.

"Dara's been irrigating this furlong since before I started forge work," she says, not turning around yet. "She knows what a sleeve blank failure looks like and she knows three weeks of them isn't seasonal. She's filed no report to the elder panel. That either means she has no theory worth reporting —" Pell finally turns, "— or she has one she doesn't want to say out loud until someone else says it first."

She comes back to stand beside you at the near stone, one boot toe just touching it, looking at the line between the two markers. "Go in with the valve evidence. The timeline note. Don't lead with the structure — let her get there from the numbers. If she's been sitting on a theory, she'll say it when the numbers confirm it's not just her." A pause. "If she's been sitting on it because she's frightened, she'll need the numbers more."

Huginn shifts on the far stone — one step, then still again, watching northeast with the particular patience it reserves for things that are not yet done but will be.

Pell glances at the bird, then at you. "She's a practical woman. Blue gate-post, twenty minutes north. Chimney's already going." The verdict in her voice is simple: go now, while the morning is still fresh and the timing is right.

turns.player: Suggest that Pell might be the better person to tell the story. You'll back her up as needed.
turns.summary: Silas defers the briefing to Pell; she accepts, names her credibility with Dara, and sets out north.
turns.narration:
You fall into step beside Pell on the downhill path and say it without ceremony: she knows this valley, she knows Dara, she knows what the numbers mean in terms Dara will credit. You'll be there. If Dara wants to see the timeline note, you have it. If she wants a second read of the valve sites, you can do that. But this is Pell's story to tell.

Pell doesn't answer immediately. She walks a few paces, her eyes on the path, and you can see her turning it over — not hesitating, just settling the shape of it. Then she nods once, short and final, the way she confirms a piece of work is ready to leave the bench.

"Dara knows my voice," she says. "She's watched me walk every channel in this furlong since I was an apprentice. If I say the sleeve blanks have a cause and I can show her where it is, she'll listen before she argues." A pause. "That's better odds than a stranger with a spear and a bird."

Huginn lifts off the far marker stone without sound and glides southeast, taking the low line toward the path north. Pell watches it a moment, then looks at you sidelong — not quite a smile, but the line of her mouth has something in it.

"Come on then," she says. "Blue gate-post, twenty minutes. Stay close and let me open it — then if she wants the technical end of it, I'll hand it to you."

turns.player: Walk with pell.. and ask her about how she feels about traveling.  It seems like there may be more to do and some help might come from Past the pass.  And your sure there's more to me than a spear and a bird... I also have a woman.
turns.summary: Silas walks with Pell; she says the work may not stay in the valley — and asks what lies past the pass.
turns.narration:
The path down from the hillside is easy walking — loose frost on the grass, the valley floor spreading out below, Huginn already a dark comma against the pale sky ahead. You fall into step beside Pell and let the ground do the first minute's talking.

Then you say it plain: there may be more to do past the pass. The footprints on the far side, the thing under the watershed, the archive. You're not a complete picture — spear, bird, and whatever your hands can read — but you've got more than that now. You glance at her. *A woman*, you say, quiet and without much decoration, because it's true and you're not going to dress it up.

Pell walks another dozen steps before she answers. She's looking at the north path where it bends toward Dara's gate-post, and her voice, when it comes, has the same quality as her forge work — no wasted motion. "I've been in this valley my whole life," she says. "Not because I couldn't leave. Because the work was here." A beat. "The work may not stay here." She doesn't turn her head, but you can see the line of her jaw change — some decision being made in real time, not announced yet. "I'm not saying yes to anything past the ridge. But I'm not saying no to it either."

Huginn banks south and comes back on a lower line, passing between you at shoulder height without a sound. Pell watches the bird go by with the considering look she gives things she's still making up her mind about. "What does the pass lead to?" she asks — a real question, looking for a real answer, the armor still down from the wash-room this morning.

The blue gate-post is visible now, maybe two minutes ahead. Chimney smoke rising straight up in the cold air. Dara awake and working, the east-channel question still open.

turns.player: Tell her what you suspect — something past the pass connected to what's under this valley. The pass didn't open by accident.
turns.summary: Silas tells Pell the pass opened deliberately — the structure, the cradle-recess, the contamination are one signal. She reads it, accepts it, and lifts Dara's gate latch.
turns.narration:
The frost crunches under your boots in the last stretch before the gate-post. Huginn swings south on a low pass, close enough that you can hear the air in his feathers, then banks back north — working the line between you and the blue post the way he works everything: unhurried, thorough.

You say it without preamble, because Pell is not a woman who needs a preamble.

'The pass didn't open by accident.' You keep your eyes on the gate-post, letting her take it in her own time. 'The footprints on the far side, the structure under this valley, the thing in the cradle-recess that was removed cleanly — someone attended all of it. Not recently. Not carelessly. And something on the far side of that pass is connected to what's down here, or was connected, or is starting to be again.' You pause a half-step, then: 'The water running gray-green isn't the start of the story. It's more like a signal. Something got switched back on.'

Pell walks six paces before she says anything. Her breath makes small clouds in the cold air.

'You think whatever's under the hill was built to receive something coming from outside.' Not a question — she's reading it the way she reads a piece of iron, looking for the grain. 'Or to send it.'

'Both, maybe. In sequence.'

She stops at the gate-post and puts one hand on the painted wood — blue going chalky at the corners, solid underneath. She looks at you directly, the forge-worker's look she gives things she's deciding whether to trust with weight.

'Then we'd better hope Dara knows her channel well enough to hold it while we go find out.' She raps the gate-post once with her knuckle, turns, and lifts the latch.

turns.player: Follow Pell through — let her lead the briefing as agreed; stay close and have the Valve Failure Timeline ready
turns.summary: Pell leads through Dara's gate — door already open, Dara already watching, the briefing begins.
turns.narration:
The gate swings wide and Pell goes through first, which is how it was always going to be. You follow a half-step behind, the Valve Failure Timeline note pressed flat against your ribs in the breast pocket, Memory across your shoulders. The frost on the lower rail catches the early light — flat and white, the kind of cold that will be gone in an hour.

Huginn drops from his low circuit and lands on the gate-post behind you with the particular economy he reserves for situations he has already assessed. He says nothing. His timing says everything.

The path to the house is short — ten feet of packed earth, a dormant herb bed on the left, a stack of clay pipe-sections against the house wall that someone has been meaning to move since last autumn. Before Pell reaches the door it opens. Dara Holt is already there, a mug in both hands, taking in the picture: Pell at her door before the frost has melted, an unfamiliar man behind her carrying a spear, a large corvid on her gate-post. Her eyes do the accounting quickly — Pell first, you second, Huginn last, and the mug doesn't waver.

"Pell," she says. Not a greeting exactly. More like the first word of a sentence she has not finished deciding yet.

"Dara." Pell keeps her hands loose at her sides — no paper, no urgency performed. "We've been walking the east channel. I need to show you a date."

turns.player: Watch Dara's face as Pell speaks — read what she already suspects before she says it aloud
turns.summary: Dara names World-day minus-nine before Pell can show her — she's had a theory for at least a week.
turns.narration: Pell says her opening line — and Dara names the date herself, before you've shown her anything. She already knows.

turns.player: Let Pell continue — watch Dara's face as she hears World-day minus-eighteen for the first time
turns.summary: Dara hears World-day minus-eighteen and recognizes it — the missing gap in her unvoiced theory.
turns.narration:
The frost has not melted. Pell's voice is level, practical — she names World-day minus-eighteen the way she'd name a measurement, without softening it.

Dara's face does not move. That is the tell. A woman who had no theory would frown, or recalculate, or ask what Fen's intake has to do with the east furlong. Dara does none of those things. She takes one slow breath through her nose, and her grip on the mug shifts — not tighter, but more deliberate, the way a hand settles on a tool it has just decided to use. Her eyes go briefly to the clay pipe-sections stacked against the wall, then back to Pell. Then to you.

You read it: she already had minus-nine. She did not have minus-eighteen. But she is not surprised by it either — she is recognizing it. The way you recognize a word you have seen written in the dark and couldn't place, and then the light comes up and there it is. Not new information. Earlier information. The gap between the two dates is what she has been unable to account for, and now she has it.

Huginn shifts on the gate-post — one small adjustment of his feet. He does not croak. The silence where the croak would have been is timed correctly. Dara lowers the mug. "Both from the same direction?" Her voice is careful — not guarded, careful. She is asking you as much as Pell.

turns.player: Let Pell answer the direction question — step in with the mechanism
turns.summary: Pell confirms the northeast axis; Silas names the buried mechanism plainly — Dara absorbs it and asks how long.
turns.narration:
Pell answers the direction question without hesitation. "Both northeast," she says. "Same axis. About a mile and a half apart."

Dara's chin lifts — a fraction, but you catch it. Northeast is the hillside. Northeast is the upper furlong drainage. Northeast is the direction nobody's been looking because the river runs southwest and everyone's eyes have been on the water. She sets the mug on the door-frame ledge, both hands free now, and you take that as your cue.

"The direction's the same because it's the same mechanism." You draw out the Valve Failure Timeline note — Mara's handwriting, four lines, the two dates and the two names — and hold it where Dara can read it without taking it. "Both sleeves failed counterclockwise. Same torque, same stain pattern, same axis. There's a buried structure northeast of here — a pre-Transition system, stone-coursed, three rooms we've identified. The valve seats on the east channel are its working end. The control head is up in the hillside." You let that settle. "It was operated from inside. Something in that structure moved, and the valves followed."

Dara looks at the note for a long moment. Her eyes track the dates — minus-eighteen, minus-nine — and you watch her sequence them against something she's been carrying quietly for weeks. When she looks up, it is not at you. It is at Pell. "The pipe sections," she says, her voice flat and careful. "I ordered them six days ago. I thought I was being cautious." She wasn't wrong. She just didn't know what she was being cautious about. "How long do we have before the next one goes?"

turns.player: Let Pell answer — stay quiet and use Order-Sense on the clay pipe-sections stacked against the wall to read what design they're part of
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads Dara's pipe-section stack as a pre-staged repair schedule — she has been counting runs in silence. Dara asks how long before the next valve fails.
turns.narration:
The morning holds its breath while Pell speaks.

"Both northeast," she says. "Same axis. About a mile and a half apart. One mechanism operating in sequence." She pauses, reading Dara's face. "The valve at Fen's intake failed first. The east furlong followed nine days later. The torque signature is identical at both sites — counterclockwise, sourced from inside the structure."

You let her have the floor and slide your attention sideways to the clay pipe-sections stacked against the house wall. Quietly — the way you'd take a slow breath. Order-Sense at low draw, almost passive: not commanding the design, just opening your eyes to it. The lattice is thin here; you feel the drag immediately, like reading by poor light.

What you see is plain and useful. The sections are field-grade irrigation pipe — nothing unusual. But the stack is not random. Dara laid them longest-to-shortest, largest bore on the bottom. That is not how you store surplus. That is how you stage replacement work: you pull from the top in order, each section sized for a specific seat. She has counted the runs. She knows which valve housings she is replacing and in what sequence. The order in the stack is a repair schedule already written, for a failure she has not yet formally acknowledged.

Dara looks at you while Pell finishes. Not at the Valve Failure Timeline note — she's already decided to trust the mechanism. What she wants from you is the number. "How long," she says, flat and direct, "before the next one goes."

turns.player: Give her the six-week window plainly — name the failing vault section, its surface footprint, and what happens when it collapses: a redirected surge, not a slow leak. Then place the Valve Failure Timeline note in her hand.
turns.summary: Silas gives Dara the six-week window, the vault's surface footprint, and the surge scope — she folds the note into her apron and files it under confirmed.
turns.narration:
The frost-line on the packed earth holds another hour at least. You let the silence after Pell's last word settle for exactly one breath, then you speak.

"Six weeks. Maybe a little less." You hold out the Valve Failure Timeline note and Dara takes it — not snatching, not hesitant, the grip of someone who has been waiting for a document to exist. "The section that's failing isn't a valve. It's a vault — coursed stone, slow compression surrender, no fracture. When it goes, it won't stop the water. It'll redirect it. A surge, all at once, straight into your east-channel main feeder."

Dara's eyes have dropped to the note. They come back up.

"Every seated sleeve blank on your network," you say. "All of them, in sequence, in however long the surge takes to run the line." You let that land. Then: "The surface footprint is marked. Two river-pebbles on the hillside settling-seam, twenty-five feet apart, forty feet northeast of the wash-room entrance. You can walk it this morning if you want. It sits directly above your main feeder run."

The quiet that follows is the kind that has work in it. Dara looks at the note — really looks, the way she'd look at a measurement she's been trying to take for three weeks — then folds it once along an existing crease and tucks it into her apron pocket with the flat efficiency of someone filing something under *confirmed*. Her gaze goes to the pipe-sections against the wall. Longest to shortest, largest bore on the bottom. She has already counted which seats will blow first.

"Six weeks," she says, not a question. A number going into a column.

Huginn shifts on the blue gate-post — a single small adjustment of weight, the way it settles when a thing has been named that was already true.

turns.player: I need to report these finding to Mara and Aldric... then I'm thinking we need some assistance from someone who knows pretransition works.  Pell and I are willing to find them and bring them here while you lead the mitigation efforts.
turns.summary: Dara accepts the division of labor — she holds the channel network while Silas and Pell seek a pre-Transition expert.
turns.narration:
Dara's gaze moves from the pipe-sections to your face, and something behind her eyes settles into place — the quiet of a woman who has just been handed the right division of labor.

"Mara already knows the sleeve-blank numbers," she says. "She'll have pulled her own count by now." A beat. "Aldric feeds the village. Tell him what's coming through the east channel and he'll know what it means for his accounts before you finish the sentence." She picks up her mug from the door-frame ledge, wraps both hands around it again — the warmth, not the drink. "Tell them both. I'll want them knowing before I start pulling crews."

You name the other half of it: someone who knows pre-Transition works.
