Not an irrigator, not a blacksmith — someone who has read these structures before, who knows what a cradle-recess is for and what might have been taken from it. Pell straightens slightly beside you, and you feel her read the same shape in it that you do: the wash-room, the missing instrument, the deliberate removal. Dara will hold the channel network together from this end. The two of you will go find whoever can explain what's been taken and why.

Dara nods once — the nod of a woman calculating which channel crew to pull first. "If they know the design, I want them here before the fifth week." She glances at the pebble-marked hillside above her pipe-sections, just briefly, as if checking that the seam is still where you said it was. "I'll start with the east-bend junctions while you're moving. If the surge comes early, I'll have the bore-sequence staged." She tucks the mug against her side and looks at you directly. "Who are you going to find?"

Huginn shifts on the blue gate-post, once, unhurried. The chimney smoke above the house rises straight into the still cold air.

turns.player: I don't know who we're going to find, but we'll follow leads - even if, as I suspect, they take us beyond the valley...
turns.summary: Dara accepts the search beyond the valley — sets a hard deadline before week five, and sends Silas to brief Mara and Aldric before dawn is full.
turns.narration:
Dara nods once — the nod of someone filing a statement under *acceptable*.

"Beyond the valley." She says it flat, not a question, not an objection. Her eyes move to Pell for half a second, then back to you. "Then you'd better be gone before the fifth week. Not by the fifth week. *Before* it." She lifts her mug, drinks, sets it back down. "I'll have the east-bend junctions shored and the crew briefed by week two. Whatever you bring back, I can work with — provided there's still a channel to work on."

Pell shifts her weight on the path beside you, just slightly. She's reading the same shape you are: the expert could be anyone. Someone who came through the pass in the old days and stayed. Someone in the Heights who worked pre-Transition systems before the Transition made them ceremonial. Someone in the Plateau's old records. Someone on the far side of those boot prints in the new pass. None of those are clean answers, and all of them are a direction.

Huginn drops from the blue gate-post to the path without ceremony and walks three steps northeast — the direction of the wash-room site, of the buried structure, of whatever is out there — then stops and looks back at you. Not an omen. Just extremely unhurried orientation.

Dara watches the bird. "Brief Mara and Aldric this morning, before the village is fully up. Mara will have questions; Aldric will want numbers. Give them both." She pauses. "Then go find your expert."

turns.player: Ask Pell what she thinks — where does someone who knows pre-Transition works actually live?
turns.summary: Pell names the Radiant Plateau Edge District as the first lead; Huginn orients northwest and silently disagrees.
turns.narration:
Pell's answer comes without hesitation — she's already been turning it over.

"Not here." She says it the way she says most things: plain, and slightly dry. "Not the Heights either — their acoustic work is old but it's theirs, layered on top of what was already there. They understand the resonance of it, not the bones." She shifts her weight off the path-edge and faces you properly, arms loose at her sides. "The Plateau's closer. Their optical arrays are built on pre-Transition infrastructure — the guides and the anchors both. Someone in the Edge District will know the older layer, even if they don't talk about it." A short pause. "Or they'll know who does."

Huginn moves three steps northwest on the packed earth — a correction — and settles again, looking back at you both. Pell clocks it.

"Your bird disagrees," she says. Not sarcastically. Actually noting it.

Dara watches the exchange from her doorstep without comment, mug still in both hands, waiting to see where it lands. The frost is lifting off the herb bed in thin wisps. Somewhere uphill a door opens and closes — the village waking into its morning — and the clay pipe-sections lean against the wall in their silent staged order, already counting down.

You have two things to do before you leave: Mara, and Aldric. And then the question of which direction to walk.

turns.player: Pell - Huginn is pretty reliable - are you ok with following his lead for a bit when we start out, after bringing Mara and Aldric up to speed?  Of course, I do Expect Huginn's lead to take us past that clear lake you mentioned...
turns.summary: Silas proposes following Huginn northwest after the morning briefings; Pell accepts; Dara sends them off with plain advice.
turns.narration:
You turn to Pell and nod toward Huginn, who is still facing northwest on the packed earth — unmoving, with the patient certainty of something that has already decided.

"Huginn's reliable," you say. "When we leave — after Mara and Aldric — I'd like to follow his lead for a bit. See where he points us." A pause, and then the rest of it, because it's worth naming: "I do expect his lead takes us past that snowmelt pool you mentioned."

Pell looks at Huginn. Huginn does not look back. He is, as far as anyone can tell, simply looking northwest, with the calm of a creature that finds human deliberation mildly unnecessary. "I can follow a bird," Pell says. It's almost dry enough to be deflection, but not quite — there's something quieter under it, the acceptance of someone who has spent enough time around you to know that Huginn's northwestward attention is probably a data point, not theater. Her eyes stay on the corvid a moment longer than casual. "Three hours north of the interference ridge, you said." She says it to the bird as much as to you.

Dara has been watching from her doorstep without comment. She takes a last pull from her mug. "Go brief Mara first," she says, quietly enough that it's advice, not an order. "She'll have already counted the blanks. Tell her what it means and she won't need much else. Aldric — tell him there's something wrong with the water he already suspects is wrong." She tilts her chin toward the village, where the first chimney smoke is thickening uphill. "Then go."

turns.player: Say a proper goodbye to Dara before leaving her doorstep — she gave you her network and her deadline
turns.summary: Proper farewell to Dara — she sends them off with one instruction: someone who can get their hands dirty, before week five.
turns.narration:
The goodbye is short, the way the right goodbyes are. You turn back to Dara where she stands at her doorstep with the mug in both hands, the frost still lifting off the herb bed to her left, and you meet her eyes — not the eyes she shows the channel crew, the other ones, the ones that are doing arithmetic about how much can still be held.

"Thank you," you say. "For the network. For the deadline. For not asking us to be certain when we aren't."

Dara looks at you for a moment. Then she tips her mug toward you — not quite a salute, not quite a toast — and says, "Week five. Before it." She glances once at Pell, then at Huginn's broad back still oriented northwest. "Don't bring me a scholar. Bring me someone who can get their hands dirty."

She steps back through her door without ceremony. It closes on the sound of clay pipe-sections shifting against the wall — she is already moving to the first task of the day.

Pell exhales through her nose beside you. "Right," she says. "Mara first, then Aldric. You take them through it — I'll fill what you miss." Huginn has not moved. He stands on the packed earth three steps northwest, patient as a question that has already decided its own answer, and the dawn light sits flat and cold across his shoulders.

The village is waking. Chimney smoke thickens uphill. The well and the red door of Mara's store are ten minutes south down the lane, and the smokehouse a few minutes beyond.

turns.player: Head to Mara's store first — red door, east side of the well. Brief her on the surge scope; she already knows the sleeve blank count, she just needs to know what it means.
turns.summary: Silas briefs Mara on the surge scope — she files it, ties off her ledger, and sends him to Aldric.
turns.narration:
The path from Dara's gate back into the village takes maybe five minutes at a walk — past the terraced gardens with their frost-damp soil, past a farmer moving early with a yoke across his shoulders who clocks Huginn and gives the three of you a wide berth without breaking stride. The well is the center of things, and at this hour it's just starting to gather its first traffic: a girl drawing water, an old man on the bench with his hands in his lap. Pell falls into step half a pace behind and says nothing, which is its own kind of company.

Mara's store is where it always is — red door, east side of the well, the faint oil-and-rope smell that leaks from the frame even before you knock. You don't knock. The door is open; she is already behind the counter with her ledger, and she looks up when Huginn's shadow crosses the sill ahead of you.

"Sleeve blanks," you say, because that's where she is. "Four failures in three weeks — you know the count. Here's what it means." You lay it out plain and brief: the buried vault section northeast of the wash-room, its surface footprint marked on the hillside, six weeks before load-surrender. Not a slow leak when it goes — a redirected surge, straight into the east-channel main feeder run. Dara Holt is already staging pipe-sections by sequence. You and Pell are leaving the valley to find someone who knows the older layer, pre-Transition structural work. Deadline before week five.

Mara listens the way she keeps her ledger — without interrupting, filing each piece as it lands. When you finish, she closes the book in front of her with two fingers, neatly. "East furlong farmer," she says. "Came back for a second blank last week. Said his first one just went." She pulls a cord from under the counter and ties it around the spine of the ledger — her own signal to herself that this volume doesn't leave the shelf until this is resolved. Then she looks at you directly: "Aldric will want to hear this from you, not from me. He's been watching the catch numbers. He'll have things to say." A pause that is not quite a question. "Are you going to him next?"

Pell is at the doorframe, arms folded, letting you run this. Huginn is on the step behind her, watching the well.

turns.player: Ask Mara about anyone she knows from the Edge District... and also ask her to hold the Valve Failure Timeline note as a second copy.
turns.summary: Mara names two Edge District contacts — Sorel and Leth — and takes the timeline note as a second copy without ceremony.
turns.narration:
Mara listens to the question about Edge District contacts the same way she listens to everything — head slightly tilted, one hand resting on the tied-off ledger, no visible reaction until she's ready to give one.

"I don't run accounts with the Plateau directly," she says, "but the old pass circuit did. Three, maybe four traders who worked the edge before the new route closed." She pulls a stub of chalk from her apron and writes two names on a scrap without being asked: Sorel and the Archivist woman, Leth — the first a spice-broker who wintered in the Edge District two years running, the second someone Mara describes only as "kept very careful records and did not like being surprised." She slides the scrap across the counter. "Sorel I haven't heard from in a season. Leth I expect is still there — she had the look of someone who'd found a place to stay."

You set the Valve Failure Timeline note on the counter beside the scrap. "Second copy," you say. "Hold it."

Mara looks at it. Looks at you. Picks it up and folds it once, precisely, and slides it under the tied-off ledger without a word — as if it were a receipt she'd already accounted for. The ledger goes on top. She taps it once with two fingers, which is apparently that settled.

Huginn shifts on the step outside. Through the open door the well's first traffic is thickening — a second yoke, a woman with a basket — and the smokehouse chimney east of the water wheel is already trailing its morning thread of gray. Aldric will be in. One stop left.

turns.player: Ask Mara if she has anything that would be useful for an expedition that will likely be needed... Pell can provide weapons and armor, but perhaps she has other useful things.
turns.summary: Mara equips Silas with expedition basics — tallow, oilcloth, pine-tar salve — and suggests Aldric may have a second waterskin.
turns.narration:
The question lands easy — Mara is already thinking in expedition terms, you can see it in the way she turns to face the back shelves before you finish speaking. She's been running the math since you told her about the surge scope.

"Rope you have," she says, more to herself than to you, scanning the shelves with the same economy she applies to her ledger. She comes back with three things set on the counter in a row: a wrapped block of hard tallow — rendered and pressed flat, good for leather, boots, rope-ends, and a dozen other trail needs; a fold of oilcloth, heavy enough to shed a night of rain, light enough to pack small; and a small tin of pine-tar salve, the kind that goes on cracked skin and keeps infection out of shallow cuts. She lines them up and looks at them, then looks at you.

"Edge District in early spring means cold nights and wet passes," she says. "The tallow doubles as fire-starter if the wood's damp. Salve is Dara's recipe — she'd agree you need it." A pause. "I'd add a second waterskin if you have the weight for it, but I'm out of good ones. Try Aldric — he sometimes has cured bladder from the winter butchering."

Pell, at the doorframe, doesn't move, but there's a faint tilt of her chin that reads as *she's right about the oilcloth.* Mara states the price plainly — eight cord-weight for all three, or she'll hold it against the Aldric offset if you'd rather keep the ready cord for the road.

turns.player: Take all three and run it through the Aldric offset — cleaner for the road; reduces your standing credit there.
turns.summary: Aldric offset settled; expedition kit packed; Mara advises briefing Aldric on the east-channel situation directly.
turns.narration:
The decision lands cleanly. "The Aldric offset," you say, and Mara nods once — already writing. She lifts the tied-off ledger, adds a line, and folds it back closed with the same quiet efficiency she brings to everything. The three items come across the counter: tallow block into the pack's side pocket where it won't bleed through, oilcloth folded flat against the bedroll, pine-tar tin tucked where you can reach it without unpacking everything. Thirty seconds of work. She doesn't watch you do it — she's already tallying something else in her head.

"Aldric's standing credit adjusted," she says, handing you the offset note — a single torn strip with her initials and a figure. "Give that to him when you go. He'll know what it means." She pauses, then adds: "He should also know the east furlong situation directly, if Dara hasn't already sent word. He's been watching the catch numbers and not saying anything out loud. He'll want to connect the pieces."

Pell pushes off the doorframe with a small shift of her shoulders — the posture of someone ready to move. From the step behind her, Huginn rotates his head northwest and holds it there, patient as stone.

Outside, the village is waking up in earnest. A pair of farmers arguing in low, tight voices pass the well without stopping — the word *share* audible, then *upstream*, then nothing. A child chasing a dog cuts between them and almost catches it. Normal morning, except for the faint gray-green tinge where water has pooled in the ruts near the pump-base — a thin, wrong color that nobody walking past looks directly at.

turns.player: Head straight to Aldric's smokehouse — hand him the offset note, brief him on the east-channel situation, and close out the provisioning account so you can leave.
turns.summary: Aldric briefed on the east-channel situation — offset note handed, account closed, Aldric commits to watching the downstream end.
turns.narration:
The smokehouse is two minutes from Mara's door — past the well, south along the lane, until the smell of cured fish and woodsmoke tells you you've arrived before the building does. Aldric is already at his block, knife back in hand, working through a row of river perch in the gray morning light. He hears your step and doesn't look up.

"Offset note," you say, and set Mara's strip on the clean end of the block where he can reach it. He picks it up, reads it in one pass, and tucks it into his apron without comment. The ledger adjustment will happen on his end before noon — you know him well enough to trust that.

The east-channel brief takes a little longer. You lay it out straight: the vault section northeast of the wash-room, six weeks to failure, the redirected surge landing directly on the east-channel main feeder run above his downstream customers. His hands slow on the third perch. He doesn't stop working, but he slows — and that, with Aldric, is the same as sitting down.

"So the fish count and the water are the same problem," he says. Not a question. He sets the knife flat, initials the offset note's counterpart in his own ledger with a short clean mark, and looks at you properly for the first time. "Dara knows?" You tell him she does, that she's already staging pipe-sections and counting seat sequences, and that Mara has a second copy of the timeline. He nods once — the slow, thorough kind — and picks the knife back up. "Then go," he says. "I'll watch the downstream end." Huginn, perched on the smokehouse eave, shifts once and reorients northwest. The provisioning account is closed. The smokehouse will hold the thread while you're gone.

turns.player: Ask Aldric if he has a cured bladder waterskin going spare — Mara mentioned he sometimes carries them, and a second water vessel is worth asking about before the road.
turns.summary: Aldric produces a cured bladder waterskin — two cord-weight or off the standing credit. The village stirs; the road waits.
turns.narration:
The question costs nothing to ask. You catch Aldric between perch — knife lifted, fish set aside — and put it plainly: Mara said he sometimes has a cured bladder going spare.

He doesn't answer immediately. He wipes the knife on his apron, sets it on the block, and disappears through the low door at the back of the smokehouse. You hear the scrape of a crate on stone. He comes back with something in his hand: a pig's bladder, cured and dried, sealed at the neck with a cord-wrapped wooden plug — not pretty, but tight, and it will hold water as well as anything Mara stocks. He turns it over once in his hands, checking the seam, and sets it on the block in front of you.

"Two cord-weight," he says. "Or I pull it from the standing credit." He picks up his knife and returns to the perch row without waiting for an answer, the transaction already decided as far as he's concerned. Outside, Huginn shifts on the eave — a small adjustment, wings tucked back down, gaze still fixed northwest. Pell stands just past the doorframe, pack on, not hurrying you, but the posture says the road is ready when you are.

The village is stirring behind you — a door somewhere, a dog, the first real light warming the lane stones from gray to pale gold. The smokehouse smells of woodsmoke and salt and the particular cold of a morning that hasn't decided to be warm yet. Everything that needed doing in Millbrook is done.

turns.player: Pull the bladder waterskin from standing credit and head out with Pell... we will want to stop by the forge first... we need to equip ourselves and rest and plan before the journey.
turns.summary: Aldric offset settled; cured bladder waterskin added to the pack; Silas and Pell head for the forge to rest and plan before departure.
turns.narration:
You tell Aldric to take it off the standing credit — simpler than coin at this hour — and he nods, already pulling his ledger from under the block. A mark goes down, the plug is checked once more by calloused thumb, and the bladder waterskin joins the pack without ceremony. Aldric's knife is back in the perch row before you've finished tightening the strap.

"Safe road," he says to the block, not to you. It's the closest thing to a farewell he has.

Pell is waiting at the doorframe when you turn, pack settled, Memory across your shoulders. She falls in beside you without a word — her own kit is light and organized, the way a smith packs when she's been watching other people pack badly for years. Huginn drops from the eave and banks northwest once, low over the lane, then rises to a rooftop ahead: directional, unhurried, the way it always means *this way*.

The forge is four minutes south down the lane — you make it before the village is properly awake, smoke rising thin from the banked fire. Inside, the smell of iron and coal and the faint resin note of the wine you left behind. The forge holds warmth like a promise. You have time: gear to check, the road ahead to name aloud, and a fire to sit beside before the day opens in full.

turns.player: Ask Pell what order she'd like to do things... we have expedition kit to go through and augment with weapons and tools and armor, talk about the rough plan forward, eat a meal, snuggle, rest.
turns.summary: Pell and Silas inventory the kit, plan the Edge District approach, share a meal, and rest together in the forge before departure.
turns.narration:
The forge is warm enough. Pell sets her pack against the bench leg and stretches her neck once — the small, unselfconscious movement of someone who has been carrying something and is glad to put it down. She looks at you with the patient, considering expression you've been learning to read: it means she is already sorting through options, it just hasn't come out as words yet.

"Kit first," she says. "I want to see what you're actually carrying before we talk routes, because half of route-planning is what you can carry, and I've seen you pack." She means it without heat — it's the same register she uses to assess iron. She pulls her own roll open on the bench: two good wool blankets, a short-hafted hatchet with a replaced eye, a coil of thin braided line, a leather kit-case with a few small tools inside — punch, draw-knife, small folded blade — and a wrapped bundle that turns out to be food. Hard cheese, a dense loaf gone slightly dry at the crust, a fold of salted strips. She lines them up on the bench without ceremony and nods at your pack. "Your turn."

You go through it together. She handles each item with the evaluating directness of someone who knows what things are actually for — turns the oilcloth over, checks the tallow for press marks, pinches the pine-tar salve tin's seal with her thumb. She notes the three waterskins and the cured bladder and says, quietly, "good." She approves of the rope, frowns at nothing specific about the trail parcels, and sets Memory across the two packs like a bar across the end of the bench when she's done with the inventory portion. Then she sits, pulls two pieces of cheese and a strip of salted meat from her bundle, sets one portion in front of you without asking, and begins to eat.

The planning talk is easier fed. She lays out her read: Edge District is three days if the weather holds and the new pass behaves — she has walked the old upper path as far as the interference ridge twice, but never beyond, and she says this plainly. She wants to know what Sorel and Leth look like as leads before they're standing in the Edge District asking strangers. "Sorel is a broker," she says, meaning she will read the contact as a transaction. "Leth is an archivist — come with a question worth writing down." She does not tell you what to do with that; she offers it and goes back to her bread. After a while she moves to the other side of the bench — her shoulder against yours, ankles hooked, the warmth of the banked fire ahead and the warmth of her beside you. Huginn shifts once on the roofline and resettles. Nobody talks for a while. The forge ticks as the metal cools. Outside, Millbrook wakes slowly — a door, a bucket, a child somewhere — but nothing comes through the doorway, and the morning holds.

turns.player: Tell Pell there's more to my pack and kit than she's seen so far... unbutton my shirt and show her the mark from the palework feedback... show her other hidden body areas with the scars of my craft.... then say, "I'd like to see the rest of your kit too"
turns.summary: Silas shows Pell his palework scars; she meets it with her own — the forge quiet, the exchange unhurried and honest.
turns.narration:
You set Memory against the bench and say, plainly, that there is more to the kit than what she's unpacked. Then you reach for the top button of your shirt.

You undo three buttons — no more than needed — and open the collar. The palework scar is on your left side below the clavicle: a pale branching trace, fine as frost on glass, where craft feedback closed too hard into skin during a bad read three winters back. You tilt the fabric to the forge light so she can see it properly. Then you turn your right forearm and push the sleeve: a shorter mark there, older, the kind that silvered clean. Two scars. You don't perform the showing — you just hold still and let her look.

"There's a third," you say, and touch your ribs through the shirt, over the lower left — you don't unbutton further, just mark the location. "Palework. When you push the read too hard into something that doesn't want to be read, it comes back along the same channel it went in."

Pell has not moved. She is sitting with her bread forgotten in one hand, looking at the branching mark with the focused attention she gives to a flaw in iron — not alarmed, not soft. Cataloguing. She reaches out and traces the branch-tip with two fingers, not asking permission, because she is the kind of person who touches things to understand them and that is simply who she is. Her fingers are warm and carry the faint roughness of a smith's hands.

"Palework," she says. The word is flat, but her attention is not. She holds the contact a moment longer than the assessment requires. You watch her face the way she watched yours: the information is all there, and you don't miss any of it. When you have found everything worth finding you bring it all to bear at once, unhurried and deliberate, and you watch what that does.

Afterward she's quiet for a long moment, weight settled against you, one hand loose on your sternum. The forge ticks as it cools. Huginn shifts once on the roofline — a single talon-tap, timed — and Pell lets out a short breath through her nose that is almost a laugh.

"Thorough," she says. A plain verdict. She means it as a compliment and you both know it.

turns.player: Not thorough enough yet... you've heated the iron... now it's time to seat it. Enter her with an angle tilt of her hips, slowly, watching her response and seeing her nipples spike.
turns.summary: Silas enters Pell slowly, watching her response — the forge morning deepening, Huginn attending from the roofline.
turns.narration:
The morning holds them both — forge amber low behind you, the lane outside fully awake with cart-noise that belongs to another world.

You read her the way she read you: where her breath changes, what her hands do when she's not directing them. The round scar at her hipbone again under your thumb — you've found it twice now and said nothing, and the silence is its own attention. When you shift her weight, tilting her hips with both hands, deliberate and without hurry, she understands what you're doing before you do it. Her breath catches in her chest — not surprise, not resistance, just her body marking the moment before — and you watch her nipples spike as you enter her, slowly, the angle precise, her hands tightening at your shoulder. She stays with her eyes open. So do you.

Her back arches just enough. One long exhale through parted lips — controlled, barely — and then she is looking at you with an expression that is not quite composed and knows it, and does not look away. You hold the pace she gave you: unhurried, nothing missed, returning to what you find. Every adjustment she makes is information. You read it the way she reads iron — weight, give, the place that holds heat longest — and you work it accordingly.

Huginn, on the roofline, gives one deliberate talon-tap. The timing, as always, is a comment. Neither of you acknowledge it.

turns.player: Ask her what that feels like... tell her this is a race that neither of us wants to be the first to end... because it's like watching her run ahead of you - give her the full measure of depth and watch what she does.
turns.summary: Silas gives Pell the full depth and names the race neither wants to win — she answers plainly, admits she's already behind, and doesn't look away.
turns.narration:
You give her the full depth — no tease, no staged arrival, just the whole of it, deliberate, watching her face as it lands.

She doesn't close her eyes. That's what you notice first. Her head tips back a degree, her breath goes out through parted lips, one hand grips your shoulder hard enough to whiten the knuckles — but her eyes stay on yours, and that's a choice, that's her matching you measure for measure. Her hips settle into the angle you set, taking the weight of it, and for a moment the forge morning simply stops: cart-noise outside, amber light behind you, the tick of cooling iron — all of it held at the edge of the scene, not touching you.

"What does that feel like?" You ask it quietly, without breaking the contact. And you tell her: *this is a race neither of us wants to be the first to end.* A pause. *Like watching you run ahead of me.*

Pell holds the eye contact for one more beat — two — and something in her expression shifts. Not armor down; it's already been down a while. This is the layer underneath that, the one she didn't announce. Her jaw tightens, releases. "Like —" She starts, stops, tries the register she uses for ironwork diagnosis and discovers it doesn't fit here. Her hand moves from your shoulder to the back of your neck, not pulling, just holding. "Like I am already behind," she says, plainly, and the admission costs her something she pays without flinching. "And I don't know when it happened."

She moves then — not racing, not redirecting, just answering in the only register she's fully fluent in, her hips finding your rhythm and adding to it, the shift small and precise and completely felt. Her breath comes faster. She still doesn't look away.

turns.player: Be the hammer to her anvil - Let her take the lead but then give her what she wants - watch as much detail as can come through - what bounces, what makes sounds, the tempo.
turns.summary: Silas is the hammer to Pell's anvil — the forge morning running at the rhythm they've found together.
turns.narration:
You let the rhythm find you both — not passive, not leading, the particular thing between hammer and anvil where neither is nothing and the force moves through both.

She takes it. Her hips roll to meet each depth, a tempo she sets without announcing it, and you read it the way she taught you to read iron: by what it does under pressure, not what it says. Her breath comes in short, even pulls through parted lips. Her hand stays at the back of your neck, not pulling, just there — the way a hand rests on a piece that's in process, feeling for the heat. The sound of it is low and specific: the shift of the bench beneath, the soft percussion of skin, her exhale on the close beat that is almost a sound and not quite a word. You watch the flush move up from her chest. You watch her jaw set when you hit the angle she needs and her eyes go briefly, involuntarily bright before she pulls them back to yours.

What bounces: her hair, loose at the temple, one strand across her jaw she doesn't touch. The slight give at her hip where your grip is. The small shudder that starts high in her chest when you match her upstroke exactly, when you stop compensating and simply give her what she's moving toward.

She makes a sound — short, involuntary, the register of something that slipped past her — and her rhythm breaks for half a beat. She finds it again. Her other hand, the one not at your neck, presses flat against your chest, not pushing, the same gesture she uses on iron to feel the even heat. Checking. Taking stock. Her eyes come back to yours and there is nothing composed in them, and she is aware of it and she does not look away.

From the roofline, Huginn is still.

turns.player: Let her set the pace to its end — follow every signal, match every shift, give her exactly what she moves toward until she gets there first
turns.summary: The forge morning concludes — Pell gives her verdict, and an unknown knock interrupts the quiet.
turns.narration:
The rhythm doesn't break — it builds the way iron builds heat, degree by degree until the thing is done.

You read every signal she gives you. When her hips roll forward you meet them. When her grip tightens at your neck you give her more weight, more depth, and you watch: her throat working, the flush moving from her chest upward, her free hand fisting into the bench-cloth and releasing, fisting again. Her breath comes faster and loses its evenness — short pulls through parted lips, no longer measured, the composure she held so long finally spending itself. Her eyes stay on yours until they can't. Her head drops back, jaw loose, a sound leaving her that is nothing like the controlled register of ironwork — low, specific, hers alone — and her whole body draws up tight around you and holds.

You hold the depth. Let her have all of it, nothing taken back, nothing hurried — the same thoroughness she named and the same patience. Her hips work through the finish in long, shuddering pulls, taking the last of what she needs, and you give it without reservation. The forge-tick and the cart-noise outside are very far away.

When she comes back to herself her hand is still at your neck. Her breathing is deliberate — not composed yet, just deliberate, the way she breathes when she's deciding something. She looks at you. A long beat. Then, flat and certain, the ironworker's verdict: "Thorough." A pause. "Genuinely." Something in her face shifts — not soft exactly, but open in a way the forge-face isn't. She lets out a slow breath through her nose and her hand moves from your neck to flat on your chest, the forge-worker's check: feeling for even heat. Then three brisk knocks land on the south-lane door — not Corvin's knock and not Aldric's. Someone waiting. Huginn shifts on the roofline above: one slow adjustment of weight, head angling down toward the door.

turns.player: Before the echoes of the release fully end - bring Pells hand gently with yours to touch Memory - and rune whatever skills come most readily through the spear, inviting Pell to do the same.  The spear itself is linked to endings, to Pell and to you.  This acts may activate or reveal something.
turns.summary: Silas and Pell touch Memory together at the forge morning's end — the spear takes a shared imprint, Deathsense and The Attended End running in one channel through joined hands.
turns.narration:
The knock hangs unanswered. You don't move yet.

Her hand is still flat on your chest, rising and falling with you both. The forge-face is gone — her eyes open, clear, a little unguarded in the way of someone who has just spent something real and has not yet put the room back on. You bring your hand over hers, and without a word you move it — both your hands together — to Memory, where it lies across the packs at the bench end. The spear is cool under your palm. Hers settles beside yours a half-second later, and you feel the moment she recognizes what you're asking: her fingers spread and flatten, the grip-instinct of the forge-worker reading a surface.

You don't announce it. You let Deathsense open the way it does — not a door swinging wide but a narrowing of what you're attending to, the noise of the morning dropping away until only the spear's register is left. The death-bound thread is there: load-bearing, clean end to end, the capacitor flush at the collar. The Attended End opens alongside it without effort, the two running in the same channel the way water finds the grade — and you feel the difference immediately. Deathsense reads the spear's relation to endings in general; The Attended End reads *this* ending, *this* moment, what is concluding *here* and *now*. What concludes here is the forge morning — this particular gathered thing, the two of you and the banked fire and the lane waking outside. The spear catches both readings at once, and the runes you pressed into the collar north quarter warm under your fingers, not with heat, but with *attention*.

Pell's hand moves. She's not guided — you feel her find it herself, some grain-reading instinct older than instruction. Her thumb presses into the maker's mark she set oblique, the one you told her was load-bearing, and she holds it the way she'd hold a weld that hasn't quite set. She says nothing. Huginn shifts on the roofline above — one quiet adjustment of weight, head tilting down. The rune-thread that runs from collar to blade hums once, barely audible, the sound a struck knife makes against still water: one clean ring, then gone. The forge space holds it for a moment — the three of you, the spear, the ending of the morning caught in the binding like a pressed flower. Then the lane outside reasserts itself: cart-wheels, a voice calling a name, the knock waiting at the south door.

Pell lifts her eyes to yours. The forge-face is still down. She says, flat and certain, the ironworker's register back but softer at the edges: "It took something from both of us. Something it didn't have before." She means it as a technical observation. She also means it as the other thing.

turns.player: It did.  And we both took and gave something to eachother... we are more bound together now Pell.  In writing or not... I hope you liked what you received because I certain did, and I love thinking that we can continue to have this kind of thing any time we want...
turns.summary: Silas names the binding plainly — Pell receives it, turns her hand, and closes her fingers around his.
turns.narration:
The knock waits. You don't rush it.

Her hand is still on your chest, fingers loose now, and you cover it with yours — not holding it down, just acknowledging it. The forge ticks as it cools. Outside, cart-wheels on the south lane, someone calling a name you don't recognize. In here, the particular quiet of a thing that has run its full course and left something behind.

"We are more bound together now," you tell her. Plain. Not a question. "In writing or not." You watch her face for it — the way you've learned to watch her, not for the forge-face but for what comes after it goes. "I hope you liked what you received. Because I certainly did." A pause, and then the rest of it, because it's true and there's no reason to keep it: "And I love thinking that we can have this kind of thing — any time we want."

Pell looks at you for a moment with that open-faced stillness she lands in after the composure goes. Not undone — just present. Something moves at the corner of her mouth that isn't quite a smile but is adjacent to one. She turns her hand under yours so her palm faces up, and her fingers close once around your hand — the grip of a smith checking a fit, and then softer than that. "You're going to make that hard to argue with," she says, low, entirely even. "Good." She means both things. She always means both things.

Huginn shifts on the roofline. One deliberate rearrangement of weight, head angled toward the south-lane door. The knock hasn't come again yet, but the morning is still out there, waiting.

turns.player: Now, are you going to answer the door, or do you want another round of forging?!
turns.summary: Pell chooses the door — and opens it.
turns.narration:
The knock does not repeat. Whoever is on the south-lane side has settled into waiting — patient, or uncertain, or both.

Pell's eyes come back to yours. The open-faced look holds a beat longer, and then the corner of her mouth moves — not a full smile, something dryer than that, the forge-face reassembling itself one feature at a time. She looks down at her hand, still closed around yours, and then back up.

"Forging," she says, flat and certain, the way she names a temperature. Then she releases your hand, sits up, and reaches for her shirt in one unhurried motion. "But the door first."

She pulls the linen over her head and shakes her hair back. Her hands go to the buttons — efficient, the same hands that held rhythm for an hour now moving through a familiar task — and she is already half-composed by the time she stands. She glances once at Memory, still warm across the packs, and says nothing about it. Some things don't need a second word this morning.

Huginn shifts on the roofline — a single deliberate resettling of weight, head angled toward the lane — and that, apparently, is the bird's opinion on the matter.

Pell steps to the south-lane door and lifts the latch.

turns.player: Stand, dress quietly, and see what the morning brought — this is her forge and her door
turns.summary: The carter is turned away, the forge-face reassembles, Pell sets the whetstone apart and asks if Silas is ready.
turns.narration:
The knock was a carter — a sleeve-blank order, wrong day, sent away with Pell's instructions and a look that closed the subject. The south-lane door swings back to the amber interior and she latches it behind her, one hand flat against the wood as though checking it's truly shut.

You've been dressing quietly while she handled it. Shirt, belt, boots — the familiar reassembly. Memory is already across both packs, the forge-tick the only sound now that the carter's wheels have moved off. The banked fire has gone from amber to cherry-low; another hour and it'll be out. Huginn shifts on the roofline above — you can hear the small adjusting weight — and resettles without comment.

Pell turns from the door. She looks at you the way she looks at a finished piece: straight, without apology, checking for something she's not going to name. Whatever she finds, it settles her. She crosses to the bench and picks up the whetstone — your whetstone, the fine river-stone you gave her — and sets it deliberately to one side, apart from the tool-clutter. A small act. She doesn't explain it.

"Ready?" The forge-face is on, but not all the way — there's a seam in it, visible now that you know where to look. She reaches for her pack.

turns.player: Start taking your clothes back off - She said she wanted another forging... and you are ready to give her one.
turns.summary: Silas undresses again; Pell crosses the floor and comes back to him without hesitation.
turns.narration:
The whetstone is still apart from the clutter. That was deliberate — you noticed it when she set it there, the small ceremony of a thing placed rather than put down.

You reach for your shirt hem and pull it back over your head in one motion, folding it across the pack with the same unhurried quiet you used to dress. Boots next, unlaced and set aside. By the time you straighten she has turned from the door and is watching you from across the forge-floor, one hand still flat against the latch. The forge-face is half-assembled. You watch it come apart again.

"You said you wanted another round," you say, not a question, and there is nothing in your voice but plain intent and the particular warmth you've learned she responds to — not performance, not theater, just the thing itself offered directly. You watch her eyes. The flat certainty she brings to ironwork is there, but it's aimed differently now, and when she crosses the space between you her stride has the same quality as her hammer stroke — decided before she moves, no wasted motion. She does not stop when she reaches you. She stops just past the point where stopping would still be neutral, close enough that the warmth between you is already present before either of you lifts a hand.

Huginn shifts on the roofline. One small sound — not a comment, just attendance. You are aware of it the way you are always aware of him at moments that matter: present, patient, entirely unrushed. The banked fire has gone cherry-low and the south-lane light has softened at the door. There is no knock. The morning has given you this, and Pell's hands, when they find the edge of your belt, move with the same methodical attention she brought to the spear's collar — knowing what she's doing, taking the time to do it right.

turns.player: Take the opening she's given — set the tempo yourself this time, give her something to meet
turns.summary: Silas sets the tempo — the second round is his to open and close.
turns.narration:
The second round is yours to open.

You cross the last few feet of forge-floor to her — not fast, not slow, the same deliberate register she used when she came to you — and bring one hand to the side of her face, thumb at her jaw. She's watching you with the forge-face fully gone now, which you have learned takes something. You tip her chin up slightly and kiss her once, unhurried, just long enough that she has to decide whether to lean in. She does. Her hand finds your shirt — your missing shirt — and closes on your collarbone instead, a reflex redirected.

You break it, stay close. 'Your tempo last time,' you say, quiet. 'Mine now.'

The flat look she gives you carries something underneath it — not argument, more like a recalibration. Her hand slides from your collarbone to your shoulder. She says nothing, which is its own answer.

What follows has the quality of iron-work done well: patient, purposeful, no wasted motion. You read her the way you read the shaft this morning — not performing attention but actually paying it, tracking every shift and where it wants to go. The forge-tick slows in your awareness. Huginn is silent on the roofline. The cherry-low fire gives the room its amber and nothing else.

When she finally gets there — on your terms, at your tempo, but undeniably hers — she makes a sound that has no forge-face in it at all, and her forehead comes down to your shoulder. Her fingers are in your hair. She breathes. You hold what you built together and let it settle, the way you held the binding after the rune rang.

After a moment she lifts her head and looks at you with an expression that is, for once, entirely unguarded — and then the dry edge comes back to her voice: 'Thorough,' she says. 'Again.'

turns.player: Just want to prove to you how practical I can be.  You had a need... I will fill it.  As many times as you want.  You scars are exotic and beautiful, your concentration and ironsense is a love tonic, I am a wildcard and strange and that undoes you.  I like that.  I want you to want me whenever you want to.  We are bonded.
turns.summary: Silas names the bond plainly at the forge — Pell receives it, cheek to palm, and admits she's managing it badly.
turns.narration:
You say it into the space between you — low, without ceremony, the way you'd name a fact you've been sitting with long enough that it's stopped feeling like a confession.

'Just want to prove to you how practical I can be. You had a need. I'll fill it — as many times as you want.' The hand at her jaw stays. 'Your scars are exotic and beautiful. Your concentration, the way you read iron — it undoes me. And I think I undo you. I like that. I want you to want me whenever you want to. We are bonded.'

She goes still in the way she goes still — not withdrawn, the opposite. The full attention she usually keeps distributed across the forge, the fire, the work in hand, all of it arriving in one place. You.

For a moment she doesn't answer. The cherry-low fire ticks. Above the roofline, Huginn doesn't move.

Then she turns her head just enough to press her cheek once into your palm — a small thing, deliberate as the whetstone set apart from the clutter — and looks back at you. 'Practical,' she repeats, dry as ash. 'Yes. You are exactly that.' The corner of her mouth moves. 'You're also strange and you've put a dead bird's thread into my spear and I let you, which tells you something about my current reasoning.' She turns her hand and lays it flat against your sternum — not pushing, just present, taking inventory. 'I don't need you to want me to want you. I already do. That's the problem I'm managing.' A beat. 'I'm managing it badly.' The flat look, and underneath it everything that isn't flat at all. 'Good.'

turns.player: talk about what you can tell of our bond through Memory, how it will grow as it awakens... how it is made of both of us, our first child, so to say. Concede that she is managing her want of me very poorly indeed, and that is all the more intoxicating.  Ask her if there are any wanding tinkers or mooning farmhands you will need to challenge to duels because of this.
turns.summary: Silas and Pell hold Memory between them and name it plainly — first child, made of both; the want between them named again, intoxicating and admitted.
turns.narration:
You lift Memory from where it rests across both packs — one hand at the socket throat, one at the butt — and hold it between you the way you'd show someone a thing you'd made together. Which is, you suppose, exactly what it is.

'Feel it,' you say, and she does — her fingers close around the shaft just above yours, not gripping, reading. The binding is quiet at this hour, cherry-low like the forge, but it's there: the thread that runs from the collar rune through the capacitor and back, the joined imprint from this morning, Huginn's patience threaded through the iron at a depth neither of you put in consciously. You can sense it the way you sense a room you've stood in long enough — not seeing it, knowing it. 'It's made of both of us,' you say. 'Your maker's mark is load-bearing. Not decoration. Not acknowledgment. It holds. What grows in here grows out of what we both put down, and it will keep growing as it wakes — the more endings it attends, the more it will carry.' You pause, considering. 'Our first child. Not the most conventional way to start, but I've heard worse.' You watch her face, the flat look that is not flat at all.

Her fingers tighten once on the shaft, testing the balance, then release. She doesn't say anything for a moment — she is, you have learned, someone who weighs a thing before she sets it down. When she speaks it is quiet and direct: 'I can feel the collar. When I'm not looking at it.' That is, from her, a significant admission. You rest Memory back across the packs.

'You are managing your want of me very poorly,' you tell her, without any attempt to soften it. 'Badly, by your own account. In front of your own forge.' The corner of your mouth moves. 'I want you to know that is entirely intoxicating and I have no intention of making it easier for you.' She gives you the look — the one where the forge-face has completely abandoned the project — and you hold it. Then, because the morning is clear and you have a road to walk and you are still a person with a sense of proportion: 'Are there any wandering tinkers I should be aware of. Mooning farmhands. Someone with eyes on you who is going to require me to defend your honor in a formal capacity. I want the names before we leave the village.'

turns.player: Wait for her answer — let whatever she says about tinkers or farmhands land fully before moving
turns.summary: Pell names no prior claims — one admission about two winters ago, then a child at the door breaks the quiet and the road calls.
turns.narration:
The silence before she answers is not discomfort. You have learned to read her silences — this one is the kind she takes when she is deciding whether to say the plain thing or the careful thing, and the plain thing is winning.

'No tinkers,' Pell says. She sets Memory back across the packs with both hands, deliberate, a smith returning a finished piece to its rest. 'No farmhands.' A beat. 'There was a man in the Heights two winters back — sonic work, came down for iron fittings three times over a season. I let it run longer than it should have.' Her jaw shifts once, not quite a shrug. 'He stopped coming. I didn't go looking.' She picks up the river whetstone from the bench — not to use it, just to hold it, the familiar weight of the thing. 'So. No. No one you need to duel.'

She sets the stone back in exactly the same spot.

The door rattles — not a knock, the sound of someone leaning against it from outside and then reconsidering. A thin voice, a child's, comes through the planking: 'Is the smith in? My da says the—' A pause. A longer pause. Then, with the absolute clarity only a child achieves: 'There's someone else in there.' The voice retreats, quick-footed on the lane stones, and is gone.

Huginn, from the roofline, offers a single dry syllable. Not quite a word. Not quite anything else.

Pell looks at the door. Then at you. Something in the forge-face has not fully reassembled, and she seems to have decided it doesn't need to. 'We should move,' she says, reaching for her pack. She says it the way she names most things — practical, clean — and then she does not take the pack yet, which means she is waiting for something she hasn't asked for.

turns.player: We should move, yes... thank you for telling me - kiss her on the cheek.  What haven't you asked for?
turns.summary: Silas kisses Pell on the cheek and asks what she hasn't asked for — she names the habit plainly, picks up her pack, and the road calls.
turns.narration:
You lean in and press your lips to her cheek — not the jaw, not the mouth, the cheek, which is its own thing entirely: the kind of touch that doesn't reach for anything, just lands and means it. She goes still for half a second the way she does, that full-attention arrival, and then she breathes out and the forge-face comes back up, softer than usual at the edges.

'What haven't I asked for.' She says it flat, not quite a question — she's repeating the shape of it back to herself, holding it up to the forge-light to see what it's made of. The river whetstone goes into her coat pocket, which is not where it lives, and she notices and puts it back on the bench with a small precise gesture that has nothing to do with the whetstone. 'I haven't asked for any of it.' A beat. 'That's the habit.'

She picks up her pack — the real pack, both hands, settled onto her shoulder with the ease of something done a thousand times — and there is something in the motion that is also an answer. Not deflection. Resolution. She has been waiting for the road because the road is the thing she knows how to ask for. The other wanting, the particular wanting she named at the forge morning's close with your hand on her jaw and her cheek pressed into your palm — that one she's been carrying without a word for it for longer than two winters, you suspect, and she is not about to start making requests now. She'll manage it badly and be content that she is, and she'll let you watch her do it, and that is, from Pell, an enormous amount.

Huginn drops from the roofline and lands once, hard, on the doorpost. One sound — low, deliberate, timed — and it tilts its head toward the south lane and the road north. It is not gentle about it. The forge fire has gone to ash. The morning is moving without you.

turns.player: Before you go - tell her that asking is a skill, and she cna practice it on you... hand her the whetstone and head out the door.
turns.summary: Silas gives Pell the whetstone and the simplest possible lesson — asking is a skill, practice it on me — and both of them walk out into the morning.
turns.narration:
You pick up the river whetstone from the bench — the exact spot she returned it to, twice — and hold it out to her on your open palm. Not a gesture, not a ceremony. Just the stone, offered.

'Asking is a skill,' you say. 'You can practice it on you.'

She looks at your hand for a moment. Then she takes the whetstone and turns it once between her fingers — reading it the way she reads everything, grain and weight and what it's for — and tucks it into her coat pocket. This time she leaves it there.

Huginn drops from the doorpost to your shoulder in one unhurried movement, settles, and says nothing. The forge behind you has gone fully cold. The south-lane door is open and the mid-morning light is coming through it flat and pale, early-spring without any warmth in it yet, and the lane is quiet enough that you can hear the river two blocks over running its faint gray-green sound.

Pell shoulders her pack, adjusts the strap once with a smith's economy of motion, and steps through the door ahead of you. She does not look back at the forge. The road north is what she knows how to want openly, and she is already wanting it.

turns.player: Walk out with Pell and let the road settle around you both before saying anything — let the village fall behind in silence
turns.summary: Silas and Pell walk north out of the village in silence — a child runner catches them just short of the north gate with a cloth-wrapped message from Mara.
turns.narration:
The south lane is narrow enough that your shoulders almost brush walking side by side. Huginn rides your left shoulder, oriented north, head moving once to clock a cat on a windowsill and then settling. Pell's pack sits square on her back, the way she carries everything — no adjustment, no shifting. Memory lies across both packs, ash shaft pale in the early light, collar rune hidden against her oilcloth roll.

The lane opens past the well, and then you are past the well, and the village begins to thin at the edges the way villages do — one more house, a fence, a garden plot with last year's bean-stakes still standing, and then the track north with the upper meadow ahead and the river sound on your left. The cold is real but not biting. The light is the thin honest kind that comes before the day commits to anything.

Huginn shifts his weight once on your shoulder. Pell walks.

Then — behind you, quick feet on packed earth — a child's voice: 'Warden.' Not a question. Not the retreating child from the south-lane door, but the same age, maybe, or close. A girl, seven or eight, in a worn blue wool coat two sizes large, catching up at a run. She stops four feet back when you turn, breathing hard, and holds out a folded scrap of cloth as if she's been told to deliver it and not lose it. 'Mara said to catch you before the north gate. Said you'd know what it was.'

Pell glances at the cloth, then at you. Huginn's head turns. The upper meadow is fifty yards ahead; the north gate, not much more.

turns.player: Take the cloth and read it now
turns.summary: Mara's note names Sorel — a key Edge District contact — spotted at the smokehouse. Decision point before the north road.
turns.narration:
The cloth scrap is small — torn from a piece Mara keeps behind the counter, the kind she uses for wrapping cord-weight parcels. You take it from the child's hand and open it with your thumb.

Four words in Mara's neat utility script, no preamble:

*Pip saw Sorel. Smokehouse.*

Huginn turns his head to read it over your shoulder. Or seems to. He does not comment.

You fold it back along its crease and look up. The child — Pip Cotter herself, you realize, which means Mara sent the message by the subject of the message, which is exactly the kind of practical efficiency that makes her store run — is still breathing a little hard from the run, watching your face for whether she's done her job or not.

Pell hasn't moved from where she stopped. She's read your expression already and is waiting, Memory balanced across the top of her pack, nothing impatient in her stance. The north gate stands open thirty yards ahead, the track beyond it pale with morning frost going soft in the sun.

The smokehouse is back south. Sorel is one of the two names Mara gave you for Edge District contacts — the spice-broker who wintered there two years running, not heard from in a season. If Pip saw him, he is in Millbrook now, or was recently enough for the sighting to matter.

turns.player: Show Pell the note and decide together - give Pip one piece of dried starfruit as a thank you for a job well done.
turns.summary: Mara's note shared with Pell; Pip paid in starfruit for a job well done — Sorel at the smokehouse changes the calculus.
turns.narration:
You hold the cloth scrap out to Pell without comment. She takes it with two fingers, reads it once, reads it again the way she reads anything load-bearing, and hands it back.

'Sorel,' she says. Not a question — she's filing it. 'Your contact from the edge district is currently standing in Aldric's smokehouse.' A beat. 'Which is behind us.'

Huginn makes a sound that could be agreement or commentary. It is impossible to tell.

You reach into the traveler's pack and find the dried starfruit by feel — the sealed packet, cloth-wrapped tight. You open it at the fold and work one piece free without tearing the rest, then crouch down to Pip Cotter's level and hold it out on your palm the way you held the whetstone to Pell not ten minutes ago. Different weight entirely — this is for a job done well and the kid knows it.

Pip takes it with both hands, examines it the way a serious person examines an unfamiliar thing, and bites off a corner. Whatever she expected, the tartness catches her. She holds her ground. Four feet back, Pell watches this with something that is not quite a smile but occupies the same space.

You straighten up. North gate thirty yards ahead, morning track beyond it, Sorel behind you at the smokehouse — a contact who went quiet for a season and is now, without announcement, present. The road can wait a few minutes.

turns.player: Let Pell know that you'll shadow step to get to the smokehouse as fast as possible.  Have her meet you there.
turns.summary: Silas shadowsteps to the smokehouse ahead of Pell — arrives unseen at the back door, Sorel still unaware.
turns.narration:
The north gate is thirty yards ahead, pale track open, meadow light just beginning to warm. You turn back toward Pell instead.

"Smokehouse shadow," you say, low enough that it's just the two of you. You tip your chin toward the nearest shadow — the gate's dry-stone wall throws a good one this time of morning, dark against the frost-pale ground. "I can be there before Aldric's had time to explain our absence. Meet me."

Pell looks at the shadow, looks at you, and her expression does the small thing it does when she is filing new information under practical. "Go," she says, and reaches across both packs to check Memory's lash without breaking eye contact. "I know the lane."

Huginn lifts off your shoulder and is simply gone — somewhere between here and there, already ahead of you, the way he gets. Pip Cotter watches with the wide-eyed gravity of a child who has just understood that the morning got interesting, starfruit pinched between her fingers.

You step into the gate's shadow, feel the cold swallow the light, and step out of the smokehouse's rear overhang shadow on the other side — salt and wood-smoke landing on you all at once. The back door is two strides ahead. Whatever Sorel is doing inside, you have arrived ahead of the news of your coming.

turns.player: Pause at the back door and use Order-Sense on the interior — read what situation Sorel is actually in before you step through.
turns.summary: Silas reads the smokehouse interior through Order-Sense — Sorel is present and circling something with Aldric, news withheld on both ends.
turns.narration:
The back-door shadow is still cold and deep — frost on the latch-iron, salt and woodsmoke catching at the back of your throat. You settle one shoulder against the overhang post and let Order-Sense open quietly, the way you'd open a hand rather than reach.

The substrate is thin here. The sense comes in at about half weight — shapes and relationships, not fine grain. But it's enough.

Sorel is at the smoking rack. Standing, not seated — someone who arrived recently and hasn't found their ease yet. Aldric is on the far side of the block, knife in hand, the familiar rhythm of the morning perch row. Between them: a conversation that has been going for some time without going anywhere, the structural pattern of two people who know each other well enough to talk without saying the thing they're actually circling. The design of it reads as: *news being withheld on both ends*.

Nothing armed. Nothing cornered. No urgency in the physical arrangement that suggests Sorel is leaving soon, but the pack reads as present — the weight distribution of someone who hasn't put it down yet.

Huginn shifts one talon on the ridge above you and looks at the door with the patient attention he gives to things that are about to change.

turns.player: Use The Attended End on Sorel through the wall — read what's ending for this person before you put your face in front of them.
turns.summary: Silas reads Sorel through the wall — a season ending, not a crisis, something withheld and compressed. The frost on the latch holds.
turns.narration:
The back-door shadow holds you a moment longer. You set Memory's butt against the overhang post quietly, close your eyes, and let The Attended End do what it does.

It comes in partial — the smokehouse wall is not much, but Millbrook's death-life axis runs toward the living side, and the ability works best where endings are close to the surface. What you get is impressionistic rather than precise: something is ending for Sorel, yes, and it has the texture of a season rather than a crisis. Not an emergency, not a wound. A situation that has been going on long enough to have a shape, and the shape is coming to a natural close. You catch the faint register of travel in it — a road that is finishing, not one that is beginning. There is also something compressed in there, something withheld, and you cannot tell if the withholding is from Aldric or from themselves or from the situation entire. What Marrow does not give you: cause. Whether this ending is chosen or imposed. What is on the other side of it.

You open your eyes. Huginn, up on the ridge, has tilted its head five degrees toward you — it noticed. It does not add anything. It doesn't need to.

From inside: a low creak of the smoking rack. Aldric's knife pauses on the block, then resumes. They are still circling. Pell will be two minutes, maybe three. The frost on the latch-iron is starting to read as a question.

turns.player: Step inside now, without announcing anything — walk in through the back door on your own terms, let your presence interrupt the circle, and see where Sorel's news lands without giving them more time to decide what to say.
turns.summary: Silas steps into the smokehouse unannounced — Sorel's first words reveal they expected him.
turns.narration:
The latch lifts without a sound — frost-iron cold through your palm, door swinging inward on oiled hinges. The smokehouse interior comes in at once: salt and old smoke, the low warmth of the curing fire, rows of perch hanging in their neat order above the block. Aldric's knife resumes its stroke before he registers the motion. Sorel looks up from the rack.

You don't announce yourself. You step in, set Memory's butt against the door-frame, and let the interruption do the work.

Aldric reads you in under a second — the knife pauses again, a small reset, and something in his expression settles into the version he wears when things are about to become less circuitous. "Silas." Nothing else. He glances once at Sorel the way a man glances at a door he's been trying to remember to oil.

Sorel is younger than the name on Mara's scrap suggested — mid-thirties at most, travel-worn in the specific way of someone who has been on a single road for a long time rather than many roads in sequence. The pack is still on their shoulder, which tells you something: they came in to say a thing, and then found the saying of it harder than expected. They meet your eyes with the steady assessment of someone who has spent years reading rooms they just walked into.

"You're earlier than I thought," Sorel says, which is interesting phrasing for someone who has no reason to have thought about you at all.

turns.player: "Mara sent me — she thought you might have something I need before I leave the valley." Name the connection openly; let Sorel understand you know who they are and where they've been.
turns.summary: Silas names Mara as his referral — Sorel recognizes the credential and opens the conversation.
turns.narration:
The words land easy, the way true things do. "Mara sent me," you say, not quite to Aldric and not quite to Sorel — to the room, letting each of them sort out which part belongs to them. "She thought you might have something I need before I leave the valley."

Sorel's eyes move once — a quick inventory: your face, Memory's shaft against the door-frame, the faint crow-call from the roof ridge where Huginn has settled. Then they come back to you, and the wariness softens into something more complicated. Not relief. Recognition, maybe, with an edge of decision finally made for them by someone else's hand. They set the nearest rack-rung down and shift the pack off the shoulder — not all the way off, just enough to take the weight. "You're the Ashwarden's man," they say, and it is not quite a question. "Mara's been careful about who she passes names to. If she sent you to me, you're past the first cut."

Aldric makes a small sound — the sound of a man watching two separate threads arrive at the same loom. He sets the fillet knife down on the block and picks up a cloth. Done.

Sorel's gaze settles on you properly now, steady and appraising. The pack is still half-on, half-off — they haven't decided about the weight yet, which is to say they haven't decided how long they're staying. "The Edge District," they say. "That's what you're walking toward. Tell me what you need to find there, and I'll tell you what I know about the road."

turns.player: Wait for Pell — she's two minutes behind on the south lane. Hold the load-bearing conversation until she's in the room. Introduce them, let Sorel take the full picture of who you're traveling with.
turns.summary: Pell arrives; Sorel takes in the full picture and opens the real conversation.
turns.narration:
The wait is shorter than two minutes. You hear Pell's step on the packed earth outside before you see her — a deliberate, unhurried cadence, the kind of walk that doesn't announce itself but doesn't hide either. She comes through the back door the way she moves through her forge: oriented, taking the room in one glance, Memory's shaft touching her hand briefly as she passes it in the door-frame. She reads Aldric, reads Sorel, reads you. Doesn't say anything yet.

You give her the half-beat to settle. "This is Pell Marsh," you say to Sorel. "She's the reason I know what I'm looking for." Not a credential-drop, just true. Pell doesn't react to the introduction except to shift her weight slightly — comfortable with being named, not interested in being performed at.

Sorel takes her in the same way they took you — face, hands, the easy authority of someone who's been inside hard work for a long time. The edge of decision softens another degree. "Millbrook's smith," they say, and it's not a question. The old pass circuit keeps its own register, apparently. They glance between you once, sorting out the geometry of what they're looking at, and something in their posture completes a calculation. The pack comes all the way down now, set against the rack-leg with a quiet thud. "Alright," Sorel says, to both of you. "Edge District. Tell me what kind of person you're trying to find and how long you have."

Aldric picks up his knife — back to the perch row, not leaving, just giving the conversation its rightful owners. The curing fire breathes low and steady. Huginn shifts once on the roof ridge, audible through the thin boards, and doesn't move again.

turns.player: Describe the structural problem precisely — the vault section, the torque signature, the instrument removed from the cradle-recess. Let Sorel understand you need someone who knows pre-Transition hydraulic engineering from the inside, not just a scholar who's read about it.
turns.summary: Silas lays out the full structural case; Sorel names a man through Leth who ran pressurized pre-Transition systems and would recognize the torque signature immediately.
turns.narration:
The explanation takes less than three minutes. You lay it out in order — the way a clean account deserves to be given — and you watch Sorel's face change.

"East-channel main feeder. Buried vault section, one structural room northeast of the operator's station — the wash-room, flagged floor, saddle-mount hooks, cradle-recess at the back wall." You let that sit a breath. "Something was housed in that recess. Tall, iron-sheathed, cylindrical — seated for years, maybe decades, removed cleanly. No drag-scarring. No fracture. Whoever took it knew what they were doing and had time to do it carefully." You pause again, because the next part matters. "When it came out, the load-bearing stress it was managing redistributed. The vault section northeast of the wash-room is failing — not collapsing, surrendering. Twenty-five feet of it, six weeks from now, directly above the east-channel main feeder run."

Sorel has stopped moving entirely. Pack still at the rack-leg. Both hands resting.

"Both valve sleeve failures show identical torque," you continue. "Counterclockwise. Same failure mode, same axis, same stain. One mechanism operating in sequence across the full buried structure — wash-room at the northeast end, valve housings downstream, the east furlong intake at the terminus. The torque signature means it is not degradation. Something is still turning." You meet Sorel's eyes. "I need someone who knows pre-Transition hydraulic engineering from the inside. Not a scholar who has read about pressurized channel systems. Someone who has stood in one while it was running."

The smokehouse holds the silence a moment — salt air, the low creak of the perch rows. Aldric's knife has gone still again. Pell is watching Sorel the way she watches iron that has reached working temperature: present, ready, not rushing it.

Sorel breathes out through the nose — a long, considered exhale — and looks at the middle distance for exactly two seconds. Then: "The Edge District. There is a woman named Leth." The name comes out without deliberation, which means it was already there. "She keeps records the way your Mara keeps ledgers — cross-referenced, annotated, nothing lost. But she is not what you are asking for." A beat. "Leth knows a man. He worked the old infrastructure during the late-Transition years — hands-on, not theoretical. Ran pressurized systems through the Plateau's foundation array before they finished the photonic overlay. He is difficult. Probably will not want to come." Sorel's gaze returns to you, direct and without apology. "But he would know exactly what you are describing from the moment you said counterclockwise torque."

turns.player: Let Pell take the next question — she knows what she needs in a collaborator better than anyone in this room.
turns.summary: Pell takes the next question; Sorel confirms the unnamed engineer improvises but doesn't leave the Edge District easily.
turns.narration:
The room shifts when you step back from it — a small, deliberate thing, and Sorel reads it immediately.

Pell moves a half-step forward. She doesn't look at you to check whether this is right; she already knows it is. When she speaks, her voice carries the same register she uses in the forge when she's explaining a problem to someone who might actually understand it: direct, without condescension, interested in the answer.

"The man you're describing — the one through Leth," she says. "Does he work with what's there, or does he need to bring his own kit?" She lets that breathe for a beat. "I need to know whether we're carrying tools in or finding them on-site. The cradle-recess was built to house something specific. If that instrument is gone, we may be working around its absence rather than replacing it — and that's a different kind of problem."

Sorel straightens a little. It's the question of someone who has actually looked at the site, not hypothesized about it, and they recognize the difference. "He improvises," Sorel says, carefully. "He's run pressurized systems with three components missing and field-cobbled the fourth. He won't need the original piece. He'll need to understand why it was there." A pause. "That's not the hard part with him. The hard part is that he doesn't leave the Edge District easily."

Aldric sets the fillet knife down, flat on the block. He's been quiet long enough that the sound carries weight.

"Then someone has to give him a reason that's bigger than his comfortable arrangements," Aldric says, not unkindly — a man summarizing an account. "Which is what you two are for."

turns.player: Ask what Sorel's own stake is in this — they came back to Millbrook for a reason, and it may intersect with yours.
turns.summary: Sorel names their real reason for returning — Leth wrote about a targeted archive breach, and someone appears to be working through old infrastructure with a list.
turns.narration:
The question lands quietly, the way the right question always does — not as a demand, but as an opening.

Sorel's eyes move once to Aldric, then back. A small tell. Aldric sets his knife down flat again, which means he already knows what's coming and has been waiting to see if it would be named.

"I came back because Leth wrote to me," Sorel says. Not reluctant — just measured, the way a person speaks when they've been deciding for weeks how much to say and have finally decided to say it. "She's been keeping records at the Edge District for three years. Careful ones. And last month she wrote that someone had been through the archive room — not to study. To remove." A pause. "She didn't know what was taken. But she knew the shape of the absence. It was specific. Targeted." Sorel's hands settle on the pack-strap. "Whoever it was knew what they were looking for, the same way whoever pulled your instrument knew what they were taking. That's not coincidence. That's a person with a list."

The room is very still. Aldric hasn't moved. Pell's attention has narrowed — you can see her running the geometry without being asked to.

"I came home because I didn't want to be the only one holding that," Sorel finishes. "And because if someone is moving through the old infrastructure deliberately — and it sounds like they are — then Millbrook needs to know before it needs to know."

turns.player: Let Aldric speak — he and Sorel were mid-conversation when you arrived, and he's been holding something back since. Put the question to him.
turns.summary: Aldric reveals he recognized the engineer's work in the sealed original east-channel bed — the same CCW stain pattern, seen before Silas arrived.
turns.narration:
The question is simple: you look at Aldric.

Not a demand. Just the weight of a man who has been watching another man hold something since before he arrived, and is done pretending otherwise.
