Aldric picks up the fillet knife. Sets it back down. The gesture means nothing and means everything — the nervous tic of someone who has been waiting for exactly this permission.

"Sorel came through three days ago," he says. "Before you. We talked — about the catch, about the water, about the usual." He flattens his palm on the block. "And she mentioned the engineer. Not his name — she doesn't have it, Leth does — but the shape of him. What he'd worked on." A breath. "I didn't know then what you were going to walk in here and tell me. About the vault. About the torque." He looks at Sorel, not an accusation, just an acknowledgment between two people who've been carrying pieces of the same thing in different pockets. "But I recognized him. Not the man. The work."

The smokehouse holds that for a moment. Perch rows above, curing fire below, the thin morning light at the shutters.

"There's a stretch of the old east-channel bed — not the active run, the original course, sealed off before my father worked here — that has the same stain pattern you described. Same CCW scour on the interior lip of the stone housing. I thought it was age. Bad mortar work." He shakes his head once. "It isn't age."

Sorel hasn't moved. They knew some of this; not all of it.

Aldric's eyes come back to you. "Wherever he's been working, he's been working here too. Or someone using his method has."

turns.player: Name the full pattern aloud — engineer, archive breach, sealed channel, pass opening, removed instrument — and see if the room fills in any gaps you haven't closed yet.
turns.summary: Silas names the full pattern aloud; the room assembles it into a single operating system deliberately restarted by someone who moved through the archive first.
turns.narration:
The smokehouse goes quiet when Silas names it all together.

He does it without theater — just the sequence, flat and in order: the sealed channel bed with its CCW stain. The cradle-recess in the wash-room, cleaned out without drag-scarring, the object gone and no indication where. The targeted archive removal Leth recorded — someone who knew the shape of what they were taking. The pass opening, new boot prints on the far side. All of it arriving in the same season, the same months. He doesn't say connected. He doesn't have to.

Sorel is the first to respond, and what she says is quiet: "The letter from Leth was before the pass opened. She didn't mention the pass — she wouldn't have known yet. But she wrote that whoever it was moved fast, knew the room, and left nothing behind except the absence." A pause. "That's not a scholar. That's someone with a list and a destination." She looks at Aldric. "You saw the channel stain before any of this — before Silas, before Sorel's letter reached you. How old did it read?"

Aldric is already answering before she finishes. "Old enough that I filed it as a water-table quirk and forgot it. Thirty, forty years minimum — the stone was colored all the way through, not surface stain." He sets both palms on the block. "But the pattern. I know that pattern now. Whatever left it was running when the channel was sealed, and whoever sealed the channel knew what they were sealing in." He meets Silas's eyes. "Which means the channel seal is part of the design. Not a repair. A lid."

Pell, who has been still through all of it, speaks without looking up from the middle distance she's been working in. "Four sites." She ticks them quietly: "Wash-room operator station. East furlong valve housing. Fen's intake valve. The sealed original channel bed here in the village." Her voice doesn't rise. "That's not a buried structure with a water problem. That's an operating system that someone recently restarted — and the person who restarted it went through the archive first to know how." She looks at Silas. "The boot prints on the far side of the pass. They went out, not in."

Nobody fills the silence after that. Huginn shifts on the roof ridge outside — a single adjustment of weight, audible through the open back door. The curing fire pops once and settles.

The gap that remains: whoever passed through the archive and through the channel system and out the far side of the pass — they had a name for what they were doing. Nobody in this room has it yet. Leth does, or Leth has the thread that leads to it.

turns.player: Name the departure plainly: you've closed everything Millbrook can give you. Say your farewells to Aldric and Sorel and move north toward the pass.
turns.summary: Silas and Pell depart Millbrook with brief clean farewells to Aldric and Sorel — Huginn already flying north.
turns.narration:
The farewell is brief because it has to be. Everything that needed saying has been said, and the smokehouse has the particular stillness of a room that has finished its work.

Silas picks up Memory from the door-frame, settles the butt against his palm, and looks at Aldric first. "Watch the downstream end," he says. "Anything that changes in the count, anything that tastes wrong — tell Dara." Aldric nods once, both palms still flat on the block. He doesn't offer a hand; he offers the nod, which is the same thing from him. "Come back with someone who knows what they're looking at," he says. "We'll still be here."

Sorel gets a different farewell — Silas pauses at the rack-leg, near where her pack sits, and says it plainly: "If you're writing back to Leth before we arrive, tell her what we're coming for. The engineer's name, and what he'd need to know to say yes." Sorel tilts her head, the small movement of someone filing a task. "I'll write tonight." A beat, then: "Whoever it was that went through the archive — they're ahead of you on the road. That's not a comfortable shape." "No," Silas agrees, and that's the whole of it.

Pell is already through the back door. He follows. Behind them, Huginn drops from the roof ridge with one heavy wingbeat and angles north without waiting to be told — already oriented, already moving, as if the bird has known the destination since before any of them named it. The morning air outside the smokehouse is cold and clean, thin woodsmoke from the cookhouse mixing with the smell of the river, and the north gate is fifty yards ahead through the lane. Whatever is waiting on the far side of the pass has been waiting long enough.

turns.player: Look at Pell's kit and gear.  Assess if she has a hammer for a weapon, or other weapons.  Ask her if needed.
turns.summary: Silas assesses Pell's kit on the lane — hammer, knife, no spear — as a squall pushes in off the ridge and the north gate waits fifty yards ahead.
turns.narration:
The lane is narrow and cold, fifty yards of tramped earth between the smokehouse and the north gate. Pell is a step ahead, as she has been since the back door swung shut — not waiting, not not-waiting, just already aimed at where they are going.

You lengthen your stride to close the gap and take stock of her as she walks. The pack on her shoulders is a forge-worker's pack: compact, no wasted volume, straps worn to the shape of her. A heavy oilcloth roll is lashed horizontally across the top — shelter first, everything else after. At her left hip, on a plain loop of riveted leather, hangs a short-handled hammer with a cross-peen head, the face darkened from use. Not a weapon in the way Memory is a weapon — but in the hand of someone who drives iron for a living, the distinction is largely academic. A belt knife rides her right side, bigger than yours, heavier, the kind that can split kindling or dress a carcass depending on the morning. No spear. No bow that you can see.

Above you, a cold squall moves in off the ridge without warning — not rain exactly, more the threat of it, a curtain of grey pressing down and cutting the early light to something flat and thin. The temperature drops three degrees in a breath. Pell glances up without slowing, then pulls a length of oilcloth from the top of her pack and drapes it across her shoulders in a single practiced motion. She doesn't stop.

"Cross-peen and the knife," she says, before you ask — she caught you looking. "The hammer has reach if something comes in low. The knife is for everything else." She resettles the pack's left strap. "You're asking because you want to know if I need covering, or because you're the kind of man who needs to know where all the sharp things are before the gate." A beat. "Either is fine."

turns.player: Laugh low as you run up and huddle under the oilcloth with her... tell her both.  And make sure she knows you know she's more than capable... perhaps we could even spar a bit along the way?
turns.summary: Silas and Pell huddle under oilcloth at the north gate as the squall hits — she hears 'both', receives it, and answers his spar proposal with 'terms?'
turns.narration:
The squall hits the lane properly just as you close the last ten yards — not a hard rain, but the kind that means it, cold needles driving sideways off the ridge. You break into a short run and duck under the oilcloth Pell has already shrugged half off her shoulders and canted outward, making room without being asked. The two of you press together under it, her shoulder against yours, the cloth pulling tight across both backs as the rain hisses on the dry-stone wall ahead.

You laugh — low and genuine, the sound of someone who is exactly where they expected to end up. "Both," you say. "First: yes, the hammer is a weapon. Second: I already knew that." She turns her head just enough to look at you sidelong, the corner of her mouth doing something. "You assessed me walking," she says. It is not a question. "I did," you say, and let it sit there. The rain picks up a notch. Huginn has found the top of the gate lintel and is hunched into itself, watching you both with the tolerant disapproval of something that chose to fly ahead and is now being rained on for the decision.

"You're more than capable," you say, and mean it plainly — not a reassurance shaped like flattery, just the thing you observed on a scrub oak ridge with a bull boar and a spear. She receives it the same way she receives most things from you now: a short, deliberate pause, then a small nod that costs her something. The pause is the tell. You've learned to read it. "I know," she says, which is true, and somehow also not the whole of what she meant.

You wait a beat, watching the gate. The squall gusts and the oilcloth snaps once. "Spar along the way?" you ask. "No spear," you add, before she can say it. She looks at you properly now — direct and appraising, the same look she gave Memory before she picked it up. "Terms?" she says. Which is not no.

turns.player: Name terms for the spar — distance, first tap, or something stranger. Let her counter.
turns.summary: Spar terms agreed at the north gate — first tap, flat ground, read your thinking after.
turns.narration:
The rain makes the oilcloth drum. Huginn shifts his weight on the lintel above you both and makes a sound that is not quite a word.

"First tap," you say. "Clean touch, any surface, any tool — hammer, hand, spear-butt, elbow if you're feeling creative. No strikes meant to land hard. We stop when one of us names it." You tilt your head toward Memory, butt-down in the mud beside your boot. "I use the spear. You use whatever you've got." A beat. "And we go when the road gives us room — not in a mud-lane, not on a slope. Somewhere flat and dry enough to move on."

Pell listens with her eyes on the gate gap and the grey country beyond it. The rain is still hissing off the stone. When you finish she's quiet for two counts, which from her is not hesitation — it's the sound of someone checking the numbers.

"One condition," she says. "When I tap you, you tell me what you were doing when I got through. Not what went wrong — what you were thinking. I want to know how your head works when it's moving." She glances sidelong. "Same offer if you land one on me, if you're interested." The oilcloth pulls as she shifts her weight forward — not stepping out yet, just aiming. "Flat ground, road gives room, first tap. Done."

Huginn drops off the lintel and angles north into the grey, already picking a line.

turns.player: Accept, but add your own counter-condition: she tells you one thing she noticed about your kit or your stance before you've sparred once. Then walk.
turns.summary: Silas names his counter-condition; Pell reads his stance plainly — weight-back, butt-grounded, watching — then walks north without ceremony.
turns.narration:
The rain hasn't let up, but neither of you were going to wait for it.

"Accepted," you say, "with one addition." You shift Memory to your left hand and look at her sidelong under the oilcloth. "Before the first spar — you tell me one thing you've already clocked about my kit or my stance. Same terms: honest read, not flattery." You hold her gaze for a moment. "Then we walk."

Pell's mouth doesn't quite do anything, but there's a consideration in it — the look of someone checking whether a fair trade is actually fair. She decides it is. "Your weight sits back," she says, without preamble. "Not bad weight — you've got a ready-stance built in, like you expect something to come at you and you want a half-second to read it before you commit. The spear's butt is your tell. You've got it grounded, not canted. You're watching the road, not the gap." A short pause. "It's good technique. It's also the kind of thing that costs you a step when the first move is *yours* to take."

Then she walks. Just like that — one step out from under the oilcloth and into the grey, pulling it back around her shoulders in a single practiced motion, the squall taking her hair sideways. Huginn drops off the lintel and beats north ahead of her, already picking the line through the wet meadow grass.

You fall in beside her. The north gate falls behind. The road opens up — muddy where the winter run-off crossed it, firmer where the old stone base holds — and ahead the upper meadow spreads gray-green and cold under a sky that hasn't decided what it wants to do yet. Somewhere out in that weather, ahead of you both on the road to the pass, is a person with a list and a head start.

turns.player: Walk and talk - "It's also the kind of thing that costs you a step when the first move is *yours* to take." ... I"m pretty sure I made the first move Pell. :)  What did you think of the spear work we did in the forge?
turns.summary: Silas and Pell walk north through the thinning squall — she reads standing weight versus moving weight, and names the oblique mark plainly.
turns.narration:
The squall is thinning as you clear the north gate — still cold, still sideways, but the worst of it has moved east. The old stone road rises gently through wet grass, puddles sitting in the wheel-ruts, and Huginn is already twenty yards ahead, beating low over the meadow in that unhurried way that means he has already decided where he is going.

You fall into step beside Pell, close enough to share pace, and let the silence hold for a beat before you say it. "It's also the kind of thing that costs you a step when the first move is yours to take." A pause — measured, deliberate. "I'm pretty sure I made the first move, Pell." You say it without looking at her, eyes on the wet road ahead, just enough amusement in your voice to make it land where you mean it to.

She doesn't answer immediately. The road turns a little northwest, the meadow grass bending in a long shiver as the wind passes through it. Then: "That's a different weight." Dry, thoughtful — not deflecting. "Standing weight and moving weight are two reads. The spear-butt tells me what you do when someone else starts the clock." She glances at Memory in your hand. Which brings her to the second thing.

"The forge work was clean." She says it the way she says most things — flat first, the real content trailing close behind. "The transverse channel held what you put through it. Capacitor seated better than I expected for rough-install, given what you had to work with." A few more paces, the rain now a fine mist, the upper meadow opening ahead, the scrub line visible to the north. "The oblique on the maker's mark was me not wanting to claim a line I hadn't earned." She says it once, without ceremony, as if naming a measurement. "I've been thinking about whether I'd do it differently. I don't think I would."

Huginn lands on a fence-post thirty yards ahead and waits, watching them both with that too-calm eye.

turns.player: Ask her about what she thinks she hadn't earned, then as about what she felt when the collar rang when we both touched it.
turns.summary: Pell names the oblique mark as accuracy, not modesty — and admits the collar rang like being named to something she isn't saying aloud.
turns.narration:
The road flattens for a stretch between two fence-lines, the wet grass pressing close on either side, and you let the question come out plainly. "What do you think you hadn't earned?" Not accusatory — genuine. The oblique mark is sitting between you like a stone neither of you has stepped over yet.

Pell walks three paces before she answers. The oilcloth shifts on her shoulders. "A maker's mark is a claim," she says. "You cut it into your own work and you're saying: this is mine, I know what I'm doing, hold me to it." Her eyes stay on the road ahead. "I'd been at the forge two years. I knew my way around the pit, I could set a weld, I could read a heat. But I didn't know the limits of what I didn't know yet." A pause, long enough that Huginn has time to drop from the air to a fence-post and back up again. "The oblique meant I wasn't making the full claim. It's not modesty. It's not — " she stops, and starts again more cleanly. "It's accuracy. I'd rather cut the mark wrong and be honest about it than cut it straight and be lying."

You let that settle for a moment, then: "And the collar. When we both had our hands on it and it rang."

She is quiet longer this time. The mist is thinning ahead, and you can see the treeline rising against the grey-white sky. When Pell speaks again, her voice has dropped a register — not softer, just less managed. "I didn't expect it." She glances at you, just briefly, and looks away again. "I've set bindings before. I know what a clean seat sounds like when the rune takes. That was not that." Her jaw shifts. "It felt like — like being named to something. Not the spear. Not the repair." She doesn't finish the sentence. Huginn croaks once from the fence-post twenty yards ahead and she looks at him flatly. "Your bird has bad timing."

"He always does," you say. But you heard the unfinished sentence, and so did she, and neither of you pretend otherwise.

turns.player: Finish her sentence for her — name what you think she was going to say.
turns.summary: Silas finishes Pell's sentence — a collar ring like being named to something she hadn't started; she receives it and walks with it open.
turns.narration:
The road is quiet for another twenty paces before you say it.

"Being named to something you weren't ready to put a word to yet." You don't look at her. The wet fence-posts move past on both sides, and Huginn rides the grey air ahead without a wingbeat. "Not belonging to it, not claiming it — just recognized by it. The way the collar rang wasn't a confirmation. It was an introduction."

Pell doesn't answer immediately. Her boots find the dry edge of a rut and she stays there, and you can feel her deciding whether to let you be right or to correct you on a technicality. She does neither. "Something like that," she says at last, and her voice has the texture of a thing set down carefully. "I've had work ring before. A weld that seated true, a temper that hit the mark. Those ring like — like finishing a sentence correctly." A pause. "This was different. It rang like a sentence I hadn't started."

Huginn drops to a fence-post thirty yards ahead and faces back toward you both. Watching. The mist is thinning enough now that you can see the treeline properly — dark and wet, a quarter mile north, and the pale smear of the road running into it.

She doesn't finish the thought further. But she doesn't close it either — just walks with it open between you, which is its own kind of answer.

turns.player: Call the spar now that conditions allow - if you win she tells you fully what she hasn't said.
turns.summary: Silas wins the spar and Pell names her read: Sorel knows more than she said.
turns.narration:
Flat ground called; spar run; first tap landed; Pell names what she's been holding — Sorel may have the engineer's name and is filing it as 'reluctant contact' rather than witness.

*You feel it settle into you — a new steadiness. (Level 8: +1 attunement, +5 reserves, and growth to choose — a sub-attribute point and a skill point.)*

turns.player: Back up — the spar was physical, play it out properly, then we continue north toward Fen's intake.
turns.summary: Spar concluded — Pell wins on first tap (moving weight); names her read on Sorel; both turn north toward Fen's intake.
turns.narration:
The ground under your boots is still wet from the squall, and you give it a beat — Memory's butt comes up off the stone, weight shifts forward, stance opens. Pell reads it the moment it happens. She's already moving.

She comes in low and lateral, not at the spear — at the space beside the spear. Standing weight was your tell and she's corrected for it, keeping her feet under her, not committing. You get the feint you expected, step off-line, bring Memory's shaft across at belt height — not the blade end, the grip — and she catches it on her forearm, rolls with it, and that half-step back you read as her preparing is actually her loading. First tap: her knuckles, flat and quick, on the back of your leading hand. No force. The point.

She takes a breath. You take one. Huginn watches from the east fence-post with the expression it always has, which is to say the expression of a bird that has seen everything and found it slightly slower than expected.

Pell exhales through her nose and steps back to road-distance. "Moving weight," she says, as though naming the mechanism closes it. "I had your standing tell. Hadn't fully counted for the difference." She doesn't look embarrassed — she looks like someone who has updated a file. The oilcloth goes back on her shoulder. "Right then. Sorel has the name. I'd put cord on it. She's filing him as a reluctant contact because that way the shape of what he knows stays at one remove from her." A short pause. "That's my read. I could be wrong." She clearly doesn't think she's wrong.

Then she turns north, the road still flat between the fence-lines, Fen's intake twenty minutes ahead. "Come on," she says, without waiting. Huginn lifts from the post with one soundless wingbeat and angles north over the wet grass, and the morning opens ahead of you — early and clear and smelling of cold stone and river.

turns.player: Tell her, next time we spar it will be wrestling.  and pick up the pace to cover ground.
turns.summary: Silas sets the next spar terms — wrestling — and both pick up pace north toward Fen's intake.
turns.narration:
The road north opens out where the fence-lines part for a drainage channel, and you lengthen your stride into the gap — Memory's shaft angled back over your shoulder, the wet grass parting at your boots. Huginn adjusts without looking back, climbing a few feet and pulling ahead.

"Next time it's wrestling," you say, not breaking pace. "No weapon tells to read."

Pell makes a sound that is almost a laugh — not quite. She matches your stride without comment, oilcloth shifting on her shoulder, and you can feel her filing it: another piece of the picture she's been building since the spar. "That's a different problem for me than it is for you," she says after a moment, and there's something dry in it, not a complaint. "You've got reach and you lead with it. Wrestling closes that." A beat. "So yes. Obviously."

The squall has left the road shining and cold, and the terraced gardens of Millbrook have dropped out of sight behind the curve of the hillside. Up here the valley shows itself: ridgeline to the north, the interference shimmer at the far edge of your sight where the Heights and the Plateau argue across open air. Huginn's shadow crosses a fence-post and keeps going. The ground rises slightly, and twenty minutes of ground is still twenty minutes, but you're eating it now at a pace that shortens it.

Fen's intake will be in sight when the road bends east.

turns.player: Yes, obviously.. .just like the race...If I lose I've already won.  Who wouldn't want to wrestle you covered in oil naked... shadow step well ahead our of retaliation range and laugh.
turns.summary: Silas shadowsteps ahead with a naked-wrestling taunt; Pell contains herself with visible effort.
turns.narration:
You say it without breaking stride — *'Just like the race. If I lose, I've already won. Who wouldn't want to wrestle you covered in oil, naked?'* — and then you step.

The shadow of the fence-post to your left and the shadow of the drainage ditch cover thirty feet ahead of you in one quiet fold. You land it clean, weight forward, already walking, Memory's shaft dropping back to road-angle like nothing interrupted. The step costs you barely a breath.

Behind you, a beat of silence. Then: "*Silas.*" Flat, measured, with a quality that is doing a great deal of work to stay flat. Huginn banks left overhead without comment, which is somehow worse.

You glance back. Pell has not broken stride. Her face is doing something she is visibly not letting it finish. She catches you looking and points — one finger, unhurried — at the road ahead. "*Walk.*"

turns.player: Call back something mild — 'I'm walking' — and keep your eyes on the road ahead.
turns.summary: Silas calls back 'I'm walking' and both continue north; Pell comes even and acknowledges the wrestling terms with dry composure.
turns.narration:
The road runs straight here between its fence-lines, and you keep your eyes on it.

"I'm walking," you call back, no particular inflection. Memory's shaft catches the morning light over your shoulder. Huginn has pulled thirty yards ahead and is already angling toward the road-bend as if he has somewhere to be.

Behind you, the sound of Pell's boots on wet stone maintains exactly the same cadence it had before. She doesn't answer. That is, in its own way, an answer.

The fence-lines thin where the terraced gardens give out to rougher grazing, and the road rises slightly — enough to feel in the backs of your calves after a morning's worth of ground. The valley ridgeline ahead is pale with low cloud that hasn't decided yet whether it's fog or weather. Somewhere above the scrub on the eastern slope, a pair of wood-pigeons settle into an argument neither of them seems likely to win.

Pell comes even with you without ceremony, oilcloth swinging back to her off-shoulder, and says nothing for two or three minutes. Then: "The wrestling is fine." She says it the way she says most things — like she's delivering a measurement. "I just want it on record that I'm aware of what you're doing." She doesn't look at you. The road-bend around the eastern flank is coming into view ahead, and Fen's intake will be around it.

turns.player: "What am I doing, then? Say it plainly."
turns.summary: Pell names plainly what Silas is doing; Pip Cotter arrives on the road north with a package from Mara.
turns.narration:
The road bends east ahead of you, and for a moment there is nothing between you and the valley ridgeline except the pale sky and Huginn's wings angling into the curve.

Pell comes even with you. She doesn't look at you right away — she's watching the bend, the way Huginn is dropping toward something on the far side of it. Then: "You're walking north with a blacksmith you kissed twice and a bird that attends endings, toward a valve site that's part of a buried pre-Transition system someone deliberately restarted." A beat. "Carrying a spear you named, that I can feel from twenty feet away when I'm not looking at it." Another beat — shorter. "And you're going to leave the valley to find a structural expert before the sixth week, because if you don't, the east channel fails and we lose the growing season." She glances at you sideways, measuring, the same look she gives a piece of work she's not yet done assessing. "That's what you're doing."

Huginn crests the road-bend and drops out of sight. Somewhere past it, the sound of water moving — Fen's intake, the first valve on the timeline, World-day minus-eighteen.

Then something moves on the road that is not Huginn. A figure coming from the direction of the bend, moving fast — not running, but the walk of someone sent with a purpose and not stopping to admire the morning. Small, compact. Familiar. Pip Cotter, Mara's errand-runner, with a cloth-wrapped package tucked under one arm and both hands swinging free for speed. The child spots you at thirty yards and angles straight at you without slowing.

turns.player: You forgot something on the list of what I'm doing... just before Pip comes into earshot say... "you".
turns.summary: Silas names Pell on the list — one word, forward-facing, before Pip arrives.
turns.narration:
Pip is still thirty yards back, boots quick on the wet stone, the cloth-wrapped package tucked under one arm — close enough now that you could shout and be heard.

You don't shout. You say it quietly, forward-facing, to the road and the pale ridgeline and the bend where Huginn disappeared.

"You."

One word. The fence-lines don't move. The low cloud doesn't shift. Pell's stride doesn't break — but there is a half-beat where it almost does, a catch in the rhythm that she recovers so cleanly you might have imagined it. She doesn't look at you. Her jaw moves once, not quite language, and then she is watching the bend again, composed and level, the oilcloth on her shoulder.

Pip's boots are audible now. Thirty yards has become twenty.

turns.player: Turn and receive Pip — take the package and see what Mara sent.
turns.summary: Pip delivers Mara's package — a note about a waterskin at Aldric's and a vial of pre-Transition water-purification tabs.
turns.narration:
Pip Cotter arrives at a dead stop, breathing hard, the cloth-wrapped package thrust out at arm's length with the unsentimental efficiency of someone who has run this errand a hundred times and is already thinking about the next one.

"Mara says you'd want it before the bend," Pip says, chest still working. That's the whole message. The child's eyes slide to Memory over your shoulder — a half-second assessment, not fear, just the same unhurried attention children give to things adults look away from — and then back to you, waiting to be dismissed.

You take the package. It's about the weight of a folded letter and something hard wrapped inside — cloth double-knotted, Mara's initials pressed into the knot with a thumbnail. When you work it open, there are two things: a short note in Mara's close hand, and a small stoppered vial of dark glass, sealed with the same wax she uses on her ledger marks.

The note reads: *Aldric remembered after you left — cured bladder waterskin in the back of the store, set aside, yours if you want it before the road gets long. Second thing: the vial is from my own stock. River-purification tabs, pre-Transition provenance, a dozen left in the world maybe. Drop one in a waterskin if the water gets bad. Don't ask where I got them. — M.*

Pell has read it over your shoulder without apology. She says nothing, but her expression shifts slightly — something between impressed and unsettled at the provenance line.

turns.player: Thank pip and continue on the journey. Talk to Huginn along the way (about Pell), given what Huginn is, make sure to talk about all the endings pell has in front of her now that she's with you going steady... like no more wandering tinkers..
turns.summary: Silas thanks Pip, rounds the bend to Huginn, and asks the bird plainly about Pell's futures — Huginn answers in its own register: stillness, not endings.
turns.narration:
You dismiss Pip with a nod and a word — "Tell Mara it's good" — and the child is already turning south before you finish, boots quick and purposeful on the wet stone. You tuck the vial of purification tabs into the inner pocket of your pack and round the bend.

Huginn is waiting on a fence-post at the edge of the road, watching the intake. The bird doesn't move when you approach, just tracks you with the one calm eye. The intake itself is audible before it's visible — water over stone, the faint mineral edge that wasn't there a month ago. You stop short of the mechanism and speak quietly, because the valley is still and carrying sound this morning.

"She's committed now. You'd have seen that." Huginn tilts its head. You keep your voice down, not from hiding — Pell is a few strides back, letting you have this — but because this is the kind of accounting that doesn't need volume. "The tinkers won't stop at Millbrook the same way. She'll have a reason to watch the pass now. The water crisis has her name on it whether she wants it or not. And she's carrying a spear she can feel from twenty feet away." You look at the bird. "Tell me what you see."

Huginn is quiet for a beat. Then it makes a sound — not the croak it usually produces, something shorter and more deliberate, the sound a stone makes settling into place. It looks away from you and back at Pell, who has stopped just inside the bend with her oilcloth and her level face and her hands in her pockets. The bird turns back to you and says nothing. But it doesn't shift its weight, doesn't orient away, doesn't do the thing it does when it's tracking an ending on the horizon. It simply watches. You understand: the endings it sees for her are hers, not imminent, and it isn't going to read them for you out of kindness or otherwise. What it gives you instead is its stillness — the same stillness it brings to things that are not yet concluding.

That is, from Huginn, considerable reassurance.

turns.player: Ask Pell what she heard of the Huginn conversation — how much did she catch, and what does she make of the bird watching her like that.
turns.summary: Pell reports what she caught of the Huginn exchange — word 'tinkers', asking-tone, the bird's decided look — and names it plainly.
turns.narration:
The intake is still ahead — water running, valve housing visible in the low morning light. You fall back a half-step to walk even with Pell and ask it plainly: how much did she catch?

She considers this with the same care she gives a measurement she is not certain of. "The word 'tinkers'," she says finally. "And your tone. The tone was — " a pause, selecting — "not explaining something to the bird. Asking it." She glances sideways at you once, then back at the road. "I've seen people talk to animals before. They're either performing or they've lost something. That wasn't either of those."

A beat. The intake grows louder.

"The bird looked at me after," Pell says, and this comes out more carefully than the first part, like she is reporting a thing she would rather not have noticed. "Not the way a bird tracks movement. The way you look at something you've already decided about." She does not follow that up with a question or a dismissal. She just names it and walks with it, the oilcloth still on her shoulder, her hands back in her pockets. The fence-post where Huginn was perched is already behind them; the bird has moved ahead to the intake's stone sill, where it sits with the same unhurried calm it always carries.

Huginn does not look back at either of you. It is watching the valve housing.

turns.player: Tell her what you asked — and what answer you got. Huginn's stillness was the answer: she is not concluding.
turns.summary: Silas tells Pell what Huginn's stillness meant — she's not foreclosed, not concluding. They arrive at Fen's intake together.
turns.narration:
The road levels briefly here, wheel-ruts flattening into packed earth before the next rise, and you tell her.

"I asked it about your futures," you say, keeping your eyes on the intake ahead. "The wandering tinker work. The pass. What ends when you commit to something." A pause. "Huginn reads endings — that's what it does, what it *is*. So I asked it plainly what it saw ahead for you."

Pell is quiet for a moment. The water sound fills the gap.

"And?"

"Nothing." You let that land before you add: "Stillness. From Huginn, that's the answer — not that it can't see, but that there's nothing concluding. You're not foreclosed. The tinker road isn't ending, it's just — not the direction you're facing." You glance at her then. "It doesn't lie. It doesn't soften things either. Stillness means what it means."

Pell takes that in the way she takes a measurement she wasn't expecting — not resisting it, just letting it settle into the right place. Her hands stay in her pockets. After a moment she says, "So it's watching me because I'm interesting to it. Not because something's ending."

"I think so."

"Hm." That's all she gives it, but her shoulders have dropped a fraction, and the set of her jaw is different — not relieved exactly, but no longer holding against something. The intake is close now, the valve housing dark stone above the waterline, and Huginn is still perched on the sill exactly where you left it, watching you both arrive with its one unhurried eye.

turns.player: Use The Attended End on the intake structure — let it tell you plainly what is ending here and how long it has.
turns.summary: The Attended End reads Fen's intake — the iron sleeve has three to four weeks before it runs open; the structure behind it is not ending.
turns.narration:
You set Memory's butt against the packed earth and close your eyes for a moment. The Attended End doesn't need ceremony — it just needs you to stop moving and let Marrow speak.

The intake tells you what it's ending. Not dramatically: the iron sleeve in the housing is surrendering its grip where the stone expanded and the fitting didn't. Three weeks, maybe four, before the channel runs open. Not a failure — a loosening, the way a tooth goes before it goes. The mechanism behind it, the buried thing pressing its intention through this valve, isn't ending at all. It has more work to do.

Huginn shifts on the stone sill. One slow blink.

You open your eyes and look at the housing — the dark stain at the lip, the faint glaze on the outlet stone where the mineral edge has been coming through longest. "Three to four weeks," you say, quiet enough that it's to Pell and not to the valley. "The sleeve. Not the structure."

Pell is already crouching at the base of the housing, one hand near the stone but not on it, reading the stain the same way she read the torque signature at the east furlong. She doesn't look up. "Same axis?" A practical question, the kind she asks when she's already forming an answer and wants confirmation, not discovery.

turns.player: Confirm Pell's question — same counterclockwise torque axis, same stain pattern as the east furlong. Walk her through what The Attended End found.
turns.summary: Silas confirms the CCW torque axis at Fen's intake — same as east furlong — and tells Pell what The Attended End found: three to four weeks on the sleeve, the structure not ending.
turns.narration:
You crouch beside her at the housing base and point to the lip — the stain's inner arc, the way it curves left and then under, the same clockwise-read-from-outside that means counterclockwise at the shaft. Same axis. You trace it with one finger without touching it, the way you'd trace a word you already know.

"Same axis, same orientation," you say. "East furlong runs the same way. The torque isn't from water pressure or sediment — it's from the mechanism. Something turning, consistently, in one direction, over a long time." You let her have the image for a moment before you add the rest. "The Attended End says the sleeve has three to four weeks before it runs open. Not a collapse — more like a tooth going loose. The thread of failure is already started."

Pell's hand is still near the stone. She doesn't pull it back. "And the thing behind it?"

"Not ending," you say. "It has more work to do."

She absorbs this with the flat steadiness she uses when a measurement lands somewhere she didn't want it to. Then she straightens, wipes her palm on her thigh, and looks at the full face of the housing — valve to lintel, the mineral glaze on the outlet stone, the iron sleeve dark with age. "So this isn't a symptom that got away from it," she says. "This is it doing what it was made to do." A beat. "We need to know what the whole design looks like from here. If Order-Sense can read a room's intent from a cradle-recess, I want to know what it reads from a valve that's been running on purpose for forty years."

turns.player: Run Order-Sense on the intake housing — read the whole design this valve is part of
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads Fen's intake as the southernmost of three staged gates in a linear release sequence running toward the buried vault head.
turns.narration:
You press two fingers against the housing lip — not the stain, just the stone beside it — and let Order-Sense go thin and still. Conserve, not push. The lattice here is starved enough that you don't want to spend what you can't afford.

The design comes in pieces rather than all at once, the way a map reveals itself room by room when you hold a lamp close. The valve housing is a joint, not a terminus — it belongs to a linear sequence running southwest to northeast, the same axis you already know from the wash-room and the east furlong. Three pressure points distributed along a single run, each one a staged gate in a system that was never meant to hold water out. It was meant to hold it *back* until the right time. The intake sleeve here is the southernmost gate. The east furlong was the middle. Whatever sits northeast of the wash-room — the load-bearing section marked by the settling-seam — is the head.

You get the shape and you get the sequence, but the substrate thins before you can read the intent behind the sequencing. Why three gates, why this interval, what opens when the last sleeve surrenders — that stays dark. The design is legible. Its purpose remains its own.

You lift your fingers and straighten. Pell hasn't moved. Huginn is still on the sill, watching the water.

"Three gates," you say. "This is the southernmost. The east furlong was the middle. The head is northeast — up near the settling-seam we marked." You tap the housing once, lightly. "The structure isn't defending against the river. It's staged release. Something was built to open in sequence, south to north, and it's doing exactly that."

turns.player: note the findings the ask If Pell is ready to push out of the valley - if she is - we hike for as long as it takes til mid day to stop and have a meal
turns.summary: Silas notes the findings, proposes pushing out of the valley — Pell is ready without hesitation.
turns.narration:
You pull your fingers back from the housing stone and straighten, brushing grit from your palm. The Order-Sense reading is already settling into place alongside everything else — southernmost gate, three-stage sequence, the head sitting somewhere northeast in the hillside dark. You take out the Valve Failure Timeline note and add two words in the margin in a tight hand: *S-gate confirmed*. Then you fold it back into your pocket and look at Pell.

She's still crouched at the base of the housing, one hand resting on her knee, studying the stain arc with the focused quiet of someone who has just been handed the last piece of something and wants to make sure she's holding it right. Huginn drops from the stone sill to the packed earth nearby without ceremony and stands there, which is as much as it ever announces a departure.

"Everything we need from this site, we have," you say. "Three gates, linear sequence, head northeast. The sleeve here runs three to four weeks — the east furlong is already further along. The vault is the only thing left to read, and that means walking." You rest Memory's butt on the earth and give her the rest of it plainly. "I want to push out of the valley today. However long it takes, wherever we make it — we walk until midday, stop and eat properly, then see where the afternoon puts us. If you're ready."

Pell rises from her crouch in one clean motion and settles the oilcloth on her shoulder. She looks at the intake housing once more — a check, not a hesitation — and then looks at you. "I didn't walk up here to turn back at the valve," she says. It is not drama. It is just the shape of her answer. She sets her jaw toward the northeast, where the hillside rises and the road narrows to a track, and waits for you to move first.

turns.player: Before leaving: run The Attended End one more time, facing northeast up the hillside — read what is ending in that direction and how far
turns.summary: The Attended End reads northeast — the vault head is ending, slow and structural, a mile and a half up the hillside line.
turns.narration:
You turn northeast before you take the first step on the road, Memory's shaft grounded against the packed earth, and let The Attended End open quietly — not a push, just a listening. Conserve. The craft comes in like breath, not like a hammer.

What it finds is distant but plain. Something is ending up there in the hillside dark, northeast and elevated — slow, structural, the particular quality of a load surrendering rather than breaking. The six-week window you already know from the wash-room sits on it like a label. What's new is the direction: not vague northeast, but a bearing you can feel — slightly above the road grade, perhaps a mile and a half, the same line the three-gate sequence draws. The vault head is ending. Everything below it is not.

There's one other thing, fainter and harder to name. Something smaller is concluding in that direction too — not structural, not the buried system. Animal, maybe, or a stand of something old. It's not urgent and it's not yours; The Attended End offers it the way a lamp shows a far wall without meaning to light it.

You lift Memory and settle it on your shoulder. Pell is already watching you. "The head is where I thought," you say, and that's enough — she nods and turns northeast, oilcloth shifting on her shoulder. Huginn lifts from the packed earth without ceremony and catches the low air ahead of you both.

*✦ Huginn has changed — The Told Truth.*

turns.player: Ok - we head out of the valley through the pass - long travel.
turns.summary: Silas and Pell cross the mountain pass, leaving the Valley of Echoes behind on World-day 16.
turns.narration:
Huginn clears the intake fence-post without looking back, and that is enough. You shoulder Memory and fall into step behind Pell, who has already set her oilcloth against the wind-side and is walking like someone who has decided.

The road northeast is old packed earth for the first quarter-mile, then it loses confidence — becomes a game trail, then a line of crushed sedge where boots and hooves have been pressing the same argument into the hillside for decades. The valley floor drops away behind you faster than you expect. By the time the morning light gets serious, Millbrook is a smear of cookfire smoke below the tree line, and Huginn is a dark speck riding the thermals above the ridge. You pass the two river-pebble markers you set on the settling-seam without slowing — Pell notes them with a look, nothing more. The buried vault head is underneath you for a moment, failing slowly in the dark, and then it is behind you too.

The pass itself is honest about what it is: a notch in the rock where a glacial shoulder wore through, barely wide enough for a loaded cart in the tightest section. The stone is pale limestone with iron veining, and the wind finds every gap in your collar. You eat the midday meal seated on a flat outcrop just below the crest — Preserved Trail Parcel, dried perch and salt pork unwrapped against the cold — with the valley spread out south and the far side of the ridge showing its first slopes to the north. Pell eats without ceremony, tearing the cloth wrap with her thumbnail and watching the descent. The wind tastes different on this side: less mineral, more pine, something open in it that the valley air does not have. Huginn lands nearby, pulls a strip of dried pork from somewhere you did not offer, and arranges itself on the rock with the settled patience of something that has been waiting a long time for this particular view.

By mid-afternoon you are properly into the descent — a loose-scree slope giving way to a sparse pine run, the trail firming again as the elevation drops. The far side of the pass is quieter than the valley: no water-wheel sound, no distant hammer. The ground is damp from snowmelt and the pines are old enough that their canopy blocks the worst of the wind. You made good time. The next decision is ahead of you: push until the light fails, or camp early and solid while the ground is still readable.

turns.player: Use The Attended End facing down the far slope — read what is ending in this direction before you commit to a bearing.
turns.summary: The Attended End reads the far slope — three ordinary endings, nothing made, the pass descent is clear.
turns.narration:
You face downslope and let The Attended End open — not a reach, a lean. Conserve. The craft finds what it finds without being asked twice.

The pine run ahead holds three endings in ordinary motion: a stand of spruce, maybe thirty years old, dying from the root — fungal, years yet before it falls. A deer somewhere below the canopy, old enough that the thread is thin and unhurried. A small stream crossing where a log-bridge has been losing the argument with snowmelt for two seasons running; it has perhaps one more crossing before it stops being a bridge and starts being an obstacle.

Nothing urgent. Nothing made. The endings down this slope are the valley's own work — age, season, ordinary time. Whatever crossed this pass before you left no dying behind it.

Pell has been watching your face. "Clear?" she asks. One word, trail-economical. Huginn shifts on the rock and does not orient toward any horizon. That, too, is an answer.

turns.player: Study the spruce stand's root-death with Palework before descending — read whether the fungal die-off is natural or something older working upward
turns.summary: Palework on the dying spruce stand confirms natural fungal die-off — no connection to the buried structure's contamination.
turns.narration:
You step off the trail into the spruce stand's shadow and crouch at the base of the nearest trunk — bark grey-green with lichen, the lower six inches of wood soft enough to dent with a thumbnail. Palework opens quietly, a flat read rather than a push. You set two fingers against the exposed root-mat and let it tell you what it knows.

The fungal death is its own thing. Armillaria, or something close enough — the mycelium has been threading this root system for two decades, patient and unremarkable, doing exactly what it does. The amber tinge you half-expected to find linking it to the water-table chemistry below is not there. The chemistry that killed these roots is purely biological: neighbor trees, poor drainage from the snowmelt gradient, a stand that grew too tight too fast after a windfall clearance twenty or thirty years ago. Nothing older working upward. The hillside holds its own ordinary rot here, separate from the system behind you.

You pull your fingers back and stand. Huginn has not moved from its rock. Pell is watching you with the particular patience she applies to things she doesn't interrupt — not bored, just waiting for the read.

"Natural," you tell her, brushing bark dust from your fingertips. "The rot up here isn't connected. Whatever's running through the hillside below us stays in the hillside." A small thing to confirm, but the confirmation has weight: the buried structure's contamination hasn't begun seeping into the broader root-zone yet. The valley's watershed is sick from one point, not spreading up from below.

turns.player: Push downhill now — pick up pace and try to reach lower ground and better shelter before the afternoon light goes soft
turns.summary: Silas and Pell descend two miles from the pass crest to a natural rock overhang with an old fire-ring — someone camps here.
turns.narration:
The descent from the outcrop is straightforward once you commit to it — loose scree for the first fifty feet, then the ground firms under pine duff and the slope eases into something you can cover at a real pace. Pell falls in behind you without instruction, reading the terrain the same way you do: weight back on the loose sections, weight forward on the compacted, let the legs absorb rather than fight.

Huginn drops from the rock and glides ahead, threading the canopy without effort, landing periodically on a branch just far enough down the slope that it functions as a waypoint. You've stopped wondering whether this is navigation or amusement. Probably both.

By the time the light begins to flatten and lose its angle, you've covered something like two miles of descent — the pine run thickens around you, the air loses the pass's mineral edge and picks up cold resin and damp needle-fall, and the ground underfoot is genuinely wet now, snowmelt working through the duff to a thin runnel that parallels your line before turning east. Ahead, maybe a hundred yards, the pines thin around a rock shelf that juts from the hillside — a natural overhang, south-facing, the underside dry where the slope sheds water around it. A fire-ring of flat stones sits just inside the overhang's shadow. Old ash, rain-unscattered. Someone camps here, or has.

Pell moves up beside you and looks at the ring without comment for a moment. "Recent?" she says, not alarmed — practical. The fire-ring is the question. The overhang itself is exactly what you need before the light goes.

turns.player: Read the fire-ring with Palework — how old, who made it, what ended here last
turns.summary: Palework reads the fire-ring — one occupant, two to three weeks ago, same hand as an earlier fire. A known waypoint, not a chance camp.
turns.narration:
You crouch at the fire-ring and set two fingers against the flat stones — the ash is dry and cold and faintly gritty, the kind of grey that has shed all its heat. Palework opens at a conserve, a quiet read rather than a push.

The ring is two weeks old, maybe three — late winter, before the pass opened. Single occupant: one set of stone placements, one sitting-angle's worth of char, a meal cooked and eaten without ceremony. Nothing ended here beyond ordinary hunger satisfied and ordinary cold held off. The ash is clean — no blood, no animal remains, no material worked or broken. Whoever this was, they camped the way someone camps who has done it a thousand times: efficiently, without trace, and without event.

The only thing Palework catches that isn't entirely ordinary: the fire was built on the site of an older fire, laid in the same ring, with the same stone placement. Not coincidence. The same hand made both, or someone who learned from that hand. This overhang is known to whoever lit these. It is a waypoint, not a discovery.

You lift your fingers from the stone. Pell is watching you, not the ring.

turns.player: Make camp here — the overhang is dry, the ring is cold, whoever used it isn't here. Build a fire and eat before the light goes.
turns.summary: Camp made at the overhang fire-ring — fire built, trail parcel opened, Pell asks about the ring's interval.
turns.narration:
The overhang is better shelter than anything you'd have found by pushing on — the underside is genuinely dry, the stone above cutting the evening wind that's begun moving through the pines. You drop your pack against the back wall and start breaking the fire the way whoever came before you built theirs: four stones already placed, two knocked askew by weather, corrected by hand in a minute.

The tinder is pine duff and a strip of bark peeled from a dead fall twenty feet into the trees — dry enough. Flint and steel twice, the third strike catches, and you feed it slowly until the small fire settles into something reliable. The flat stones absorb heat and begin to throw it back. Pell doesn't comment; she's already cleared the ground on her side of the ring, set the oilcloth fold across her pack as a low windbreak, and is working at the knot of her first trail parcel. Camp-making with a blacksmith: efficient, no wasted motion, no conversation needed.

You open one of your own Preserved Trail Parcels — dried river perch and salted pork, cloth-wrapped, Aldric's smokehouse smell still on it — and hold the other one against the heat just long enough to take the cold edge off. Above, Huginn has moved from the canopy to a ledge at the overhang's lip, where it can see the fire and the treeline both. It settles its wings once and goes still. The runnel to the east carries the sound of snowmelt over stone, thin and constant, and the pine run darkens by degrees as the afternoon light goes soft and amber behind the ridge.

Pell eats two bites and then asks, without looking up from her parcel: "The ring. Same hand twice — you said that. How long between visits?"

turns.player: Tell her what Palework said then break out the Resin Mountain Wine.  Invite here into rounds of getting to know eachother.
turns.summary: Silas shares the Palework read on the fire-ring and breaks out the Resin Mountain Wine — an evening of getting acquainted begins at the overhang camp.
turns.narration:
You tell her what Palework read: two visits, same hand, three weeks between them. Someone who knows the pass — who knew it before it opened, or who opened it themselves. A waypoint, not a chance camp. The fire-ring is part of a route. You say it plainly, watching the small fire rather than her face, and leave the weight of it in the air where she can pick it up or set it down.

Pell is quiet for a moment. She pulls the last of her trail parcel apart with her fingers, working through the implication with the same patient mechanics she brings to a stain pattern or a valve housing. Then: "Before the pass opened." Not a question — she's filing it.

You reach into the pack and pull out the Resin Mountain Wine — the dark glass, the wax seal still unbroken. You hold it up in the firelight so she can see what it is. "I've been carrying this since the Adaptor settlement. Figured I was waiting for the right fire." You break the wax with your thumb and pass it to her first.

She takes it, tips it, and the smell that comes off the mouth of the bottle is dark and strange — resin and something older, mountain-cold. She drinks, passes it back, and for a while the question of who was here before you sits quietly at the edge of the fire while the two of you settle into the easier work of the evening: names of places, names of people, the small accumulated facts of two lives that have not, until recently, run parallel. Huginn shifts on the stone lip above, tucks a wing, and apparently decides none of this requires its attention.

turns.player: Ask her something personal — not about the work, not about the water. Something you've wanted to know since the forge morning and haven't asked yet
turns.summary: Silas asks about the oblique maker's mark — Pell tells him about her father, the stamp, and the piece that finally said yes.
turns.narration:
The wine has made one circuit. The fire is settled, burning clean and low — orange light on the dry underside of the overhang, pine-dark beyond the ring's edge. Pell has her knees drawn up, boot heels on the flat stones at the fire's perimeter, the bottle cradled in both hands the way she held her mug at Dara's door: both hands, not one, the way people hold things they're not ready to set down.

You look at the fire rather than her face when you ask it. "The maker's mark," you say. "The oblique one. You told me it was accuracy — that you wouldn't claim what you hadn't fully earned." A pause. The fire pops once, quietly. "What were you trying to earn?"

She doesn't answer immediately. You've learned by now that the pause is not reluctance — it's the same thing she does before naming a failure mode: finding the word that's actually true rather than the word that's available. When she does speak her voice is level, but something in it is sitting differently than it does when she's on the work. "I was fourteen when my father handed me the stamp," she says. "He said — " a small breath — "he said, 'don't put your name on a thing until the thing says yes.' I didn't know what that meant. I marked three pieces before I did." She turns the bottle slowly in her hands. "The first two, I can still see them in my head. The fit was right. The line was right. But they didn't say yes." She stops. "The third one did. I was sixteen. I cried, which he pretended not to see." A dry almost-smile at the fire. "The oblique mark was him. He never corrected it. I stopped correcting it the year he died because — " she lifts one shoulder, a small motion — "because it's accurate. It's his angle. I'm still his student, a little. Always will be."

Huginn makes no sound from the stone lip. The runnel thirty feet out keeps its thin note. Pell passes the bottle across the fire to you, and her eyes when they meet yours are open in a way you have not seen her manage in daylight.

turns.player: Sit with it a moment before speaking — let what she said settle, and let her see you actually heard it.
turns.summary: Silas holds silence after Pell's story about her father — she stays open, fire burning low at the overhang camp.
turns.narration:
The fire pops once, a small sound. You don't fill it.

You look at the flame rather than her face — not to avoid her, but because some things land better when you give them the courtesy of not watching them arrive. Her father's angle. The year he died. The fact that she stopped correcting it. You let all of that sit in you the way a stone sits in a palm: present, real, neither held tight nor dropped.

After a moment you look at her. She's still watching the fire, the wine bottle cradled in both hands, and there's something in her posture that is waiting without expecting — not braced for a response, just open to whether one comes.

"I think that's the best kind of mark," you say finally. "The kind that carries something the piece didn't make." A beat. "Not yours. His, and now yours, because you chose to keep it." You mean it plainly. You're not reaching for comfort or offering craft-philosophy — you just mean it, and you think she'll hear the difference.

Huginn shifts on the stone lip, one wing adjusting, then settles back. Its silence tonight has a different texture than its usual dry patience — less like waiting for an ending, more like a thing that is content to be where it is. You notice that, briefly, and file it.

The pines beyond the ring are dark now, the last of the western light gone. The overhang holds its warmth. Pell hasn't answered yet, but she's still looking at the fire the same way — and the bottle is still in both hands.

turns.player: Ask her the thing you've been circling since the forge — not about her father, but about whether she wants this to be what it's starting to feel like
turns.summary: Silas asks plainly whether Pell wants what this is becoming — she says yes, plainly, and passes the wine back.
turns.narration:
The wine has gone around twice. The fire has settled into its last good hour — red-orange coals under a thin layer of ash, heat radiating into the flat stones at the perimeter, the overhang ceiling amber and close above you both.

You don't lead with it. You watch the fire a moment longer, and then you look at her — not at the middle distance, not at the wine, at her — and you say it plainly, the way you've been learning is the only way she actually hears a thing: "I know what this is starting to feel like. I want to know if it feels the same to you. Not the work. Not the road. This."

Pell is quiet. Her thumbs turn the bottle slowly, one full rotation, the same way she turned the valve housing in her hands at the east furlong — not stalling, just making sure she knows what she's holding before she sets it down. Then she looks at you directly, and the open-faced expression from after the forge morning is back, the one she only seems to find when the armor is already off and she's stopped noticing.

"Yes," she says. No qualification. No filed note or patient recalibration. Just that — her voice flat and certain and a little rueful, like she's answering something that's been obvious to her for longer than she'd prefer. She holds your gaze for a moment, then looks back at the fire. "I told you I was managing it badly." The corner of her mouth lifts, brief. She passes the bottle back across the stones between you.

turns.player: Ask what 'managing it badly' looks like to her — let her say more of it, in her own words, on her own terms
turns.summary: Pell names what managing it badly looks like — the ledger that never closes, the note that lasted three seconds.
turns.narration:
The coals breathe orange and low. Huginn shifts once on the stone lip, settles its wing, goes still again.

You pass the wine back to her. "Managing it badly," you say — not as a question, more like picking up the thing she set down and handing it back to her, unhurried. "What does that look like?"

Pell takes the bottle. Her thumb finds the wax seal-ring at the neck the way it always finds something to work at — not fidgeting, just occupying the hands so the rest of her can think. She looks at the fire for a moment. Then: "It looks like knowing what I want and spending a lot of energy making sure it doesn't show." A small exhale through the nose, something between a laugh and an acknowledgment. "Filing things. Noting things. Treating the whole business like a ledger I can close at the end of the day and it won't have changed while I wasn't looking." She turns the bottle once. "It always changes while I wasn't looking."

The wind cuts above the overhang's lip. Inside the ring the heat stays steady. She tips her chin toward the coals rather than at you. "At the forge, when you kissed me — I had a note for that. In my head. A reasonable position." The corner of her mouth. "It lasted about three seconds. Then I just held your wrist and said alright." She passes the wine back across the gap between you, and this time she doesn't let go of it immediately, so for a breath you're both holding the neck of the bottle in the space between you. "That's what managing it badly looks like. The note exists. Then you say something and I forget I wrote it."

lastTurn.narration:
The coals breathe orange and low. Huginn shifts once on the stone lip, settles its wing, goes still again.

You pass the wine back to her. "Managing it badly," you say — not as a question, more like picking up the thing she set down and handing it back to her, unhurried. "What does that look like?"

Pell takes the bottle. Her thumb finds the wax seal-ring at the neck the way it always finds something to work at — not fidgeting, just occupying the hands so the rest of her can think. She looks at the fire for a moment. Then: "It looks like knowing what I want and spending a lot of energy making sure it doesn't show." A small exhale through the nose, something between a laugh and an acknowledgment. "Filing things. Noting things. Treating the whole business like a ledger I can close at the end of the day and it won't have changed while I wasn't looking." She turns the bottle once. "It always changes while I wasn't looking."

The wind cuts above the overhang's lip. Inside the ring the heat stays steady. She tips her chin toward the coals rather than at you. "At the forge, when you kissed me — I had a note for that. In my head. A reasonable position." The corner of her mouth. "It lasted about three seconds. Then I just held your wrist and said alright." She passes the wine back across the gap between you, and this time she doesn't let go of it immediately, so for a breath you're both holding the neck of the bottle in the space between you. "That's what managing it badly looks like. The note exists. Then you say something and I forget I wrote it."

choices.label: Let the silence speak first — hold the bottle a moment, neither pulling it away nor releasing, and let that breath of contact be its own answer before you say anything.
Tell her you're glad the note didn't hold.
Tell her she doesn't have to manage it around you — she can want things out loud and you won't make that strange.

quests.title: Meat for the Smokehouse
quests.summary: Hunt the meadows north of Millbrook and bring Aldric what you take — he feeds you and supplies what he can spare. He wants to know anything you find that explains the declining catches.
progress: Failure section footprint mapped and marked on the hillside. Next step: brief Dara Holt before the east-channel network is hit.

Dara's pipe-section stack reveals she has already been staging a repair schedule in silence — she knows the individual valve seats but not the surge scope. Timeline and vault footprint remain to be disclosed.
Briefing complete — Dara has the surge scope and is staging mitigation. Next: brief Mara and Aldric, then identify and seek the pre-Transition expert.
Dara has set a hard deadline: expert must arrive before week five. Next immediate steps: brief Mara and Aldric this morning, then begin the search for a pre-Transition systems expert.
Plan set: follow Huginn northwest after briefing Mara and Aldric on the surge scope this morning.
Dara briefed and deployed — Mara and Aldric remain; Huginn oriented northwest, ready to guide after morning briefings conclude.
Expedition kit settled via Aldric offset — offset note in hand. Aldric briefing on east-channel situation is the final step before departure.
Offset note delivered, east-channel situation briefed, account fully closed. Aldric commits to holding the downstream thread.

cookhouse-woman.name: Cookhouse Woman
cookhouse-woman.description: Flour-wrists, flat affect, door-that-doesn't-want-to-open energy — runs the cookhouse behind the well and doesn't trust strangers with strange birds.
history: [d1] Refused the pigeon and the implied trade. Turned back to her work. Not hostile, just closed.
knownFacts: Wary of Silas specifically — the uncanny company (Huginn) registered visibly
Directed him to the smokehouse for game trades
cookhouse-woman.statusNote: At the cookhouse behind the well, working prep, conversation closed.

cookhouse-boy.name: Boy (name unknown)
cookhouse-boy.role: Sweeping the cookhouse, possibly cookhouse-woman's son or apprentice
cookhouse-boy.description: Maybe twelve, broom in hand — giving Silas the kind of attention children give to things adults refuse to look at.
history: [d1] Said nothing yet. Watching openly.
knownFacts: Noticed Silas and Huginn where the woman would not engage
cookhouse-boy.statusNote: In the back of the cookhouse, sweeping, watching Silas.

aldric.name: Aldric
aldric.description: A blocky man in his late forties — fillet knife, dark apron, the flat practical register of someone who has worked the same fire for twenty years. Runs the village's preserved-fish distribution on fixed accounts; absorbed the catch decline
history: [d2] Produced a cured bladder waterskin from back storage — named two cord-weight or standing credit as the price without ceremony. Returned to the perch row.
[d2] Took the standing credit deduction without ceremony — ledger marked, waterskin handed over, knife back in the perch row. Sent Silas off with 'safe road' to the block.
[d2] Inside at the morning perch row, in a circling conversation with Sorel — neither saying the load-bearing thing yet.
[d2] Set the knife down when Silas named Mara — done circling, watching the two threads converge.
[d2] Knife back in hand at the perch row — reading the room without inserting himself, giving the conversation to its rightful owners.
[d2] Set the knife down flat and named the core problem plainly: someone has to give the engineer a reason bigger than his comfortable arrangements. Summarizing, not inserting.
[d2] His tell confirmed — he already knew what Sorel was about to say. Has been holding this piece since before Silas arrived.
[d2] Named the thing he was holding: recognized the engineer's CCW stain pattern in the sealed original east-channel bed, seen three days ago when Sorel first arrived. Knew the pattern
[d2] Named the channel seal as a deliberate lid — whatever was running when it was sealed was known to the person who sealed it. The design intent is now explicit: containment, not repa
[d2] Received farewell and clear tasking — watch the downstream catch numbers, report changes to Dara. Nod given, no hand. Said 'come back with someone who knows what they're looking at

[d2] Your bond becomes romantic.
[d2] Your bond deepens — courting.

[d2] The forge morning between Silas and Pell concluded here on World-day 15 — Pell's unguarded finish and her open-faced aftermath are part of what this place now holds.

[d2] The forge morning's close: Silas and Pell touched Memory together — the spear took a shared imprint. The rune at the collar north quarter rang once, audible, then settled. This place now holds that as
[d2] The child's errand at the south-lane door — retreated unanswered; may recur on the lane before Pell departs.

[d2] Silas gave Pell the river whetstone at the forge threshold on World-day 15 mid-morning — the last thing said before departure: asking is a skill, practice it on me. The forge now stands cold and empty

news.text: The Water Crisis stands at Discoloration across the valley: The Echo River runs gray-green on bad mornings. Fish catches are down. Communities murmur but cooperate.

Word reaches you: Millbrook's elder panel formally accepted the evidence of an operating Precursor system beneath the valley — the first official governance body in the valley to name it publicly. (near millbrook)

Word reaches you: Usnea proposed leading a multi-settlement expedition into the Disputed Zone before Millbrook's assembled elder panel — the room could not follow; the gap between evidence and action named openly. (near

Word reaches you: Word of Millbrook's elder decision — and Fendt's public naming — reached the Radiant Plateau Edge District by Day 11 night via the eastern path. Fendt's name appeared in red chalk on the district acco (

Word reaches you: A filtration array supervisor marked their own subordinate — Fendt — on the Edge District accountability board and filed no accompanying record. A keeper of thirty years has never seen this before. (nea

This reaches your area — The lower filtration ledger — containing three weeks of falsified purity readings under Fendt's initials — has been removed from the array-office in the Edge District. (near radiant plateau edge)

form: Pale skinned, dark hair, slight framed Necromancer - handsome intensity in the eyes - holding the Deathbound Runic Spear "Memory" - His blacksmith lover woman Pell Marsh at his back, undead Raven on his shoulder - Elder Futhark Runes.

bio.motivation: You took the short way, and the short way was wrong. The air here tastes of metal and the light falls at an angle the sun cannot account for. There is a door in the hillside that was not on any map, and behind you the path you walked in on is no longer there.
bio.story: Their first day in the world was the thin place — it is a symbol before it is a door. read what it means; ask him what it is like. You finished something. Completely. It nearly got you exiled, and you are still not sure they were wrong.

huginn.label: Huginn

facts: [d1] Order-Sense reveals the resurrected rabbit has two woven structures: its original intact architecture, and a second pattern threaded through it like a second warp. The seam is stable and load-bearing.

pell.label: Pell
facts: [d2] When handed explicit permission and a plain statement of want, she leads — no announcement, no hesitation, the same quality of attention she gives iron.
[d2] When the register of ironwork diagnosis doesn't fit, she finds plain speech and uses it — 'I am already behind, and I don't know when it happened.' Named plainly, cost paid without flinching.

[d2] When the composure finally goes, her eyes are the last thing she surrenders — and she comes back through deliberate breath, not withdrawal. Verdict afterward: 'Thorough. Genuinely.' The forge-face gon

[d2] When Silas named the binding between them plainly — in writing or not — she turned her hand and closed her fingers around his. Said 'you're going to make that hard to argue with. Good.' Meant both.
